1800        This option is deprecated in favor of the <literal>sslmode</literal>
1801        setting.
1802       </para>
1803
1804       <para>
1805        If set to 1, an <acronym>SSL</acronym> connection to the server
1806        is required (this is equivalent to <literal>sslmode</literal>
1807        <literal>require</literal>).  <application>libpq</application> will then refuse
1808        to connect if the server does not accept an
1809        <acronym>SSL</acronym> connection.  If set to 0 (default),
1810        <application>libpq</application> will negotiate the connection type with
1811        the server (equivalent to <literal>sslmode</literal>
1812        <literal>prefer</literal>).  This option is only available if
1813        <productname>PostgreSQL</productname> is compiled with SSL support.
1814       </para>
1815      </listitem>
1816     </varlistentry>
1817
1818     <varlistentry id="libpq-connect-sslnegotiation" xreflabel="sslnegotiation">
1819      <term><literal>sslnegotiation</literal></term>
1820      <listitem>
1821       <para>
1822        This option controls how SSL encryption is negotiated with the server,
1823        if SSL is used. In the default <literal>postgres</literal> mode, the
1824        client first asks the server if SSL is supported. In
1825        <literal>direct</literal> mode, the client starts the standard SSL
1826        handshake directly after establishing the TCP/IP connection. Traditional
1827        <productname>PostgreSQL</productname> protocol negotiation is the most
1828        flexible with different server configurations. If the server is known
1829        to support direct <acronym>SSL</acronym> connections then the latter
1830        requires one fewer round trip reducing connection latency and also
1831        allows the use of protocol agnostic SSL network tools. The direct SSL
1832        option was introduced in <productname>PostgreSQL</productname> version
1833        17.
1834       </para>
1835
1836        <variablelist>
1837         <varlistentry>
1838          <term><literal>postgres</literal></term>
1839          <listitem>
1840           <para>
1841             perform <productname>PostgreSQL</productname> protocol
1842             negotiation. This is the default if the option is not provided.
1843           </para>
1844          </listitem>
1845         </varlistentry>
1846
1847         <varlistentry>
1848          <term><literal>direct</literal></term>
1849          <listitem>
1850           <para>
1851            start SSL handshake directly after establishing the TCP/IP
1852            connection.  This is only allowed with
1853            <literal>sslmode=require</literal> or higher, because the weaker
1854            settings could lead to unintended fallback to plaintext
1855            authentication when the server does not support direct SSL
1856            handshake.
1857           </para>
1858          </listitem>
1859         </varlistentry>
1860        </variablelist>
1861      </listitem>
1862     </varlistentry>
1863
1864     <varlistentry id="libpq-connect-sslcompression" xreflabel="sslcompression">
1865      <term><literal>sslcompression</literal></term>
1866      <listitem>
1867       <para>
1868        If set to 1, data sent over SSL connections will be compressed.  If
1869        set to 0, compression will be disabled.  The default is 0.  This
1870        parameter is ignored if a connection without SSL is made.
1871       </para>
1872
1873       <para>
1874        SSL compression is nowadays considered insecure and its use is no
1875        longer recommended.  <productname>OpenSSL</productname> 1.1.0 disabled
1876        compression by default, and many operating system distributions
1877        disabled it in prior versions as well, so setting this parameter to on
1878        will not have any effect if the server does not accept compression.
1879        <productname>PostgreSQL</productname> 14 disabled compression
1880        completely in the backend.
1881       </para>
1882
1883       <para>
1884        If security is not a primary concern, compression can improve
1885        throughput if the network is the bottleneck.  Disabling compression
1886        can improve response time and throughput if CPU performance is the
1887        limiting factor.
1888       </para>
1889      </listitem>
1890     </varlistentry>
1891
1892     <varlistentry id="libpq-connect-sslcert" xreflabel="sslcert">
1893      <term><literal>sslcert</literal></term>
1894      <listitem>
1895       <para>
1896        This parameter specifies the file name of the client SSL
1897        certificate, replacing the default
1898        <filename>~/.postgresql/postgresql.crt</filename>.
1899        This parameter is ignored if an SSL connection is not made.
1900       </para>
1901      </listitem>
1902     </varlistentry>
1903
1904     <varlistentry id="libpq-connect-sslkey" xreflabel="sslkey">
1905      <term><literal>sslkey</literal></term>
1906      <listitem>
1907       <para>
1908        This parameter specifies the location for the secret key used for
1909        the client certificate. It can either specify a file name that will
1910        be used instead of the default
1911        <filename>~/.postgresql/postgresql.key</filename>, or it can specify a key
1912        obtained from an external <quote>engine</quote> (engines are
1913        <productname>OpenSSL</productname> loadable modules).  An external engine
1914        specification should consist of a colon-separated engine name and
1915        an engine-specific key identifier.  This parameter is ignored if an
1916        SSL connection is not made.
1917       </para>
1918      </listitem>
1919     </varlistentry>
1920
1921     <varlistentry id="libpq-connect-sslkeylogfile" xreflabel="sslkeylogfile">
1922      <term><literal>sslkeylogfile</literal></term>
1923      <listitem>
1924       <para>
1925        This parameter specifies the location where <application>libpq</application>
1926        will log keys used in this SSL context.  This is useful for debugging
1927        <productname>PostgreSQL</productname> protocol interactions or client
1928        connections using network inspection tools like
1929        <productname>Wireshark</productname>.  This parameter is ignored if an
1930        SSL connection is not made, or if <productname>LibreSSL</productname>
1931        is used (<productname>LibreSSL</productname> does not support key
1932        logging).  Keys are logged using the <productname>NSS</productname>
1933        format.
1934        <warning>
1935         <para>
1936          Key logging will expose potentially sensitive information in the
1937          keylog file.  Keylog files should be handled with the same care as
1938          <xref linkend="libpq-connect-sslkey" /> files.
1939         </para>
1940        </warning>
1941       </para>
1942      </listitem>
1943     </varlistentry>
1944
1945     <varlistentry id="libpq-connect-sslpassword" xreflabel="sslpassword">
1946      <term><literal>sslpassword</literal></term>
1947      <listitem>
1948       <para>
1949        This parameter specifies the password for the secret key specified in
1950        <literal>sslkey</literal>, allowing client certificate private keys
1951        to be stored in encrypted form on disk even when interactive passphrase
1952        input is not practical.
1953       </para>
1954       <para>
1955        Specifying this parameter with any non-empty value suppresses the
1956        <literal>Enter PEM pass phrase:</literal>
1957        prompt that <productname>OpenSSL</productname> will emit by default
1958        when an encrypted client certificate key is provided to
1959        <application>libpq</application>.
1960       </para>
1961       <para>
1962        If the key is not encrypted this parameter is ignored. The parameter
1963        has no effect on keys specified by <productname>OpenSSL</productname>
1964        engines unless the engine uses the <productname>OpenSSL</productname>
1965        password callback mechanism for prompts.
1966       </para>
1967       <para>
1968        There is no environment variable equivalent to this option, and no
1969        facility for looking it up in <filename>.pgpass</filename>. It can be
1970        used in a service file connection definition. Users with
1971        more sophisticated uses should consider using <productname>OpenSSL</productname> engines and
1972        tools like PKCS#11 or USB crypto offload devices.
1973       </para>
1974      </listitem>
1975     </varlistentry>
1976
1977     <varlistentry id="libpq-connect-sslcertmode" xreflabel="sslcertmode">
1978      <term><literal>sslcertmode</literal></term>
1979      <listitem>
1980       <para>
1981        This option determines whether a client certificate may be sent to the
1982        server, and whether the server is required to request one. There are
1983        three modes:
1984
1985        <variablelist>
1986         <varlistentry>
1987          <term><literal>disable</literal></term>
1988          <listitem>
1989           <para>
1990            A client certificate is never sent, even if one is available
1991            (default location or provided via
1992            <xref linkend="libpq-connect-sslcert" />).
1993           </para>
1994          </listitem>
1995         </varlistentry>
1996
1997         <varlistentry>
1998          <term><literal>allow</literal> (default)</term>
1999          <listitem>
2000           <para>
2001            A certificate may be sent, if the server requests one and the
2002            client has one to send.
2003           </para>
2004          </listitem>
2005         </varlistentry>
2006
2007         <varlistentry>
2008          <term><literal>require</literal></term>
2009          <listitem>
2010           <para>
2011            The server <emphasis>must</emphasis> request a certificate. The
2012            connection will fail if the client does not send a certificate and
2013            the server successfully authenticates the client anyway.
2014           </para>
2015          </listitem>
2016         </varlistentry>
2017        </variablelist>
2018       </para>
2019
2020       <note>
2021        <para>
2022         <literal>sslcertmode=require</literal> doesn't add any additional
2023         security, since there is no guarantee that the server is validating
2024         the certificate correctly; PostgreSQL servers generally request TLS
2025         certificates from clients whether they validate them or not. The
2026         option may be useful when troubleshooting more complicated TLS
2027         setups.
2028        </para>
2029       </note>
2030      </listitem>
2031     </varlistentry>
2032
2033     <varlistentry id="libpq-connect-sslrootcert" xreflabel="sslrootcert">
2034      <term><literal>sslrootcert</literal></term>
2035      <listitem>
2036       <para>
2037        This parameter specifies the name of a file containing SSL
2038        certificate authority (<acronym>CA</acronym>) certificate(s).
2039        If the file exists, the server's certificate will be verified
2040        to be signed by one of these authorities.  The default is
2041        <filename>~/.postgresql/root.crt</filename>.
2042       </para>
2043       <para>
2044        The special value <literal>system</literal> may be specified instead, in
2045        which case the trusted CA roots from the SSL implementation will be loaded. The exact
2046        locations of these root certificates differ by SSL implementation and
2047        platform. For <productname>OpenSSL</productname> in particular, the
2048        locations may be further modified by the <envar>SSL_CERT_DIR</envar>
2049        and <envar>SSL_CERT_FILE</envar> environment variables.
2050       </para>
2051       <note>
2052        <para>
2053         When using <literal>sslrootcert=system</literal>, the default
2054         <literal>sslmode</literal> is changed to <literal>verify-full</literal>,
2055         and any weaker setting will result in an error. In most cases it is
2056         trivial for anyone to obtain a certificate trusted by the system for a
2057         hostname they control, rendering <literal>verify-ca</literal> and all
2058         weaker modes useless.
2059        </para>
2060        <para>
2061         The magic <literal>system</literal> value will take precedence over a
2062         local certificate file with the same name. If for some reason you find
2063         yourself in this situation, use an alternative path like
2064         <literal>sslrootcert=./system</literal> instead.
2065        </para>
2066       </note>
2067      </listitem>
2068     </varlistentry>
2069
2070     <varlistentry id="libpq-connect-sslcrl" xreflabel="sslcrl">
2071      <term><literal>sslcrl</literal></term>
2072      <listitem>
2073       <para>
2074        This parameter specifies the file name of the SSL server certificate
2075        revocation list (CRL).  Certificates listed in this file, if it
2076        exists, will be rejected while attempting to authenticate the
2077        server's certificate.  If neither
2078        <xref linkend="libpq-connect-sslcrl"/> nor
2079        <xref linkend="libpq-connect-sslcrldir"/> is set, this setting is
2080        taken as
2081        <filename>~/.postgresql/root.crl</filename>.
2082       </para>
2083      </listitem>
2084     </varlistentry>
2085
2086     <varlistentry id="libpq-connect-sslcrldir" xreflabel="sslcrldir">
2087      <term><literal>sslcrldir</literal></term>
2088      <listitem>
2089       <para>
2090        This parameter specifies the directory name of the SSL server certificate
2091        revocation list (CRL).  Certificates listed in the files in this
2092        directory, if it exists, will be rejected while attempting to
2093        authenticate the server's certificate.
2094       </para>
2095
2096       <para>
2097        The directory needs to be prepared with the
2098        <productname>OpenSSL</productname> command
2099        <literal>openssl rehash</literal> or <literal>c_rehash</literal>.  See
2100        its documentation for details.
2101       </para>
2102
2103       <para>
2104        Both <literal>sslcrl</literal> and <literal>sslcrldir</literal> can be
2105        specified together.
2106       </para>
2107      </listitem>
2108     </varlistentry>
2109
2110     <varlistentry id="libpq-connect-sslsni" xreflabel="sslsni">
2111      <term><literal>sslsni</literal><indexterm><primary>Server Name Indication</primary></indexterm></term>
2112      <listitem>
2113       <para>
2114        If set to 1 (default), libpq sets the TLS extension <quote>Server Name
2115        Indication</quote> (<acronym>SNI</acronym>) on SSL-enabled connections.
2116        By setting this parameter to 0, this is turned off.
2117       </para>
2118
2119       <para>
2120        The Server Name Indication can be used by SSL-aware proxies to route
2121        connections without having to decrypt the SSL stream.  (Note that
2122        unless the proxy is aware of the PostgreSQL protocol handshake this
2123        would require setting <literal>sslnegotiation</literal>
2124        to <literal>direct</literal>.)
2125        However, <acronym>SNI</acronym> makes the destination host name appear
2126        in cleartext in the network traffic, so it might be undesirable in
2127        some cases.
2128       </para>
2129      </listitem>
2130     </varlistentry>
2131
2132     <varlistentry id="libpq-connect-requirepeer" xreflabel="requirepeer">
2133      <term><literal>requirepeer</literal></term>
2134      <listitem>
2135       <para>
2136        This parameter specifies the operating-system user name of the
2137        server, for example <literal>requirepeer=postgres</literal>.
2138        When making a Unix-domain socket connection, if this
2139        parameter is set, the client checks at the beginning of the
2140        connection that the server process is running under the specified
2141        user name; if it is not, the connection is aborted with an error.
2142        This parameter can be used to provide server authentication similar
2143        to that available with SSL certificates on TCP/IP connections.
2144        (Note that if the Unix-domain socket is in
2145        <filename>/tmp</filename> or another publicly writable location,
2146        any user could start a server listening there.  Use this parameter
2147        to ensure that you are connected to a server run by a trusted user.)
2148        This option is only supported on platforms for which the
2149        <literal>peer</literal> authentication method is implemented; see
2150        <xref linkend="auth-peer"/>.
2151       </para>
2152      </listitem>
2153     </varlistentry>
2154
2155     <varlistentry id="libpq-connect-ssl-min-protocol-version" xreflabel="ssl_min_protocol_version">
2156      <term><literal>ssl_min_protocol_version</literal></term>
2157      <listitem>
2158       <para>
2159        This parameter specifies the minimum SSL/TLS protocol version to allow
2160        for the connection. Valid values are <literal>TLSv1</literal>,
2161        <literal>TLSv1.1</literal>, <literal>TLSv1.2</literal> and
2162        <literal>TLSv1.3</literal>. The supported protocols depend on the
2163        version of <productname>OpenSSL</productname> used, older versions
2164        not supporting the most modern protocol versions. If not specified,
2165        the default is <literal>TLSv1.2</literal>, which satisfies industry
2166        best practices as of this writing.
2167       </para>
2168      </listitem>
2169     </varlistentry>
2170
2171     <varlistentry id="libpq-connect-ssl-max-protocol-version" xreflabel="ssl_max_protocol_version">
2172      <term><literal>ssl_max_protocol_version</literal></term>
2173      <listitem>
2174       <para>
2175        This parameter specifies the maximum SSL/TLS protocol version to allow
2176        for the connection. Valid values are <literal>TLSv1</literal>,
2177        <literal>TLSv1.1</literal>, <literal>TLSv1.2</literal> and
2178        <literal>TLSv1.3</literal>. The supported protocols depend on the
2179        version of <productname>OpenSSL</productname> used, older versions
2180        not supporting the most modern protocol versions. If not set, this
2181        parameter is ignored and the connection will use the maximum bound
2182        defined by the backend, if set. Setting the maximum protocol version
2183        is mainly useful for testing or if some component has issues working
2184        with a newer protocol.
2185       </para>
2186      </listitem>
2187     </varlistentry>
2188
2189     <varlistentry id="libpq-connect-min-protocol-version" xreflabel="min_protocol_version">
2190      <term><literal>min_protocol_version</literal></term>
2191      <listitem>
2192       <para>
2193        Specifies the minimum protocol version to allow for the connection.
2194        The default is to allow any version of the
2195        <productname>PostgreSQL</productname> protocol supported by libpq,
2196        which currently means <literal>3.0</literal>. If the server
2197        does not support at least this protocol version the connection will be
2198        closed.
2199       </para>
2200
2201       <para>
2202        The current supported values are
2203        <literal>3.0</literal>, <literal>3.2</literal>,
2204        and <literal>latest</literal>. The <literal>latest</literal> value is
2205        equivalent to the latest protocol version supported by the libpq
2206        version being used, which is currently <literal>3.2</literal>.
2207       </para>
2208      </listitem>
2209     </varlistentry>
2210
2211     <varlistentry id="libpq-connect-max-protocol-version" xreflabel="max_protocol_version">
2212      <term><literal>max_protocol_version</literal></term>
2213      <listitem>
2214       <para>
2215        Specifies the protocol version to request from the server.
2216        The default is to use version <literal>3.0</literal> of the
2217        <productname>PostgreSQL</productname> protocol, unless the connection
2218        string specifies a feature that relies on a higher protocol version,
2219        in which case the latest version supported by libpq is used. If the
2220        server does not support the protocol version requested by the client,
2221        the connection is automatically downgraded to a lower minor protocol
2222        version that the server supports. After the connection attempt has
2223        completed you can use <xref linkend="libpq-PQfullProtocolVersion"/> to
2224        find out which exact protocol version was negotiated.
2225       </para>
2226
2227       <para>
2228        The current supported values are
2229        <literal>3.0</literal>, <literal>3.2</literal>,
2230        and <literal>latest</literal>. The <literal>latest</literal> value is
2231        equivalent to the latest protocol version supported by the libpq
2232        version being used, which is currently <literal>3.2</literal>.
2233       </para>
2234      </listitem>
2235     </varlistentry>
2236
2237     <varlistentry id="libpq-connect-krbsrvname" xreflabel="krbsrvname">
2238      <term><literal>krbsrvname</literal></term>
2239      <listitem>
2240       <para>
2241        Kerberos service name to use when authenticating with GSSAPI.
2242        This must match the service name specified in the server
2243        configuration for Kerberos authentication to succeed. (See also
2244        <xref linkend="gssapi-auth"/>.)
2245        The default value is normally <literal>postgres</literal>,
2246        but that can be changed when
2247        building <productname>PostgreSQL</productname> via
2248        the <option>--with-krb-srvnam</option> option
2249        of <application>configure</application>.
2250        In most environments, this parameter never needs to be changed.
2251        Some Kerberos implementations might require a different service name,
2252        such as Microsoft Active Directory which requires the service name
2253        to be in upper case (<literal>POSTGRES</literal>).
2254       </para>
2255      </listitem>
2256     </varlistentry>
2257
2258     <varlistentry id="libpq-connect-gsslib" xreflabel="gsslib">
2259      <term><literal>gsslib</literal></term>
2260      <listitem>
2261       <para>
2262        GSS library to use for GSSAPI authentication.
2263        Currently this is disregarded except on Windows builds that include
2264        both GSSAPI and SSPI support.  In that case, set
2265        this to <literal>gssapi</literal> to cause libpq to use the GSSAPI
2266        library for authentication instead of the default SSPI.
2267       </para>
2268      </listitem>
2269     </varlistentry>
2270
2271     <varlistentry id="libpq-connect-gssdelegation" xreflabel="gssdelegation">
2272      <term><literal>gssdelegation</literal></term>
2273      <listitem>
2274       <para>
2275        Forward (delegate) GSS credentials to the server.  The default is
2276        <literal>0</literal> which means credentials will not be forwarded
2277        to the server.  Set this to <literal>1</literal> to have credentials
2278        forwarded when possible.
2279       </para>
2280      </listitem>
2281     </varlistentry>
2282
2283     <varlistentry id="libpq-connect-scram-client-key" xreflabel="scram_client_key">
2284      <term><literal>scram_client_key</literal></term>
2285      <listitem>
2286       <para>
2287        The base64-encoded SCRAM client key.  This can be used by foreign-data
2288        wrappers or similar middleware to enable pass-through SCRAM
2289        authentication. See <xref
2290        linkend="postgres-fdw-options-connection-management"/> for one such
2291        implementation.  It is not meant to be specified directly by users or
2292        client applications.
2293       </para>
2294      </listitem>
2295     </varlistentry>
2296
2297     <varlistentry id="libpq-connect-scram-server-key" xreflabel="scram_server_key">
2298      <term><literal>scram_server_key</literal></term>
2299      <listitem>
2300       <para>
2301        The base64-encoded SCRAM server key.  This can be used by foreign-data
2302        wrappers or similar middleware to enable pass-through SCRAM
2303        authentication. See <xref
2304        linkend="postgres-fdw-options-connection-management"/> for one such
2305        implementation.  It is not meant to be specified directly by users or
2306        client applications.
2307       </para>
2308      </listitem>
2309     </varlistentry>
2310
2311     <varlistentry id="libpq-connect-service" xreflabel="service">
2312      <term><literal>service</literal></term>
2313      <listitem>
2314       <para>
2315        Service name to use for additional parameters.  It specifies a service
2316        name in <filename>pg_service.conf</filename> that holds additional connection parameters.
2317        This allows applications to specify only a service name so connection parameters
2318        can be centrally maintained. See <xref linkend="libpq-pgservice"/>.
2319       </para>
2320      </listitem>
2321     </varlistentry>
2322
2323     <varlistentry id="libpq-connect-target-session-attrs" xreflabel="target_session_attrs">
2324      <term><literal>target_session_attrs</literal></term>
2325      <listitem>
2326       <para>
2327        This option determines whether the session must have certain
2328        properties to be acceptable.  It's typically used in combination
2329        with multiple host names to select the first acceptable alternative
2330        among several hosts.  There are six modes:
2331
2332        <variablelist>
2333         <varlistentry>
2334          <term><literal>any</literal> (default)</term>
2335          <listitem>
2336           <para>
2337            any successful connection is acceptable
2338           </para>
2339          </listitem>
2340         </varlistentry>
2341
2342         <varlistentry>
2343          <term><literal>read-write</literal></term>
2344          <listitem>
2345           <para>
2346            session must accept read-write transactions by default (that
2347            is, the server must not be in hot standby mode and
2348            the <varname>default_transaction_read_only</varname> parameter
2349            must be <literal>off</literal>)
2350           </para>
2351          </listitem>
2352         </varlistentry>
2353
2354         <varlistentry>
2355          <term><literal>read-only</literal></term>
2356          <listitem>
2357           <para>
2358            session must not accept read-write transactions by default (the
2359            converse)
2360           </para>
2361          </listitem>
2362         </varlistentry>
2363
2364         <varlistentry>
2365          <term><literal>primary</literal></term>
2366          <listitem>
2367           <para>
2368            server must not be in hot standby mode
2369           </para>
2370          </listitem>
2371         </varlistentry>
2372
2373         <varlistentry>
2374          <term><literal>standby</literal></term>
2375          <listitem>
2376           <para>
2377            server must be in hot standby mode
2378           </para>
2379          </listitem>
2380         </varlistentry>
2381
2382         <varlistentry>
2383          <term><literal>prefer-standby</literal></term>
2384          <listitem>
2385           <para>
2386            first try to find a standby server, but if none of the listed
2387            hosts is a standby server, try again in <literal>any</literal>
2388            mode
2389           </para>
2390          </listitem>
2391         </varlistentry>
2392        </variablelist>
2393       </para>
2394      </listitem>
2395     </varlistentry>
2396
2397     <varlistentry id="libpq-connect-load-balance-hosts" xreflabel="load_balance_hosts">
2398      <term><literal>load_balance_hosts</literal></term>
2399      <listitem>
2400       <para>
2401        Controls the order in which the client tries to connect to the available
2402        hosts and addresses. Once a connection attempt is successful no other
2403        hosts and addresses will be tried. This parameter is typically used in
2404        combination with multiple host names or a DNS record that returns
2405        multiple IPs. This parameter can be used in combination with
2406        <xref linkend="libpq-connect-target-session-attrs"/>
2407        to, for example, load balance over standby servers only. Once successfully
2408        connected, subsequent queries on the returned connection will all be
2409        sent to the same server. There are currently two modes:
2410        <variablelist>
2411         <varlistentry>
2412          <term><literal>disable</literal> (default)</term>
2413          <listitem>
2414           <para>
2415            No load balancing across hosts is performed.  Hosts are tried in
2416            the order in which they are provided and addresses are tried in
2417            the order they are received from DNS or a hosts file.
2418           </para>
2419          </listitem>
2420         </varlistentry>
2421
2422         <varlistentry>
2423          <term><literal>random</literal></term>
2424          <listitem>
2425           <para>
2426            Hosts and addresses are tried in random order. This value is mostly
2427            useful when opening multiple connections at the same time, possibly
2428            from different machines. This way connections can be load balanced
2429            across multiple <productname>PostgreSQL</productname> servers.
2430           </para>
2431           <para>
2432            While random load balancing, due to its random nature, will almost
2433            never result in a completely uniform distribution, it statistically
2434            gets quite close. One important aspect here is that this algorithm
2435            uses two levels of random choices: First the hosts
2436            will be resolved in random order. Then secondly, before resolving
2437            the next host, all resolved addresses for the current host will be
2438            tried in random order. This behaviour can skew the amount of
2439            connections each node gets greatly in certain cases, for instance
2440            when some hosts resolve to more addresses than others. But such a
2441            skew can also be used on purpose, e.g. to increase the number of
2442            connections a larger server gets by providing its hostname multiple
2443            times in the host string.
2444           </para>
2445           <para>
2446            When using this value it's recommended to also configure a reasonable
2447            value for <xref linkend="libpq-connect-connect-timeout"/>. Because then,
2448            if one of the nodes that are used for load balancing is not responding,
2449            a new node will be tried.
2450           </para>
2451          </listitem>
2452         </varlistentry>
2453        </variablelist>
2454       </para>
2455      </listitem>
2456     </varlistentry>
2457
2458     <varlistentry id="libpq-connect-oauth-issuer" xreflabel="oauth_issuer">
2459      <term><literal>oauth_issuer</literal></term>
2460      <listitem>
2461       <para>
2462        The HTTPS URL of a trusted issuer to contact if the server requests an
2463        OAuth token for the connection. This parameter is required for all OAuth
2464        connections; it should exactly match the <literal>issuer</literal>
2465        setting in <link linkend="auth-oauth">the server's HBA configuration</link>.
2466       </para>
2467       <para>
2468        As part of the standard authentication handshake, <application>libpq</application>
2469        will ask the server for a <emphasis>discovery document:</emphasis> a URL
2470        providing a set of OAuth configuration parameters. The server must
2471        provide a URL that is directly constructed from the components of the
2472        <literal>oauth_issuer</literal>, and this value must exactly match the
2473        issuer identifier that is declared in the discovery document itself, or
2474        the connection will fail. This is required to prevent a class of
2475        <ulink url="https://mailarchive.ietf.org/arch/msg/oauth/JIVxFBGsJBVtm7ljwJhPUm3Fr-w/">
2476        "mix-up attacks"</ulink> on OAuth clients.
2477       </para>
2478       <para>
2479        You may also explicitly set <literal>oauth_issuer</literal> to the
2480        <literal>/.well-known/</literal> URI used for OAuth discovery. In this
2481        case, if the server asks for a different URL, the connection will fail,
2482        but a <link linkend="libpq-oauth-authdata-hooks">custom OAuth flow</link>
2483        may be able to speed up the standard handshake by using previously
2484        cached tokens. (In this case, it is recommended that
2485        <xref linkend="libpq-connect-oauth-scope"/> be set as well, since the
2486        client will not have a chance to ask the server for a correct scope
2487        setting, and the default scopes for a token may not be sufficient to
2488        connect.) <application>libpq</application> currently supports the
2489        following well-known endpoints:
2490        <itemizedlist spacing="compact">
2491         <listitem><para><literal>/.well-known/openid-configuration</literal></para></listitem>
2492         <listitem><para><literal>/.well-known/oauth-authorization-server</literal></para></listitem>
2493        </itemizedlist>
2494       </para>
2495       <warning>
2496        <para>
2497         Issuers are highly privileged during the OAuth connection handshake. As
2498         a rule of thumb, if you would not trust the operator of a URL to handle
2499         access to your servers, or to impersonate you directly, that URL should
2500         not be trusted as an <literal>oauth_issuer</literal>.
2501        </para>
2502       </warning>
2503      </listitem>
2504     </varlistentry>
2505
2506     <varlistentry id="libpq-connect-oauth-client-id" xreflabel="oauth_client_id">
2507      <term><literal>oauth_client_id</literal></term>
2508      <listitem>
2509       <para>
2510        An OAuth 2.0 client identifier, as issued by the authorization server.
2511        If the <productname>PostgreSQL</productname> server
2512        <link linkend="auth-oauth">requests an OAuth token</link> for the
2513        connection (and if no <link linkend="libpq-oauth-authdata-hooks">custom
2514        OAuth hook</link> is installed to provide one), then this parameter must
2515        be set; otherwise, the connection will fail.
2516       </para>
2517      </listitem>
2518     </varlistentry>
2519
2520     <varlistentry id="libpq-connect-oauth-client-secret" xreflabel="oauth_client_secret">
2521      <term><literal>oauth_client_secret</literal></term>
2522      <listitem>
2523       <para>
2524        The client password, if any, to use when contacting the OAuth
2525        authorization server. Whether this parameter is required or not is
2526        determined by the OAuth provider; "public" clients generally do not use
2527        a secret, whereas "confidential" clients generally do.
2528       </para>
2529      </listitem>
2530     </varlistentry>
2531
2532     <varlistentry id="libpq-connect-oauth-scope" xreflabel="oauth_scope">
2533      <term><literal>oauth_scope</literal></term>
2534      <listitem>
2535       <para>
2536        The scope of the access request sent to the authorization server,
2537        specified as a (possibly empty) space-separated list of OAuth scope
2538        identifiers. This parameter is optional and intended for advanced usage.
2539       </para>
2540       <para>
2541        Usually the client will obtain appropriate scope settings from the
2542        <productname>PostgreSQL</productname> server. If this parameter is used,
2543        the server's requested scope list will be ignored. This can prevent a
2544        less-trusted server from requesting inappropriate access scopes from the
2545        end user. However, if the client's scope setting does not contain the
2546        server's required scopes, the server is likely to reject the issued
2547        token, and the connection will fail.
2548       </para>
2549       <para>
2550        The meaning of an empty scope list is provider-dependent. An OAuth
2551        authorization server may choose to issue a token with "default scope",
2552        whatever that happens to be, or it may reject the token request
2553        entirely.
2554       </para>
2555      </listitem>
2556     </varlistentry>
2557
2558    </variablelist>
2559   </para>
2560  </sect2>
2561 </sect1>
2562
2563 <sect1 id="libpq-status">
2564  <title>Connection Status Functions</title>
2565
2566  <para>
2567   These functions can be used to interrogate the status
2568   of an existing database connection object.
2569  </para>
2570
2571  <tip>
2572   <para>
2573    <indexterm><primary>libpq-fe.h</primary></indexterm>
2574    <indexterm><primary>libpq-int.h</primary></indexterm>
2575    <application>libpq</application> application programmers should be careful to
2576    maintain the <structname>PGconn</structname> abstraction.  Use the accessor
2577    functions described below to get at the contents of <structname>PGconn</structname>.
2578    Reference to internal <structname>PGconn</structname> fields using
2579    <filename>libpq-int.h</filename> is not recommended because they are subject to change
2580    in the future.
2581   </para>
2582  </tip>
2583
2584  <para>
2585   The following functions return parameter values established at connection.
2586   These values are fixed for the life of the connection.  If a multi-host
2587   connection string is used, the values of <xref linkend="libpq-PQhost"/>,
2588   <xref linkend="libpq-PQport"/>, and <xref linkend="libpq-PQpass"/> can change if a new connection
2589   is established using the same <structname>PGconn</structname> object.  Other values
2590   are fixed for the lifetime of the <structname>PGconn</structname> object.
2591
2592   <variablelist>
2593    <varlistentry id="libpq-PQdb">
2594     <term><function>PQdb</function><indexterm><primary>PQdb</primary></indexterm></term>
2595
2596     <listitem>
2597      <para>
2598       Returns the database name of the connection.
2599<synopsis>
2600char *PQdb(const PGconn *conn);
2601</synopsis>
2602      </para>
2603     </listitem>
2604    </varlistentry>
2605
2606    <varlistentry id="libpq-PQuser">
2607     <term><function>PQuser</function><indexterm><primary>PQuser</primary></indexterm></term>
2608
2609     <listitem>
2610      <para>
2611       Returns the user name of the connection.
2612<synopsis>
2613char *PQuser(const PGconn *conn);
2614</synopsis>
2615      </para>
2616     </listitem>
2617    </varlistentry>
2618
2619    <varlistentry id="libpq-PQpass">
2620     <term><function>PQpass</function><indexterm><primary>PQpass</primary></indexterm></term>
2621
2622     <listitem>
2623      <para>
2624       Returns the password of the connection.
2625<synopsis>
2626char *PQpass(const PGconn *conn);
2627</synopsis>
2628      </para>
2629
2630      <para>
2631       <xref linkend="libpq-PQpass"/> will return either the password specified
2632       in the connection parameters, or if there was none and the password
2633       was obtained from the <link linkend="libpq-pgpass">password
2634       file</link>, it will return that.  In the latter case,
2635       if multiple hosts were specified in the connection parameters, it is
2636       not possible to rely on the result of <xref linkend="libpq-PQpass"/> until
2637       the connection is established.  The status of the connection can be
2638       checked using the function <xref linkend="libpq-PQstatus"/>.
2639      </para>
2640     </listitem>
2641    </varlistentry>
2642
2643    <varlistentry id="libpq-PQhost">
2644     <term><function>PQhost</function><indexterm><primary>PQhost</primary></indexterm></term>
2645
2646     <listitem>
2647      <para>
2648       Returns the server host name of the active connection.
2649       This can be a host name, an IP address, or a directory path if the
2650       connection is via Unix socket.  (The path case can be distinguished
2651       because it will always be an absolute path, beginning
2652       with <literal>/</literal>.)
2653<synopsis>
2654char *PQhost(const PGconn *conn);
2655</synopsis>
2656      </para>
2657
2658      <para>
2659       If the connection parameters specified both <literal>host</literal> and
2660       <literal>hostaddr</literal>, then <xref linkend="libpq-PQhost"/> will
2661       return the <literal>host</literal> information.  If only
2662       <literal>hostaddr</literal> was specified, then that is returned.
2663       If multiple hosts were specified in the connection parameters,
2664       <xref linkend="libpq-PQhost"/> returns the host actually connected to.
2665      </para>
2666
2667      <para>
2668       <xref linkend="libpq-PQhost"/> returns <symbol>NULL</symbol> if the
2669       <parameter>conn</parameter> argument is <symbol>NULL</symbol>.
2670       Otherwise, if there is an error producing the host information (perhaps
2671       if the connection has not been fully established or there was an
2672       error), it returns an empty string.
2673      </para>
2674
2675      <para>
2676       If multiple hosts were specified in the connection parameters, it is
2677       not possible to rely on the result of <xref linkend="libpq-PQhost"/> until
2678       the connection is established.  The status of the connection can be
2679       checked using the function <xref linkend="libpq-PQstatus"/>.
2680      </para>
2681     </listitem>
2682    </varlistentry>
2683
2684
2685    <varlistentry id="libpq-PQhostaddr">
2686     <term><function>PQhostaddr</function><indexterm><primary>PQhostaddr</primary></indexterm></term>
2687
2688     <listitem>
2689      <para>
2690       Returns the server IP address of the active connection.
2691       This can be the address that a host name resolved to,
2692       or an IP address provided through the <literal>hostaddr</literal>
2693       parameter.
2694<synopsis>
2695char *PQhostaddr(const PGconn *conn);
2696</synopsis>
2697      </para>
2698
2699      <para>
2700       <xref linkend="libpq-PQhostaddr"/> returns <symbol>NULL</symbol> if the
2701       <parameter>conn</parameter> argument is <symbol>NULL</symbol>.
2702       Otherwise, if there is an error producing the host information
2703       (perhaps if the connection has not been fully established or
2704       there was an error), it returns an empty string.
2705      </para>
2706     </listitem>
2707    </varlistentry>
2708
2709    <varlistentry id="libpq-PQport">
2710     <term><function>PQport</function><indexterm><primary>PQport</primary></indexterm></term>
2711
2712     <listitem>
2713      <para>
2714       Returns the port of the active connection.
2715
2716<synopsis>
2717char *PQport(const PGconn *conn);
2718</synopsis>
2719      </para>
2720
2721      <para>
2722       If multiple ports were specified in the connection parameters,
2723       <xref linkend="libpq-PQport"/> returns the port actually connected to.
2724      </para>
2725
2726      <para>
2727       <xref linkend="libpq-PQport"/> returns <symbol>NULL</symbol> if the
2728       <parameter>conn</parameter> argument is <symbol>NULL</symbol>.
2729       Otherwise, if there is an error producing the port information (perhaps
2730       if the connection has not been fully established or there was an
2731       error), it returns an empty string.
2732      </para>
2733
2734      <para>
2735       If multiple ports were specified in the connection parameters, it is
2736       not possible to rely on the result of <xref linkend="libpq-PQport"/> until
2737       the connection is established.  The status of the connection can be
2738       checked using the function <xref linkend="libpq-PQstatus"/>.
2739      </para>
2740     </listitem>
2741    </varlistentry>
2742
2743    <varlistentry id="libpq-PQtty">
2744     <term><function>PQtty</function><indexterm><primary>PQtty</primary></indexterm></term>
2745
2746     <listitem>
2747      <para>
2748       This function no longer does anything, but it remains for backwards
2749       compatibility.  The function always return an empty string, or
2750       <symbol>NULL</symbol> if the <parameter>conn</parameter> argument is
2751       <symbol>NULL</symbol>.
2752
2753<synopsis>
2754char *PQtty(const PGconn *conn);
2755</synopsis>
2756      </para>
2757     </listitem>
2758    </varlistentry>
2759
2760    <varlistentry id="libpq-PQoptions">
2761     <term><function>PQoptions</function><indexterm><primary>PQoptions</primary></indexterm></term>
2762
2763     <listitem>
2764      <para>
2765       Returns the command-line options passed in the connection request.
2766<synopsis>
2767char *PQoptions(const PGconn *conn);
2768</synopsis>
2769      </para>
2770     </listitem>
2771    </varlistentry>
2772   </variablelist>
2773  </para>
2774
2775  <para>
2776   The following functions return status data that can change as operations
2777   are executed on the <structname>PGconn</structname> object.
2778
2779   <variablelist>
2780    <varlistentry id="libpq-PQstatus">
2781     <term><function>PQstatus</function><indexterm><primary>PQstatus</primary></indexterm></term>
2782
2783     <listitem>
2784      <para>
2785       Returns the status of the connection.
2786<synopsis>
2787ConnStatusType PQstatus(const PGconn *conn);
2788</synopsis>
2789      </para>
2790
2791      <para>
2792       The status can be one of a number of values.  However, only two of
2793       these are seen outside of an asynchronous connection procedure:
2794       <symbol>CONNECTION_OK</symbol> and
2795       <symbol>CONNECTION_BAD</symbol>. A good connection to the database
2796       has the status <symbol>CONNECTION_OK</symbol>.  A failed
2797       connection attempt is signaled by status
2798       <symbol>CONNECTION_BAD</symbol>.  Ordinarily, an OK status will
2799       remain so until <xref linkend="libpq-PQfinish"/>, but a communications
2800       failure might result in the status changing to
2801       <symbol>CONNECTION_BAD</symbol> prematurely.  In that case the
2802       application could try to recover by calling
2803       <xref linkend="libpq-PQreset"/>.
2804      </para>
2805
2806      <para>
2807       See the entry for <xref linkend="libpq-PQconnectStartParams"/>, <function>PQconnectStart</function>
2808       and <function>PQconnectPoll</function> with regards to other status codes that
2809       might be returned.
2810      </para>
2811     </listitem>
2812    </varlistentry>
2813
2814    <varlistentry id="libpq-PQtransactionStatus">
2815     <term><function>PQtransactionStatus</function><indexterm><primary>PQtransactionStatus</primary></indexterm></term>
2816
2817     <listitem>
2818      <para>
2819       Returns the current in-transaction status of the server.
2820
2821<synopsis>
2822PGTransactionStatusType PQtransactionStatus(const PGconn *conn);
2823</synopsis>
2824
2825       The status can be <literal>PQTRANS_IDLE</literal> (currently idle),
2826       <literal>PQTRANS_ACTIVE</literal> (a command is in progress),
2827       <literal>PQTRANS_INTRANS</literal> (idle, in a valid transaction block),
2828       or <literal>PQTRANS_INERROR</literal> (idle, in a failed transaction block).
2829       <literal>PQTRANS_UNKNOWN</literal> is reported if the connection is bad.
2830       <literal>PQTRANS_ACTIVE</literal> is reported only when a query
2831       has been sent to the server and not yet completed.
2832      </para>
2833     </listitem>
2834    </varlistentry>
2835
2836    <varlistentry id="libpq-PQparameterStatus">
2837     <term><function>PQparameterStatus</function><indexterm><primary>PQparameterStatus</primary></indexterm></term>
2838
2839     <listitem>
2840      <para>
2841       Looks up a current parameter setting of the server.
2842
2843<synopsis>
2844const char *PQparameterStatus(const PGconn *conn, const char *paramName);
2845</synopsis>
2846
2847       Certain parameter values are reported by the server automatically at
2848       connection startup or whenever their values change.
2849       <xref linkend="libpq-PQparameterStatus"/> can be used to interrogate these settings.
2850       It returns the current value of a parameter if known, or <symbol>NULL</symbol>
2851       if the parameter is not known.
2852      </para>
2853
2854      <para>
2855       Parameters reported as of the current release include:
2856       <simplelist type="vert" columns="2">
2857        <member><varname>application_name</varname></member>
2858        <member><varname>client_encoding</varname></member>
2859        <member><varname>DateStyle</varname></member>
2860        <member><varname>default_transaction_read_only</varname></member>
2861        <member><varname>in_hot_standby</varname></member>
2862        <member><varname>integer_datetimes</varname></member>
2863        <member><varname>IntervalStyle</varname></member>
2864        <member><varname>is_superuser</varname></member>
2865        <member><varname>scram_iterations</varname></member>
2866        <member><varname>search_path</varname></member>
2867        <member><varname>server_encoding</varname></member>
2868        <member><varname>server_version</varname></member>
2869        <member><varname>session_authorization</varname></member>
2870        <member><varname>standard_conforming_strings</varname></member>
2871        <member><varname>TimeZone</varname></member>
2872       </simplelist>
2873       (<varname>default_transaction_read_only</varname> and
2874       <varname>in_hot_standby</varname> were not reported by releases before
2875       14; <varname>scram_iterations</varname> was not reported by releases
2876       before 16; <varname>search_path</varname> was not reported by releases
2877       before 18.)
2878       Note that
2879       <varname>server_version</varname>,
2880       <varname>server_encoding</varname> and
2881       <varname>integer_datetimes</varname>
2882       cannot change after startup.
2883      </para>
2884
2885      <para>
2886       If no value for <varname>standard_conforming_strings</varname> is reported,
2887       applications can assume it is <literal>off</literal>, that is, backslashes
2888       are treated as escapes in string literals.  Also, the presence of
2889       this parameter can be taken as an indication that the escape string
2890       syntax (<literal>E'...'</literal>) is accepted.
2891      </para>
2892
2893      <para>
2894       Although the returned pointer is declared <literal>const</literal>, it in fact
2895       points to mutable storage associated with the <literal>PGconn</literal> structure.
2896       It is unwise to assume the pointer will remain valid across queries.
2897      </para>
2898     </listitem>
2899    </varlistentry>
2900
2901    <varlistentry id="libpq-PQfullProtocolVersion">
2902     <term><function>PQfullProtocolVersion</function><indexterm><primary>PQfullProtocolVersion</primary></indexterm></term>
2903
2904     <listitem>
2905      <para>
2906       Interrogates the frontend/backend protocol being used.
2907<synopsis>
2908int PQfullProtocolVersion(const PGconn *conn);
2909</synopsis>
2910       Applications might wish to use this function to determine whether certain
2911       features are supported. The result is formed by multiplying the server's
2912       major version number by 10000 and adding the minor version number. For
2913       example, version 3.2 would be returned as 30002, and version 4.0 would
2914       be returned as 40000. Zero is returned if the connection is bad. The 3.0
2915       protocol is supported by <productname>PostgreSQL</productname> server
2916       versions 7.4 and above.
2917      </para>
2918      <para>
2919       The protocol version will not change after connection startup is
2920       complete, but it could theoretically change during a connection reset.
2921      </para>
2922     </listitem>
2923    </varlistentry>
2924
2925    <varlistentry id="libpq-PQprotocolVersion">
2926     <term><function>PQprotocolVersion</function><indexterm><primary>PQprotocolVersion</primary></indexterm></term>
2927
2928     <listitem>
2929      <para>
2930       Interrogates the frontend/backend protocol major version.
2931<synopsis>
2932int PQprotocolVersion(const PGconn *conn);
2933</synopsis>
2934       Unlike <xref linkend="libpq-PQfullProtocolVersion"/>, this returns only
2935       the major protocol version in use, but it is supported by a wider range
2936       of libpq releases back to version 7.4. Currently, the possible values are
2937       3 (3.0 protocol), or zero (connection bad). Prior to release version
2938       14.0, libpq could additionally return 2 (2.0 protocol).
2939      </para>
2940     </listitem>
2941    </varlistentry>
2942
2943    <varlistentry id="libpq-PQserverVersion">
2944     <term><function>PQserverVersion</function><indexterm><primary>PQserverVersion</primary></indexterm></term>
2945
2946     <listitem>
2947      <para>
2948       Returns an integer representing the server version.
2949<synopsis>
2950int PQserverVersion(const PGconn *conn);
2951</synopsis>
2952      </para>
2953
2954      <para>
2955       Applications might use this function to determine the version of the
2956       database server they are connected to.  The result is formed by
2957       multiplying the server's major version number by 10000 and adding
2958       the minor version number.  For example, version 10.1 will be
2959       returned as 100001, and version 11.0 will be returned as 110000.
2960       Zero is returned if the connection is bad.
2961      </para>
2962
2963      <para>
2964       Prior to major version 10, <productname>PostgreSQL</productname> used
2965       three-part version numbers in which the first two parts together
2966       represented the major version.  For those
2967       versions, <xref linkend="libpq-PQserverVersion"/> uses two digits for each
2968       part; for example version 9.1.5 will be returned as 90105, and
2969       version 9.2.0 will be returned as 90200.
2970      </para>
2971
2972      <para>
2973       Therefore, for purposes of determining feature compatibility,
2974       applications should divide the result of <xref linkend="libpq-PQserverVersion"/>
2975       by 100 not 10000 to determine a logical major version number.
2976       In all release series, only the last two digits differ between
2977       minor releases (bug-fix releases).
2978      </para>
2979     </listitem>
2980    </varlistentry>
2981
2982    <varlistentry id="libpq-PQerrorMessage">
2983     <term>
2984      <function>PQerrorMessage</function><indexterm><primary>PQerrorMessage</primary></indexterm>
2985      <indexterm><primary>error message</primary><secondary>in <structname>PGconn</structname></secondary></indexterm>
2986     </term>
2987
2988     <listitem>
2989      <para>
2990       Returns the error message most recently generated by
2991       an operation on the connection.
2992
2993<synopsis>
2994char *PQerrorMessage(const PGconn *conn);
2995</synopsis>
2996      </para>
2997
2998      <para>
2999       Nearly all <application>libpq</application> functions will set a message for
3000       <xref linkend="libpq-PQerrorMessage"/> if they fail.  Note that by
3001       <application>libpq</application> convention, a nonempty
3002       <xref linkend="libpq-PQerrorMessage"/> result can consist of multiple lines,
3003       and will include a trailing newline. The caller should not free
3004       the result directly. It will be freed when the associated
3005       <structname>PGconn</structname> handle is passed to
3006       <xref linkend="libpq-PQfinish"/>.  The result string should not be
3007       expected to remain the same across operations on the
3008       <literal>PGconn</literal> structure.
3009      </para>
3010     </listitem>
3011    </varlistentry>
3012
3013    <varlistentry id="libpq-PQsocket">
3014     <term><function>PQsocket</function><indexterm><primary>PQsocket</primary></indexterm></term>
3015     <listitem>
3016      <para>
3017       Obtains the file descriptor number of the connection socket to
3018       the server.  A valid descriptor will be greater than or equal
3019       to 0; a result of -1 indicates that no server connection is
3020       currently open.  (This will not change during normal operation,
3021       but could change during connection setup or reset.)
3022
3023<synopsis>
3024int PQsocket(const PGconn *conn);
3025</synopsis>
3026
3027      </para>
3028     </listitem>
3029    </varlistentry>
3030
3031    <varlistentry id="libpq-PQbackendPID">
3032     <term><function>PQbackendPID</function><indexterm><primary>PQbackendPID</primary></indexterm></term>
3033     <listitem>
3034      <para>
3035       Returns the process <acronym>ID</acronym> (PID)<indexterm>
3036        <primary>PID</primary>
3037        <secondary>determining PID of server process</secondary>
3038        <tertiary>in libpq</tertiary>
3039       </indexterm>
3040       of the backend process handling this connection.
3041
3042<synopsis>
3043int PQbackendPID(const PGconn *conn);
3044</synopsis>
3045      </para>
3046
3047      <para>
3048       The backend <acronym>PID</acronym> is useful for debugging
3049       purposes and for comparison to <command>NOTIFY</command>
3050       messages (which include the <acronym>PID</acronym> of the
3051       notifying backend process).  Note that the
3052       <acronym>PID</acronym> belongs to a process executing on the
3053       database server host, not the local host!
3054      </para>
3055     </listitem>
3056    </varlistentry>
3057
3058    <varlistentry id="libpq-PQconnectionNeedsPassword">
3059     <term><function>PQconnectionNeedsPassword</function><indexterm><primary>PQconnectionNeedsPassword</primary></indexterm></term>
3060     <listitem>
3061      <para>
3062       Returns true (1) if the connection authentication method
3063       required a password, but none was available.
3064       Returns false (0) if not.
3065
3066<synopsis>
3067int PQconnectionNeedsPassword(const PGconn *conn);
3068</synopsis>
3069      </para>
3070
3071      <para>
3072       This function can be applied after a failed connection attempt
3073       to decide whether to prompt the user for a password.
3074      </para>
3075     </listitem>
3076    </varlistentry>
3077
3078    <varlistentry id="libpq-PQconnectionUsedPassword">
3079     <term><function>PQconnectionUsedPassword</function><indexterm><primary>PQconnectionUsedPassword</primary></indexterm></term>
3080     <listitem>
3081      <para>
3082       Returns true (1) if the connection authentication method
3083       used a password. Returns false (0) if not.
3084
3085<synopsis>
3086int PQconnectionUsedPassword(const PGconn *conn);
3087</synopsis>
3088      </para>
3089
3090      <para>
3091       This function can be applied after either a failed or successful
3092       connection attempt to detect whether the server demanded a password.
3093      </para>
3094     </listitem>
3095    </varlistentry>
3096
3097    <varlistentry id="libpq-PQconnectionUsedGSSAPI">
3098     <term><function>PQconnectionUsedGSSAPI</function><indexterm><primary>PQconnectionUsedGSSAPI</primary></indexterm></term>
3099     <listitem>
3100      <para>
3101       Returns true (1) if the connection authentication method
3102       used GSSAPI. Returns false (0) if not.
3103
3104<synopsis>
3105int PQconnectionUsedGSSAPI(const PGconn *conn);
3106</synopsis>
3107      </para>
3108
3109      <para>
3110       This function can be applied to detect whether the connection was
3111       authenticated with GSSAPI.
3112      </para>
3113     </listitem>
3114    </varlistentry>
3115   </variablelist>
3116  </para>
3117
3118  <para>
3119    The following functions return information related to SSL. This information
3120    usually doesn't change after a connection is established.
3121
3122    <variablelist>
3123    <varlistentry id="libpq-PQsslInUse">
3124     <term><function>PQsslInUse</function><indexterm><primary>PQsslInUse</primary></indexterm></term>
3125     <listitem>
3126      <para>
3127        Returns true (1) if the connection uses SSL, false (0) if not.
3128
3129<synopsis>
3130int PQsslInUse(const PGconn *conn);
3131</synopsis>
3132      </para>
3133
3134     </listitem>
3135    </varlistentry>
3136
3137    <varlistentry id="libpq-PQsslAttribute">
3138     <term><function>PQsslAttribute</function><indexterm><primary>PQsslAttribute</primary></indexterm></term>
3139     <listitem>
3140      <para>
3141        Returns SSL-related information about the connection.
3142
3143<synopsis>
3144const char *PQsslAttribute(const PGconn *conn, const char *attribute_name);
3145</synopsis>
3146      </para>
3147
3148      <para>
3149       The list of available attributes varies depending on the SSL library
3150       being used and the type of connection.  Returns NULL if the connection
3151       does not use SSL or the specified attribute name is not defined for the
3152       library in use.
3153      </para>
3154
3155      <para>
3156       The following attributes are commonly available:
3157       <variablelist>
3158        <varlistentry>
3159         <term><literal>library</literal></term>
3160          <listitem>
3161           <para>
3162            Name of the SSL implementation in use. (Currently, only
3163            <literal>"OpenSSL"</literal> is implemented)
3164           </para>
3165          </listitem>
3166         </varlistentry>
3167        <varlistentry>
3168         <term><literal>protocol</literal></term>
3169          <listitem>
3170           <para>
3171             SSL/TLS version in use. Common values
3172             are <literal>"TLSv1"</literal>, <literal>"TLSv1.1"</literal>
3173             and <literal>"TLSv1.2"</literal>, but an implementation may
3174             return other strings if some other protocol is used.
3175           </para>
3176          </listitem>
3177         </varlistentry>
3178        <varlistentry>
3179         <term><literal>key_bits</literal></term>
3180          <listitem>
3181           <para>
3182            Number of key bits used by the encryption algorithm.
3183           </para>
3184          </listitem>
3185         </varlistentry>
3186        <varlistentry>
3187         <term><literal>cipher</literal></term>
3188          <listitem>
3189           <para>
3190            A short name of the ciphersuite used, e.g.,
3191            <literal>"DHE-RSA-DES-CBC3-SHA"</literal>. The names are specific
3192            to each SSL implementation.
3193           </para>
3194          </listitem>
3195         </varlistentry>
3196        <varlistentry>
3197         <term><literal>compression</literal></term>
3198          <listitem>
3199           <para>
3200            Returns "on" if SSL compression is in use, else it returns "off".
3201           </para>
3202          </listitem>
3203         </varlistentry>
3204        <varlistentry>
3205         <term><literal>alpn</literal></term>
3206          <listitem>
3207           <para>
3208            Application protocol selected by the TLS Application-Layer
3209            Protocol Negotiation (ALPN) extension.  The only protocol
3210            supported by libpq is <literal>postgresql</literal>, so this is
3211            mainly useful for checking whether the server supported ALPN or
3212            not. Empty string if ALPN was not used.
3213           </para>
3214          </listitem>
3215         </varlistentry>
3216       </variablelist>
3217      </para>
3218
3219      <para>
3220       As a special case, the <literal>library</literal> attribute may be
3221       queried without a connection by passing NULL as
3222       the <literal>conn</literal> argument.  The result will be the default
3223       SSL library name, or NULL if <application>libpq</application> was
3224       compiled without any SSL support.  (Prior
3225       to <productname>PostgreSQL</productname> version 15, passing NULL as
3226       the <literal>conn</literal> argument always resulted in NULL.
3227       Client programs needing to differentiate between the newer and older
3228       implementations of this case may check the
3229       <literal>LIBPQ_HAS_SSL_LIBRARY_DETECTION</literal> feature macro.)
3230      </para>
3231     </listitem>
3232    </varlistentry>
3233
3234    <varlistentry id="libpq-PQsslAttributeNames">
3235     <term><function>PQsslAttributeNames</function><indexterm><primary>PQsslAttributeNames</primary></indexterm></term>
3236     <listitem>
3237      <para>
3238       Returns an array of SSL attribute names that can be used
3239       in <function>PQsslAttribute()</function>.
3240       The array is terminated by a NULL pointer.
3241<synopsis>
3242const char * const * PQsslAttributeNames(const PGconn *conn);
3243</synopsis>
3244      </para>
3245
3246      <para>
3247       If <literal>conn</literal> is NULL, the attributes available for the
3248       default SSL library are returned, or an empty list
3249       if <application>libpq</application> was compiled without any SSL
3250       support.  If <literal>conn</literal> is not NULL, the attributes
3251       available for the SSL library in use for the connection are returned,
3252       or an empty list if the connection is not encrypted.
3253      </para>
3254     </listitem>
3255    </varlistentry>
3256
3257    <varlistentry id="libpq-PQsslStruct">
3258     <term><function>PQsslStruct</function><indexterm><primary>PQsslStruct</primary></indexterm></term>
3259     <listitem>
3260      <para>
3261       Returns a pointer to an SSL-implementation-specific object describing
3262       the connection.  Returns NULL if the connection is not encrypted
3263       or the requested type of object is not available from the connection's
3264       SSL implementation.
3265<synopsis>
3266void *PQsslStruct(const PGconn *conn, const char *struct_name);
3267</synopsis>
3268      </para>
3269      <para>
3270       The struct(s) available depend on the SSL implementation in use.
3271       For <productname>OpenSSL</productname>, there is one struct,
3272       available under the name <literal>OpenSSL</literal>,
3273       and it returns a pointer to
3274       <productname>OpenSSL</productname>'s <literal>SSL</literal> struct.
3275       To use this function, code along the following lines could be used:
3276<programlisting><![CDATA[
3277#include <libpq-fe.h>
3278#include <openssl/ssl.h>
3279
3280...
3281
3282    SSL *ssl;
3283
3284    dbconn = PQconnectdb(...);
3285    ...
3286
3287    ssl = PQsslStruct(dbconn, "OpenSSL");
3288    if (ssl)
3289    {
3290        /* use OpenSSL functions to access ssl */
3291    }
3292]]></programlisting>
3293      </para>
3294      <para>
3295       This structure can be used to verify encryption levels, check server
3296       certificates, and more. Refer to the <productname>OpenSSL</productname>
3297       documentation for information about this structure.
3298      </para>
3299     </listitem>
3300    </varlistentry>
3301
3302    <varlistentry id="libpq-PQgetssl">
3303     <term><function>PQgetssl</function><indexterm><primary>PQgetssl</primary></indexterm></term>
3304     <listitem>
3305      <para>
3306       <indexterm><primary>SSL</primary><secondary sortas="libpq">in libpq</secondary></indexterm>
3307       Returns the SSL structure used in the connection, or NULL
3308       if SSL is not in use.
3309
3310<synopsis>
3311void *PQgetssl(const PGconn *conn);
3312</synopsis>
3313      </para>
3314
3315      <para>
3316       This function is equivalent to <literal>PQsslStruct(conn, "OpenSSL")</literal>. It should
3317       not be used in new applications, because the returned struct is
3318       specific to <productname>OpenSSL</productname> and will not be
3319       available if another <acronym>SSL</acronym> implementation is used.
3320       To check if a connection uses SSL, call
3321       <xref linkend="libpq-PQsslInUse"/> instead, and for more details about the
3322       connection, use <xref linkend="libpq-PQsslAttribute"/>.
3323      </para>
3324     </listitem>
3325    </varlistentry>
3326
3327   </variablelist>
3328  </para>
3329
3330 </sect1>
3331
3332 <sect1 id="libpq-exec">
3333  <title>Command Execution Functions</title>
3334
3335  <para>
3336   Once a connection to a database server has been successfully
3337   established, the functions described here are used to perform
3338   SQL queries and commands.
3339  </para>
3340
3341  <sect2 id="libpq-exec-main">
3342   <title>Main Functions</title>
3343
3344   <para>
3345    <variablelist>
3346     <varlistentry id="libpq-PQexec">
3347      <term><function>PQexec</function><indexterm><primary>PQexec</primary></indexterm></term>
3348
3349      <listitem>
3350       <para>
3351        Submits a command to the server and waits for the result.
3352
3353<synopsis>
3354PGresult *PQexec(PGconn *conn, const char *command);
3355</synopsis>
3356       </para>
3357
3358       <para>
3359        Returns a <structname>PGresult</structname> pointer or possibly a null
3360        pointer.  A non-null pointer will generally be returned except in
3361        out-of-memory conditions or serious errors such as inability to send
3362        the command to the server.  The <xref linkend="libpq-PQresultStatus"/> function
3363        should be called to check the return value for any errors (including
3364        the value of a null pointer, in which case it will return
3365        <symbol>PGRES_FATAL_ERROR</symbol>).  Use
3366        <xref linkend="libpq-PQerrorMessage"/> to get more information about such
3367        errors.
3368       </para>
3369      </listitem>
3370     </varlistentry>
3371    </variablelist>
3372
3373    The command string can include multiple SQL commands
3374    (separated by semicolons).  Multiple queries sent in a single
3375    <xref linkend="libpq-PQexec"/> call are processed in a single transaction, unless
3376    there are explicit <command>BEGIN</command>/<command>COMMIT</command>
3377    commands included in the query string to divide it into multiple
3378    transactions.  (See <xref linkend="protocol-flow-multi-statement"/>
3379    for more details about how the server handles multi-query strings.)
3380    Note however that the returned
3381    <structname>PGresult</structname> structure describes only the result
3382    of the last command executed from the string.  Should one of the
3383    commands fail, processing of the string stops with it and the returned
3384    <structname>PGresult</structname> describes the error condition.
3385   </para>
3386
3387   <para>
3388    <variablelist>
3389     <varlistentry id="libpq-PQexecParams">
3390      <term><function>PQexecParams</function><indexterm><primary>PQexecParams</primary></indexterm></term>
3391
3392      <listitem>
3393       <para>
3394        Submits a command to the server and waits for the result,
3395        with the ability to pass parameters separately from the SQL
3396        command text.
3397
3398<synopsis>
3399PGresult *PQexecParams(PGconn *conn,
3400                       const char *command,
3401                       int nParams,
3402                       const Oid *paramTypes,
3403                       const char * const *paramValues,
3404                       const int *paramLengths,
3405                       const int *paramFormats,
3406                       int resultFormat);
3407</synopsis>
3408       </para>
3409
3410       <para>
3411        <xref linkend="libpq-PQexecParams"/> is like <xref linkend="libpq-PQexec"/>, but offers additional
3412        functionality: parameter values can be specified separately from the command
3413        string proper, and query results can be requested in either text or binary
3414        format.
3415       </para>
3416
3417       <para>
3418        The function arguments are:
3419
3420        <variablelist>
3421         <varlistentry>
3422          <term><parameter>conn</parameter></term>
3423
3424          <listitem>
3425           <para>
3426            The connection object to send the command through.
3427           </para>
3428          </listitem>
3429         </varlistentry>
3430
3431         <varlistentry>
3432          <term><parameter>command</parameter></term>
3433          <listitem>
3434           <para>
3435            The SQL command string to be executed. If parameters are used,
3436            they are referred to in the command string as <literal>$1</literal>,
3437            <literal>$2</literal>, etc.
3438           </para>
3439          </listitem>
3440         </varlistentry>
3441
3442         <varlistentry>
3443          <term><parameter>nParams</parameter></term>
3444          <listitem>
3445           <para>
3446            The number of parameters supplied; it is the length of the arrays
3447            <parameter>paramTypes[]</parameter>, <parameter>paramValues[]</parameter>,
3448            <parameter>paramLengths[]</parameter>, and <parameter>paramFormats[]</parameter>. (The
3449            array pointers can be <symbol>NULL</symbol> when <parameter>nParams</parameter>
3450            is zero.)
3451           </para>
3452          </listitem>
3453         </varlistentry>
3454
3455         <varlistentry>
3456          <term><parameter>paramTypes[]</parameter></term>
3457          <listitem>
3458           <para>
3459            Specifies, by OID, the data types to be assigned to the
3460            parameter symbols.  If <parameter>paramTypes</parameter> is
3461            <symbol>NULL</symbol>, or any particular element in the array
3462            is zero, the server infers a data type for the parameter symbol
3463            in the same way it would do for an untyped literal string.
3464           </para>
3465          </listitem>
3466         </varlistentry>
3467
3468         <varlistentry>
3469          <term><parameter>paramValues[]</parameter></term>
3470          <listitem>
3471           <para>
3472            Specifies the actual values of the parameters.  A null pointer
3473            in this array means the corresponding parameter is null;
3474            otherwise the pointer points to a zero-terminated text string
3475            (for text format) or binary data in the format expected by the
3476            server (for binary format).
3477           </para>
3478          </listitem>
3479         </varlistentry>
3480
3481         <varlistentry>
3482          <term><parameter>paramLengths[]</parameter></term>
3483          <listitem>
3484           <para>
3485            Specifies the actual data lengths of binary-format parameters.
3486            It is ignored for null parameters and text-format parameters.
3487            The array pointer can be null when there are no binary parameters.
3488           </para>
3489          </listitem>
3490         </varlistentry>
3491
3492         <varlistentry>
3493          <term><parameter>paramFormats[]</parameter></term>
3494          <listitem>
3495           <para>
3496            Specifies whether parameters are text (put a zero in the
3497            array entry for the corresponding parameter) or binary (put
3498            a one in the array entry for the corresponding parameter).
3499            If the array pointer is null then all parameters are presumed
3500            to be text strings.
3501           </para>
3502           <para>
3503            Values passed in binary format require knowledge of
3504            the internal representation expected by the backend.
3505            For example, integers must be passed in network byte
3506            order.  Passing <type>numeric</type> values requires
3507            knowledge of the server storage format, as implemented
3508            in
3509            <filename>src/backend/utils/adt/numeric.c::numeric_send()</filename> and
3510            <filename>src/backend/utils/adt/numeric.c::numeric_recv()</filename>.
3511           </para>
3512          </listitem>
3513         </varlistentry>
3514
3515         <varlistentry>
3516          <term><parameter>resultFormat</parameter></term>
3517          <listitem>
3518           <para>
3519            Specify zero to obtain results in text format, or one to obtain
3520            results in binary format.  (There is not currently a provision
3521            to obtain different result columns in different formats,
3522            although that is possible in the underlying protocol.)
3523           </para>
3524          </listitem>
3525         </varlistentry>
3526        </variablelist>
3527       </para>
3528      </listitem>
3529     </varlistentry>
3530    </variablelist>
3531   </para>
3532
3533   <para>
3534    The primary advantage of <xref linkend="libpq-PQexecParams"/> over
3535    <xref linkend="libpq-PQexec"/> is that parameter values can be separated from the
3536    command string, thus avoiding the need for tedious and error-prone
3537    quoting and escaping.
3538   </para>
3539
3540   <para>
3541    Unlike <xref linkend="libpq-PQexec"/>, <xref linkend="libpq-PQexecParams"/> allows at most
3542    one SQL command in the given string.  (There can be semicolons in it,
3543    but not more than one nonempty command.)  This is a limitation of the
3544    underlying protocol, but has some usefulness as an extra defense against
3545    SQL-injection attacks.
3546   </para>
3547
3548   <tip>
3549    <para>
3550     Specifying parameter types via OIDs is tedious, particularly if you prefer
3551     not to hard-wire particular OID values into your program.  However, you can
3552     avoid doing so even in cases where the server by itself cannot determine the
3553     type of the parameter, or chooses a different type than you want.  In the
3554     SQL command text, attach an explicit cast to the parameter symbol to show what
3555     data type you will send.  For example:
3556<programlisting>
3557SELECT * FROM mytable WHERE x = $1::bigint;
3558</programlisting>
3559     This forces parameter <literal>$1</literal> to be treated as <type>bigint</type>, whereas
3560     by default it would be assigned the same type as <literal>x</literal>.  Forcing the
3561     parameter type decision, either this way or by specifying a numeric type OID,
3562     is strongly recommended when sending parameter values in binary format, because
3563     binary format has less redundancy than text format and so there is less chance
3564     that the server will detect a type mismatch mistake for you.
3565    </para>
3566   </tip>
3567
3568   <para>
3569    <variablelist>
3570     <varlistentry id="libpq-PQprepare">
3571      <term><function>PQprepare</function><indexterm><primary>PQprepare</primary></indexterm></term>
3572
3573      <listitem>
3574       <para>
3575        Submits a request to create a prepared statement with the
3576        given parameters, and waits for completion.
3577<synopsis>
3578PGresult *PQprepare(PGconn *conn,
3579                    const char *stmtName,
3580                    const char *query,
3581                    int nParams,
3582                    const Oid *paramTypes);
3583</synopsis>
3584       </para>
3585
3586       <para>
3587        <xref linkend="libpq-PQprepare"/> creates a prepared statement for later
3588        execution with <xref linkend="libpq-PQexecPrepared"/>.  This feature allows
3589        commands to be executed repeatedly without being parsed and
