3590        planned each time;  see <xref linkend="sql-prepare"/> for details.
3591       </para>
3592
3593       <para>
3594        The function creates a prepared statement named
3595        <parameter>stmtName</parameter> from the <parameter>query</parameter> string, which
3596        must contain a single SQL command.  <parameter>stmtName</parameter> can be
3597        <literal>""</literal> to create an unnamed statement, in which case any
3598        pre-existing unnamed statement is automatically replaced; otherwise
3599        it is an error if the statement name is already defined in the
3600        current session.  If any parameters are used, they are referred
3601        to in the query as <literal>$1</literal>, <literal>$2</literal>, etc.
3602        <parameter>nParams</parameter> is the number of parameters for which types
3603        are pre-specified in the array <parameter>paramTypes[]</parameter>.  (The
3604        array pointer can be <symbol>NULL</symbol> when
3605        <parameter>nParams</parameter> is zero.) <parameter>paramTypes[]</parameter>
3606        specifies, by OID, the data types to be assigned to the parameter
3607        symbols.  If <parameter>paramTypes</parameter> is <symbol>NULL</symbol>,
3608        or any particular element in the array is zero, the server assigns
3609        a data type to the parameter symbol in the same way it would do
3610        for an untyped literal string.  Also, the query can use parameter
3611        symbols with numbers higher than <parameter>nParams</parameter>; data types
3612        will be inferred for these symbols as well.  (See
3613        <xref linkend="libpq-PQdescribePrepared"/> for a means to find out
3614        what data types were inferred.)
3615       </para>
3616
3617       <para>
3618        As with <xref linkend="libpq-PQexec"/>, the result is normally a
3619        <structname>PGresult</structname> object whose contents indicate
3620        server-side success or failure.  A null result indicates
3621        out-of-memory or inability to send the command at all.  Use
3622        <xref linkend="libpq-PQerrorMessage"/> to get more information about
3623        such errors.
3624       </para>
3625      </listitem>
3626     </varlistentry>
3627    </variablelist>
3628
3629    Prepared statements for use with <xref linkend="libpq-PQexecPrepared"/> can also
3630    be created by executing SQL <xref linkend="sql-prepare"/>
3631    statements.
3632   </para>
3633
3634   <para>
3635    <variablelist>
3636     <varlistentry id="libpq-PQexecPrepared">
3637      <term><function>PQexecPrepared</function><indexterm><primary>PQexecPrepared</primary></indexterm></term>
3638
3639      <listitem>
3640       <para>
3641        Sends a request to execute a prepared statement with given
3642        parameters, and waits for the result.
3643<synopsis>
3644PGresult *PQexecPrepared(PGconn *conn,
3645                         const char *stmtName,
3646                         int nParams,
3647                         const char * const *paramValues,
3648                         const int *paramLengths,
3649                         const int *paramFormats,
3650                         int resultFormat);
3651</synopsis>
3652       </para>
3653
3654       <para>
3655        <xref linkend="libpq-PQexecPrepared"/> is like <xref linkend="libpq-PQexecParams"/>,
3656        but the command to be executed is specified by naming a
3657        previously-prepared statement, instead of giving a query string.
3658        This feature allows commands that will be used repeatedly to be
3659        parsed and planned just once, rather than each time they are
3660        executed.  The statement must have been prepared previously in
3661        the current session.
3662       </para>
3663
3664       <para>
3665        The parameters are identical to <xref linkend="libpq-PQexecParams"/>, except that the
3666        name of a prepared statement is given instead of a query string, and the
3667        <parameter>paramTypes[]</parameter> parameter is not present (it is not needed since
3668        the prepared statement's parameter types were determined when it was created).
3669       </para>
3670      </listitem>
3671     </varlistentry>
3672
3673     <varlistentry id="libpq-PQdescribePrepared">
3674      <term><function>PQdescribePrepared</function><indexterm><primary>PQdescribePrepared</primary></indexterm></term>
3675
3676      <listitem>
3677       <para>
3678        Submits a request to obtain information about the specified
3679        prepared statement, and waits for completion.
3680<synopsis>
3681PGresult *PQdescribePrepared(PGconn *conn, const char *stmtName);
3682</synopsis>
3683       </para>
3684
3685       <para>
3686        <xref linkend="libpq-PQdescribePrepared"/> allows an application to obtain
3687        information about a previously prepared statement.
3688       </para>
3689
3690       <para>
3691        <parameter>stmtName</parameter> can be <literal>""</literal> or <symbol>NULL</symbol> to reference
3692        the unnamed statement, otherwise it must be the name of an existing
3693        prepared statement.  On success, a <structname>PGresult</structname> with
3694        status <literal>PGRES_COMMAND_OK</literal> is returned.  The
3695        functions <xref linkend="libpq-PQnparams"/> and
3696        <xref linkend="libpq-PQparamtype"/> can be applied to this
3697        <structname>PGresult</structname> to obtain information about the parameters
3698        of the prepared statement, and the functions
3699        <xref linkend="libpq-PQnfields"/>, <xref linkend="libpq-PQfname"/>,
3700        <xref linkend="libpq-PQftype"/>, etc. provide information about the
3701        result columns (if any) of the statement.
3702       </para>
3703      </listitem>
3704     </varlistentry>
3705
3706     <varlistentry id="libpq-PQdescribePortal">
3707      <term><function>PQdescribePortal</function><indexterm><primary>PQdescribePortal</primary></indexterm></term>
3708
3709      <listitem>
3710       <para>
3711        Submits a request to obtain information about the specified
3712        portal, and waits for completion.
3713<synopsis>
3714PGresult *PQdescribePortal(PGconn *conn, const char *portalName);
3715</synopsis>
3716       </para>
3717
3718       <para>
3719        <xref linkend="libpq-PQdescribePortal"/> allows an application to obtain
3720        information about a previously created portal.
3721        (<application>libpq</application> does not provide any direct access to
3722        portals, but you can use this function to inspect the properties
3723        of a cursor created with a <command>DECLARE CURSOR</command> SQL command.)
3724       </para>
3725
3726       <para>
3727        <parameter>portalName</parameter> can be <literal>""</literal> or <symbol>NULL</symbol> to reference
3728        the unnamed portal, otherwise it must be the name of an existing
3729        portal.  On success, a <structname>PGresult</structname> with status
3730        <literal>PGRES_COMMAND_OK</literal> is returned.  The functions
3731        <xref linkend="libpq-PQnfields"/>, <xref linkend="libpq-PQfname"/>,
3732        <xref linkend="libpq-PQftype"/>, etc. can be applied to the
3733        <structname>PGresult</structname> to obtain information about the result
3734        columns (if any) of the portal.
3735       </para>
3736      </listitem>
3737     </varlistentry>
3738
3739     <varlistentry id="libpq-PQclosePrepared">
3740      <term><function>PQclosePrepared</function><indexterm><primary>PQclosePrepared</primary></indexterm></term>
3741
3742      <listitem>
3743       <para>
3744        Submits a request to close the specified prepared statement, and waits
3745        for completion.
3746<synopsis>
3747PGresult *PQclosePrepared(PGconn *conn, const char *stmtName);
3748</synopsis>
3749       </para>
3750
3751       <para>
3752        <xref linkend="libpq-PQclosePrepared"/> allows an application to close
3753        a previously prepared statement. Closing a statement releases all
3754        of its associated resources on the server and allows its name to be
3755        reused.
3756       </para>
3757
3758       <para>
3759        <parameter>stmtName</parameter> can be <literal>""</literal> or
3760        <symbol>NULL</symbol> to reference the unnamed statement. It is fine
3761        if no statement exists with this name, in that case the operation is a
3762        no-op. On success, a <structname>PGresult</structname> with
3763        status <literal>PGRES_COMMAND_OK</literal> is returned.
3764       </para>
3765      </listitem>
3766     </varlistentry>
3767
3768     <varlistentry id="libpq-PQclosePortal">
3769      <term><function>PQclosePortal</function><indexterm><primary>PQclosePortal</primary></indexterm></term>
3770
3771      <listitem>
3772       <para>
3773        Submits a request to close the specified portal, and waits for
3774        completion.
3775<synopsis>
3776PGresult *PQclosePortal(PGconn *conn, const char *portalName);
3777</synopsis>
3778       </para>
3779
3780       <para>
3781        <xref linkend="libpq-PQclosePortal"/> allows an application to trigger
3782        a close of a previously created portal. Closing a portal releases all
3783        of its associated resources on the server and allows its name to be
3784        reused. (<application>libpq</application> does not provide any
3785        direct access to portals, but you can use this function to close a
3786        cursor created with a <command>DECLARE CURSOR</command> SQL command.)
3787       </para>
3788
3789       <para>
3790        <parameter>portalName</parameter> can be <literal>""</literal> or
3791        <symbol>NULL</symbol> to reference the unnamed portal. It is fine
3792        if no portal exists with this name, in that case the operation is a
3793        no-op. On success, a <structname>PGresult</structname> with status
3794        <literal>PGRES_COMMAND_OK</literal> is returned.
3795       </para>
3796      </listitem>
3797     </varlistentry>
3798    </variablelist>
3799   </para>
3800
3801   <para>
3802    The <structname>PGresult</structname><indexterm><primary>PGresult</primary></indexterm>
3803    structure encapsulates the result returned by the server.
3804    <application>libpq</application> application programmers should be
3805    careful to maintain the <structname>PGresult</structname> abstraction.
3806    Use the accessor functions below to get at the contents of
3807    <structname>PGresult</structname>.  Avoid directly referencing the
3808    fields of the <structname>PGresult</structname> structure because they
3809    are subject to change in the future.
3810
3811    <variablelist>
3812     <varlistentry id="libpq-PQresultStatus">
3813      <term><function>PQresultStatus</function><indexterm><primary>PQresultStatus</primary></indexterm></term>
3814
3815      <listitem>
3816       <para>
3817        Returns the result status of the command.
3818<synopsis>
3819ExecStatusType PQresultStatus(const PGresult *res);
3820</synopsis>
3821       </para>
3822
3823       <para>
3824        <xref linkend="libpq-PQresultStatus"/> can return one of the following values:
3825
3826        <variablelist>
3827         <varlistentry id="libpq-pgres-empty-query">
3828          <term><literal>PGRES_EMPTY_QUERY</literal></term>
3829          <listitem>
3830           <para>
3831            The string sent to the server was empty.
3832           </para>
3833          </listitem>
3834         </varlistentry>
3835
3836         <varlistentry id="libpq-pgres-command-ok">
3837          <term><literal>PGRES_COMMAND_OK</literal></term>
3838          <listitem>
3839           <para>
3840            Successful completion of a command returning no data.
3841           </para>
3842          </listitem>
3843         </varlistentry>
3844
3845         <varlistentry id="libpq-pgres-tuples-ok">
3846          <term><literal>PGRES_TUPLES_OK</literal></term>
3847          <listitem>
3848           <para>
3849            Successful completion of a command returning data (such as
3850            a <command>SELECT</command> or <command>SHOW</command>).
3851           </para>
3852          </listitem>
3853         </varlistentry>
3854
3855         <varlistentry id="libpq-pgres-copy-out">
3856          <term><literal>PGRES_COPY_OUT</literal></term>
3857          <listitem>
3858           <para>
3859            Copy Out (from server) data transfer started.
3860           </para>
3861          </listitem>
3862         </varlistentry>
3863
3864         <varlistentry id="libpq-pgres-copy-in">
3865          <term><literal>PGRES_COPY_IN</literal></term>
3866          <listitem>
3867           <para>
3868            Copy In (to server) data transfer started.
3869           </para>
3870          </listitem>
3871         </varlistentry>
3872
3873         <varlistentry id="libpq-pgres-bad-response">
3874          <term><literal>PGRES_BAD_RESPONSE</literal></term>
3875          <listitem>
3876           <para>
3877            The server's response was not understood.
3878           </para>
3879          </listitem>
3880         </varlistentry>
3881
3882         <varlistentry id="libpq-pgres-nonfatal-error">
3883          <term><literal>PGRES_NONFATAL_ERROR</literal></term>
3884          <listitem>
3885           <para>
3886            A nonfatal error (a notice or warning) occurred.
3887           </para>
3888          </listitem>
3889         </varlistentry>
3890
3891         <varlistentry id="libpq-pgres-fatal-error">
3892          <term><literal>PGRES_FATAL_ERROR</literal></term>
3893          <listitem>
3894           <para>
3895            A fatal error occurred.
3896           </para>
3897          </listitem>
3898         </varlistentry>
3899
3900         <varlistentry id="libpq-pgres-copy-both">
3901          <term><literal>PGRES_COPY_BOTH</literal></term>
3902          <listitem>
3903           <para>
3904            Copy In/Out (to and from server) data transfer started.  This
3905            feature is currently used only for streaming replication,
3906            so this status should not occur in ordinary applications.
3907           </para>
3908          </listitem>
3909         </varlistentry>
3910
3911         <varlistentry id="libpq-pgres-single-tuple">
3912          <term><literal>PGRES_SINGLE_TUPLE</literal></term>
3913          <listitem>
3914           <para>
3915            The <structname>PGresult</structname> contains a single result tuple
3916            from the current command.  This status occurs only when
3917            single-row mode has been selected for the query
3918            (see <xref linkend="libpq-single-row-mode"/>).
3919           </para>
3920          </listitem>
3921         </varlistentry>
3922
3923         <varlistentry id="libpq-pgres-tuples-chunk">
3924          <term><literal>PGRES_TUPLES_CHUNK</literal></term>
3925          <listitem>
3926           <para>
3927            The <structname>PGresult</structname> contains several result tuples
3928            from the current command.  This status occurs only when
3929            chunked mode has been selected for the query
3930            (see <xref linkend="libpq-single-row-mode"/>).
3931            The number of tuples will not exceed the limit passed to
3932            <xref linkend="libpq-PQsetChunkedRowsMode"/>.
3933           </para>
3934          </listitem>
3935         </varlistentry>
3936
3937         <varlistentry id="libpq-pgres-pipeline-sync">
3938          <term><literal>PGRES_PIPELINE_SYNC</literal></term>
3939          <listitem>
3940           <para>
3941            The <structname>PGresult</structname> represents a
3942            synchronization point in pipeline mode, requested by either
3943            <xref linkend="libpq-PQpipelineSync"/> or
3944            <xref linkend="libpq-PQsendPipelineSync"/>.
3945            This status occurs only when pipeline mode has been selected.
3946           </para>
3947          </listitem>
3948         </varlistentry>
3949
3950         <varlistentry id="libpq-pgres-pipeline-aborted">
3951          <term><literal>PGRES_PIPELINE_ABORTED</literal></term>
3952          <listitem>
3953           <para>
3954            The <structname>PGresult</structname> represents a pipeline that has
3955            received an error from the server.  <function>PQgetResult</function>
3956            must be called repeatedly, and each time it will return this status code
3957            until the end of the current pipeline, at which point it will return
3958            <literal>PGRES_PIPELINE_SYNC</literal> and normal processing can
3959            resume.
3960           </para>
3961          </listitem>
3962         </varlistentry>
3963
3964        </variablelist>
3965
3966        If the result status is <literal>PGRES_TUPLES_OK</literal>,
3967        <literal>PGRES_SINGLE_TUPLE</literal>, or
3968        <literal>PGRES_TUPLES_CHUNK</literal>, then
3969        the functions described below can be used to retrieve the rows
3970        returned by the query.  Note that a <command>SELECT</command>
3971        command that happens to retrieve zero rows still shows
3972        <literal>PGRES_TUPLES_OK</literal>.
3973        <literal>PGRES_COMMAND_OK</literal> is for commands that can never
3974        return rows (<command>INSERT</command> or <command>UPDATE</command>
3975        without a <literal>RETURNING</literal> clause,
3976        etc.). A response of <literal>PGRES_EMPTY_QUERY</literal> might
3977        indicate a bug in the client software.
3978       </para>
3979
3980       <para>
3981        A result of status <symbol>PGRES_NONFATAL_ERROR</symbol> will
3982        never be returned directly by <xref linkend="libpq-PQexec"/> or other
3983        query execution functions; results of this kind are instead passed
3984        to the notice processor (see <xref
3985        linkend="libpq-notice-processing"/>).
3986       </para>
3987      </listitem>
3988     </varlistentry>
3989
3990     <varlistentry id="libpq-PQresStatus">
3991      <term><function>PQresStatus</function><indexterm><primary>PQresStatus</primary></indexterm></term>
3992
3993      <listitem>
3994       <para>
3995        Converts the enumerated type returned by
3996        <xref linkend="libpq-PQresultStatus"/> into a string constant describing the
3997        status code. The caller should not free the result.
3998
3999<synopsis>
4000char *PQresStatus(ExecStatusType status);
4001</synopsis>
4002       </para>
4003      </listitem>
4004     </varlistentry>
4005
4006     <varlistentry id="libpq-PQresultErrorMessage">
4007      <term><function>PQresultErrorMessage</function><indexterm><primary>PQresultErrorMessage</primary></indexterm></term>
4008
4009      <listitem>
4010       <para>
4011        Returns the error message associated with the command, or an empty string
4012        if there was no error.
4013<synopsis>
4014char *PQresultErrorMessage(const PGresult *res);
4015</synopsis>
4016        If there was an error, the returned string will include a trailing
4017        newline.  The caller should not free the result directly. It will
4018        be freed when the associated <structname>PGresult</structname> handle is
4019        passed to <xref linkend="libpq-PQclear"/>.
4020       </para>
4021
4022       <para>
4023        Immediately following a <xref linkend="libpq-PQexec"/> or
4024        <xref linkend="libpq-PQgetResult"/> call,
4025        <xref linkend="libpq-PQerrorMessage"/> (on the connection) will return
4026        the same string as <xref linkend="libpq-PQresultErrorMessage"/> (on
4027        the result).  However, a <structname>PGresult</structname> will
4028        retain its error message until destroyed, whereas the connection's
4029        error message will change when subsequent operations are done.
4030        Use <xref linkend="libpq-PQresultErrorMessage"/> when you want to
4031        know the status associated with a particular
4032        <structname>PGresult</structname>; use
4033        <xref linkend="libpq-PQerrorMessage"/> when you want to know the
4034        status from the latest operation on the connection.
4035       </para>
4036      </listitem>
4037     </varlistentry>
4038
4039     <varlistentry id="libpq-PQresultVerboseErrorMessage">
4040      <term><function>PQresultVerboseErrorMessage</function><indexterm><primary>PQresultVerboseErrorMessage</primary></indexterm></term>
4041
4042      <listitem>
4043       <para>
4044        Returns a reformatted version of the error message associated with
4045        a <structname>PGresult</structname> object.
4046<synopsis>
4047char *PQresultVerboseErrorMessage(const PGresult *res,
4048                                  PGVerbosity verbosity,
4049                                  PGContextVisibility show_context);
4050</synopsis>
4051        In some situations a client might wish to obtain a more detailed
4052        version of a previously-reported error.
4053        <xref linkend="libpq-PQresultVerboseErrorMessage"/> addresses this need
4054        by computing the message that would have been produced
4055        by <xref linkend="libpq-PQresultErrorMessage"/> if the specified
4056        verbosity settings had been in effect for the connection when the
4057        given <structname>PGresult</structname> was generated.  If
4058        the <structname>PGresult</structname> is not an error result,
4059        <quote>PGresult is not an error result</quote> is reported instead.
4060        The returned string includes a trailing newline.
4061       </para>
4062
4063       <para>
4064        Unlike most other functions for extracting data from
4065        a <structname>PGresult</structname>, the result of this function is a freshly
4066        allocated string.  The caller must free it
4067        using <function>PQfreemem()</function> when the string is no longer needed.
4068       </para>
4069
4070       <para>
4071        A NULL return is possible if there is insufficient memory.
4072       </para>
4073      </listitem>
4074     </varlistentry>
4075
4076     <varlistentry id="libpq-PQresultErrorField">
4077      <term><function>PQresultErrorField</function><indexterm><primary>PQresultErrorField</primary></indexterm></term>
4078      <listitem>
4079       <para>
4080        Returns an individual field of an error report.
4081<synopsis>
4082char *PQresultErrorField(const PGresult *res, int fieldcode);
4083</synopsis>
4084        <parameter>fieldcode</parameter> is an error field identifier; see the symbols
4085        listed below.  <symbol>NULL</symbol> is returned if the
4086        <structname>PGresult</structname> is not an error or warning result,
4087        or does not include the specified field.  Field values will normally
4088        not include a trailing newline. The caller should not free the
4089        result directly. It will be freed when the
4090        associated <structname>PGresult</structname> handle is passed to
4091        <xref linkend="libpq-PQclear"/>.
4092       </para>
4093
4094       <para>
4095        The following field codes are available:
4096        <variablelist>
4097         <varlistentry id="libpq-pg-diag-severity">
4098          <term><symbol>PG_DIAG_SEVERITY</symbol></term>
4099          <listitem>
4100           <para>
4101            The severity; the field contents are <literal>ERROR</literal>,
4102            <literal>FATAL</literal>, or <literal>PANIC</literal> (in an error message),
4103            or <literal>WARNING</literal>, <literal>NOTICE</literal>, <literal>DEBUG</literal>,
4104            <literal>INFO</literal>, or <literal>LOG</literal> (in a notice message), or
4105            a localized translation of one of these.  Always present.
4106           </para>
4107          </listitem>
4108         </varlistentry>
4109
4110         <varlistentry id="libpq-PG-diag-severity-nonlocalized">
4111          <term><symbol>PG_DIAG_SEVERITY_NONLOCALIZED</symbol></term>
4112          <listitem>
4113           <para>
4114            The severity; the field contents are <literal>ERROR</literal>,
4115            <literal>FATAL</literal>, or <literal>PANIC</literal> (in an error message),
4116            or <literal>WARNING</literal>, <literal>NOTICE</literal>, <literal>DEBUG</literal>,
4117            <literal>INFO</literal>, or <literal>LOG</literal> (in a notice message).
4118            This is identical to the <symbol>PG_DIAG_SEVERITY</symbol> field except
4119            that the contents are never localized.  This is present only in
4120            reports generated by <productname>PostgreSQL</productname> versions 9.6
4121            and later.
4122           </para>
4123          </listitem>
4124         </varlistentry>
4125
4126         <varlistentry id="libpq-pg-diag-sqlstate">
4127          <term><symbol>PG_DIAG_SQLSTATE</symbol><indexterm
4128          ><primary>error codes</primary><secondary>libpq</secondary></indexterm></term>
4129          <listitem>
4130           <para>
4131            The SQLSTATE code for the error. The SQLSTATE code identifies
4132            the type of error that has occurred; it can be used by
4133            front-end applications to perform specific operations (such
4134            as error handling) in response to a particular database error.
4135            For a list of the possible SQLSTATE codes, see <xref
4136            linkend="errcodes-appendix"/>. This field is not localizable,
4137            and is always present.
4138           </para>
4139          </listitem>
4140         </varlistentry>
4141
4142         <varlistentry id="libpq-pg-diag-message-primary">
4143          <term><symbol>PG_DIAG_MESSAGE_PRIMARY</symbol></term>
4144          <listitem>
4145           <para>
4146            The primary human-readable error message (typically one line).
4147            Always present.
4148           </para>
4149          </listitem>
4150         </varlistentry>
4151
4152         <varlistentry id="libpq-pg-diag-message-detail">
4153          <term><symbol>PG_DIAG_MESSAGE_DETAIL</symbol></term>
4154          <listitem>
4155           <para>
4156            Detail: an optional secondary error message carrying more
4157            detail about the problem.  Might run to multiple lines.
4158           </para>
4159          </listitem>
4160         </varlistentry>
4161
4162         <varlistentry id="libpq-pg-diag-message-hint">
4163          <term><symbol>PG_DIAG_MESSAGE_HINT</symbol></term>
4164          <listitem>
4165           <para>
4166            Hint: an optional suggestion what to do about the problem.
4167            This is intended to differ from detail in that it offers advice
4168            (potentially inappropriate) rather than hard facts.  Might
4169            run to multiple lines.
4170           </para>
4171          </listitem>
4172         </varlistentry>
4173
4174         <varlistentry id="libpq-pg-diag-statement-position">
4175          <term><symbol>PG_DIAG_STATEMENT_POSITION</symbol></term>
4176          <listitem>
4177           <para>
4178            A string containing a decimal integer indicating an error cursor
4179            position as an index into the original statement string.  The
4180            first character has index 1, and positions are measured in
4181            characters not bytes.
4182           </para>
4183          </listitem>
4184         </varlistentry>
4185
4186         <varlistentry id="libpq-pg-diag-internal-position">
4187          <term><symbol>PG_DIAG_INTERNAL_POSITION</symbol></term>
4188          <listitem>
4189           <para>
4190            This is defined the same as the
4191            <symbol>PG_DIAG_STATEMENT_POSITION</symbol> field, but it is used
4192            when the cursor position refers to an internally generated
4193            command rather than the one submitted by the client.  The
4194            <symbol>PG_DIAG_INTERNAL_QUERY</symbol> field will always appear when
4195            this field appears.
4196           </para>
4197          </listitem>
4198         </varlistentry>
4199
4200         <varlistentry id="libpq-pg-diag-internal-query">
4201          <term><symbol>PG_DIAG_INTERNAL_QUERY</symbol></term>
4202          <listitem>
4203           <para>
4204            The text of a failed internally-generated command.  This could
4205            be, for example, an SQL query issued by a PL/pgSQL function.
4206           </para>
4207          </listitem>
4208         </varlistentry>
4209
4210         <varlistentry id="libpq-pg-diag-context">
4211          <term><symbol>PG_DIAG_CONTEXT</symbol></term>
4212          <listitem>
4213           <para>
4214            An indication of the context in which the error occurred.
4215            Presently this includes a call stack traceback of active
4216            procedural language functions and internally-generated queries.
4217            The trace is one entry per line, most recent first.
4218           </para>
4219          </listitem>
4220         </varlistentry>
4221
4222         <varlistentry id="libpq-pg-diag-schema-name">
4223          <term><symbol>PG_DIAG_SCHEMA_NAME</symbol></term>
4224          <listitem>
4225           <para>
4226            If the error was associated with a specific database object,
4227            the name of the schema containing that object, if any.
4228           </para>
4229          </listitem>
4230         </varlistentry>
4231
4232         <varlistentry id="libpq-pg-diag-table-name">
4233          <term><symbol>PG_DIAG_TABLE_NAME</symbol></term>
4234          <listitem>
4235           <para>
4236            If the error was associated with a specific table, the name of the
4237            table.  (Refer to the schema name field for the name of the
4238            table's schema.)
4239           </para>
4240          </listitem>
4241         </varlistentry>
4242
4243         <varlistentry id="libpq-pg-diag-column-name">
4244          <term><symbol>PG_DIAG_COLUMN_NAME</symbol></term>
4245          <listitem>
4246           <para>
4247            If the error was associated with a specific table column, the name
4248            of the column.  (Refer to the schema and table name fields to
4249            identify the table.)
4250           </para>
4251          </listitem>
4252         </varlistentry>
4253
4254         <varlistentry id="libpq-pg-diag-datatype-name">
4255          <term><symbol>PG_DIAG_DATATYPE_NAME</symbol></term>
4256          <listitem>
4257           <para>
4258            If the error was associated with a specific data type, the name of
4259            the data type.  (Refer to the schema name field for the name of
4260            the data type's schema.)
4261           </para>
4262          </listitem>
4263         </varlistentry>
4264
4265         <varlistentry id="libpq-pg-diag-constraint-name">
4266          <term><symbol>PG_DIAG_CONSTRAINT_NAME</symbol></term>
4267          <listitem>
4268           <para>
4269            If the error was associated with a specific constraint, the name
4270            of the constraint.  Refer to fields listed above for the
4271            associated table or domain.  (For this purpose, indexes are
4272            treated as constraints, even if they weren't created with
4273            constraint syntax.)
4274           </para>
4275          </listitem>
4276         </varlistentry>
4277
4278         <varlistentry id="libpq-pg-diag-source-file">
4279          <term><symbol>PG_DIAG_SOURCE_FILE</symbol></term>
4280          <listitem>
4281           <para>
4282            The file name of the source-code location where the error was
4283            reported.
4284           </para>
4285          </listitem>
4286         </varlistentry>
4287
4288         <varlistentry id="libpq-pg-diag-source-line">
4289          <term><symbol>PG_DIAG_SOURCE_LINE</symbol></term>
4290          <listitem>
4291           <para>
4292            The line number of the source-code location where the error
4293            was reported.
4294           </para>
4295          </listitem>
4296         </varlistentry>
4297
4298         <varlistentry id="libpq-pg-diag-source-function">
4299          <term><symbol>PG_DIAG_SOURCE_FUNCTION</symbol></term>
4300          <listitem>
4301           <para>
4302            The name of the source-code function reporting the error.
4303           </para>
4304          </listitem>
4305         </varlistentry>
4306        </variablelist>
4307       </para>
4308
4309       <note>
4310        <para>
4311         The fields for schema name, table name, column name, data type name,
4312         and constraint name are supplied only for a limited number of error
4313         types; see <xref linkend="errcodes-appendix"/>.  Do not assume that
4314         the presence of any of these fields guarantees the presence of
4315         another field.  Core error sources observe the interrelationships
4316         noted above, but user-defined functions may use these fields in other
4317         ways.  In the same vein, do not assume that these fields denote
4318         contemporary objects in the current database.
4319        </para>
4320       </note>
4321
4322       <para>
4323        The client is responsible for formatting displayed information to meet
4324        its needs; in particular it should break long lines as needed.
4325        Newline characters appearing in the error message fields should be
4326        treated as paragraph breaks, not line breaks.
4327       </para>
4328
4329       <para>
4330        Errors generated internally by <application>libpq</application> will
4331        have severity and primary message, but typically no other fields.
4332       </para>
4333
4334       <para>
4335        Note that error fields are only available from
4336        <structname>PGresult</structname> objects, not
4337        <structname>PGconn</structname> objects; there is no
4338        <function>PQerrorField</function> function.
4339       </para>
4340      </listitem>
4341     </varlistentry>
4342
4343     <varlistentry id="libpq-PQclear">
4344      <term><function>PQclear</function><indexterm><primary>PQclear</primary></indexterm></term>
4345      <listitem>
4346       <para>
4347        Frees  the  storage  associated with a
4348        <structname>PGresult</structname>.  Every command result should be
4349        freed via <xref linkend="libpq-PQclear"/> when it  is  no  longer
4350        needed.
4351
4352<synopsis>
4353void PQclear(PGresult *res);
4354</synopsis>
4355
4356        If the argument is a <symbol>NULL</symbol> pointer, no operation is
4357        performed.
4358       </para>
4359
4360       <para>
4361        You can keep a <structname>PGresult</structname> object around for
4362        as long as you need it; it does not go away when you issue a new
4363        command, nor even if you close the connection.  To get rid of it,
4364        you must call <xref linkend="libpq-PQclear"/>.  Failure to do this
4365        will result in memory leaks in your application.
4366       </para>
4367      </listitem>
4368     </varlistentry>
4369    </variablelist>
4370   </para>
4371  </sect2>
4372
4373  <sect2 id="libpq-exec-select-info">
4374   <title>Retrieving Query Result Information</title>
4375
4376   <para>
4377    These functions are used to extract information from a
4378    <structname>PGresult</structname> object that represents a successful
4379    query result (that is, one that has status
4380    <literal>PGRES_TUPLES_OK</literal>,
4381    <literal>PGRES_SINGLE_TUPLE</literal>, or
4382    <literal>PGRES_TUPLES_CHUNK</literal>).
4383    They can also be used to extract
4384    information from a successful Describe operation: a Describe's result
4385    has all the same column information that actual execution of the query
4386    would provide, but it has zero rows.  For objects with other status values,
4387    these functions will act as though the result has zero rows and zero columns.
4388   </para>
4389
4390   <variablelist>
4391    <varlistentry id="libpq-PQntuples">
4392     <term><function>PQntuples</function><indexterm><primary>PQntuples</primary></indexterm></term>
4393
4394     <listitem>
4395      <para>
4396       Returns the number of rows (tuples) in the query result.
4397       (Note that <structname>PGresult</structname> objects are limited to no more
4398       than <literal>INT_MAX</literal> rows, so an <type>int</type> result is
4399       sufficient.)
4400
4401<synopsis>
4402int PQntuples(const PGresult *res);
4403</synopsis>
4404
4405      </para>
4406     </listitem>
4407    </varlistentry>
4408
4409    <varlistentry id="libpq-PQnfields">
4410     <term><function>PQnfields</function><indexterm><primary>PQnfields</primary></indexterm></term>
4411
4412     <listitem>
4413      <para>
4414       Returns the number of columns (fields) in each row of the query
4415       result.
4416
4417<synopsis>
4418int PQnfields(const PGresult *res);
4419</synopsis>
4420      </para>
4421     </listitem>
4422    </varlistentry>
4423
4424    <varlistentry id="libpq-PQfname">
4425     <term><function>PQfname</function><indexterm><primary>PQfname</primary></indexterm></term>
4426
4427     <listitem>
4428      <para>
4429       Returns the column name associated with the given column number.
4430       Column numbers start at 0. The caller should not free the result
4431       directly. It will be freed when the associated
4432       <structname>PGresult</structname> handle is passed to
4433       <xref linkend="libpq-PQclear"/>.
4434<synopsis>
4435char *PQfname(const PGresult *res,
4436              int column_number);
4437</synopsis>
4438      </para>
4439
4440      <para>
4441       <symbol>NULL</symbol> is returned if the column number is out of range.
4442      </para>
4443     </listitem>
4444    </varlistentry>
4445
4446    <varlistentry id="libpq-PQfnumber">
4447     <term><function>PQfnumber</function><indexterm><primary>PQfnumber</primary></indexterm></term>
4448
4449     <listitem>
4450      <para>
4451       Returns the column number associated with the given column name.
4452<synopsis>
4453int PQfnumber(const PGresult *res,
4454              const char *column_name);
4455</synopsis>
4456      </para>
4457
4458      <para>
4459       -1 is returned if the given name does not match any column.
4460      </para>
4461
4462      <para>
4463       The given name is treated like an identifier in an SQL command,
4464       that is, it is downcased unless double-quoted.  For example, given
4465       a query result generated from the SQL command:
4466<programlisting>
4467SELECT 1 AS FOO, 2 AS "BAR";
4468</programlisting>
4469       we would have the results:
4470<programlisting>
4471PQfname(res, 0)              <lineannotation>foo</lineannotation>
4472PQfname(res, 1)              <lineannotation>BAR</lineannotation>
4473PQfnumber(res, "FOO")        <lineannotation>0</lineannotation>
4474PQfnumber(res, "foo")        <lineannotation>0</lineannotation>
4475PQfnumber(res, "BAR")        <lineannotation>-1</lineannotation>
4476PQfnumber(res, "\"BAR\"")    <lineannotation>1</lineannotation>
4477</programlisting>
4478      </para>
4479     </listitem>
4480    </varlistentry>
4481
4482    <varlistentry id="libpq-PQftable">
4483     <term><function>PQftable</function><indexterm><primary>PQftable</primary></indexterm></term>
4484
4485     <listitem>
4486      <para>
4487       Returns the OID of the table from which the given column was
4488       fetched.  Column numbers start at 0.
4489<synopsis>
4490Oid PQftable(const PGresult *res,
4491             int column_number);
4492</synopsis>
4493      </para>
4494
4495      <para>
4496       <literal>InvalidOid</literal> is returned if the column number is out of range,
4497       or if the specified column is not a simple reference to a table column.
4498       You can query the system table <literal>pg_class</literal> to determine
4499       exactly which table is referenced.
4500      </para>
4501
4502      <para>
4503       The type <type>Oid</type> and the constant
4504       <literal>InvalidOid</literal> will be defined when you include
4505       the <application>libpq</application> header file. They will both
4506       be some integer type.
4507      </para>
4508     </listitem>
4509    </varlistentry>
4510
4511    <varlistentry id="libpq-PQftablecol">
4512     <term><function>PQftablecol</function><indexterm><primary>PQftablecol</primary></indexterm></term>
4513
4514     <listitem>
4515      <para>
4516       Returns the column number (within its table) of the column making
4517       up the specified query result column.  Query-result column numbers
4518       start at 0, but table columns have nonzero numbers.
4519<synopsis>
4520int PQftablecol(const PGresult *res,
4521                int column_number);
4522</synopsis>
4523      </para>
4524
4525      <para>
4526       Zero is returned if the column number is out of range, or if the
4527       specified column is not a simple reference to a table column.
4528      </para>
4529     </listitem>
4530    </varlistentry>
4531
4532    <varlistentry id="libpq-PQfformat">
4533     <term><function>PQfformat</function><indexterm><primary>PQfformat</primary></indexterm></term>
4534
4535     <listitem>
4536      <para>
4537       Returns the format code indicating the format of the given
4538       column.  Column numbers start at 0.
4539<synopsis>
4540int PQfformat(const PGresult *res,
4541              int column_number);
4542</synopsis>
4543      </para>
4544
4545      <para>
4546       Format code zero indicates textual data representation, while format
4547       code one indicates binary representation.  (Other codes are reserved
4548       for future definition.)
4549      </para>
4550     </listitem>
4551    </varlistentry>
4552
4553    <varlistentry id="libpq-PQftype">
4554     <term><function>PQftype</function><indexterm><primary>PQftype</primary></indexterm></term>
4555
4556     <listitem>
4557      <para>
4558       Returns the data type associated with the given  column number.
4559       The  integer  returned is the internal OID number of the type.
4560       Column numbers start at 0.
4561<synopsis>
4562Oid PQftype(const PGresult *res,
4563            int column_number);
4564</synopsis>
4565      </para>
4566
4567      <para>
4568       You can query the system table <literal>pg_type</literal> to
4569       obtain the names and properties of the various data types. The
4570       <acronym>OID</acronym>s of the built-in data types are defined
4571       in the file <filename>catalog/pg_type_d.h</filename>
4572       in the <productname>PostgreSQL</productname>
4573       installation's <filename>include</filename> directory.
4574      </para>
4575     </listitem>
4576    </varlistentry>
4577
4578    <varlistentry id="libpq-PQfmod">
4579     <term><function>PQfmod</function><indexterm><primary>PQfmod</primary></indexterm></term>
4580
4581     <listitem>
4582      <para>
4583       Returns  the type modifier of the column associated with the
4584       given column number.  Column numbers start at 0.
4585<synopsis>
4586int PQfmod(const PGresult *res,
4587           int column_number);
4588</synopsis>
4589      </para>
4590
4591      <para>
4592       The interpretation of modifier values is type-specific; they
4593       typically indicate precision or size limits.  The value -1 is
4594       used to indicate <quote>no information available</quote>.  Most data
4595       types do not use modifiers, in which case the value is always
4596       -1.
4597      </para>
4598     </listitem>
4599    </varlistentry>
4600
4601    <varlistentry id="libpq-PQfsize">
4602     <term><function>PQfsize</function><indexterm><primary>PQfsize</primary></indexterm></term>
4603
4604     <listitem>
4605      <para>
4606       Returns  the  size  in bytes of the column associated with the
4607       given column number.  Column numbers start at 0.
4608<synopsis>
4609int PQfsize(const PGresult *res,
4610            int column_number);
4611</synopsis>
4612      </para>
4613
4614      <para>
4615       <xref linkend="libpq-PQfsize"/> returns the space allocated for this column
4616       in a database row, in other words the size of the server's
4617       internal representation of the data type.  (Accordingly, it is
4618       not really very useful to clients.) A negative value indicates
4619       the data type is variable-length.
4620      </para>
4621     </listitem>
4622    </varlistentry>
4623
4624    <varlistentry id="libpq-PQbinaryTuples">
4625     <term><function>PQbinaryTuples</function><indexterm><primary>PQbinaryTuples</primary></indexterm></term>
4626
4627     <listitem>
4628      <para>
4629       Returns 1 if the <structname>PGresult</structname> contains binary data
4630       and 0 if it contains text data.
4631<synopsis>
4632int PQbinaryTuples(const PGresult *res);
4633</synopsis>
4634      </para>
4635
4636      <para>
4637       This function is deprecated (except for its use in connection with
4638       <command>COPY</command>), because it is possible for a single
4639       <structname>PGresult</structname> to contain text data in some columns and
4640       binary data in others.  <xref linkend="libpq-PQfformat"/> is preferred.
4641       <xref linkend="libpq-PQbinaryTuples"/> returns 1 only if all columns of the
4642       result are binary (format 1).
4643      </para>
4644     </listitem>
4645    </varlistentry>
4646
4647    <varlistentry id="libpq-PQgetvalue">
4648     <term><function>PQgetvalue</function><indexterm><primary>PQgetvalue</primary></indexterm></term>
4649
4650     <listitem>
4651      <para>
4652       Returns a single field value of one row of a
4653       <structname>PGresult</structname>.  Row and column numbers start
4654       at 0.  The caller should not free the result directly.  It will
4655       be freed when the associated <structname>PGresult</structname> handle is
4656       passed to <xref linkend="libpq-PQclear"/>.
4657<synopsis>
4658char *PQgetvalue(const PGresult *res,
4659                 int row_number,
4660                 int column_number);
4661</synopsis>
4662      </para>
4663
4664      <para>
4665       For data in text format, the value returned by
4666       <xref linkend="libpq-PQgetvalue"/> is a null-terminated character
4667       string  representation of the field value.  For data in binary
4668       format, the value is in the binary representation determined by
4669       the data type's <function>typsend</function> and <function>typreceive</function>
4670       functions.  (The value is actually followed by a zero byte in
4671       this case too, but that is not ordinarily useful, since the
4672       value is likely to contain embedded nulls.)
4673      </para>
4674
4675      <para>
4676       An empty string is returned if the field value is null.  See
4677       <xref linkend="libpq-PQgetisnull"/> to distinguish null values from
4678       empty-string values.
4679      </para>
4680
4681      <para>
4682       The pointer returned  by  <xref linkend="libpq-PQgetvalue"/> points
4683       to storage that is part of the <structname>PGresult</structname>
4684       structure.  One should not modify the data it points to, and one
4685       must explicitly copy the data into other storage if it is to be
4686       used past the lifetime of the  <structname>PGresult</structname>
4687       structure itself.
4688      </para>
4689     </listitem>
4690    </varlistentry>
4691
4692    <varlistentry id="libpq-PQgetisnull">
4693     <term><function>PQgetisnull</function><indexterm
4694     ><primary>PQgetisnull</primary></indexterm><indexterm
4695     ><primary>null value</primary><secondary sortas="libpq">in libpq</secondary></indexterm></term>
4696
4697     <listitem>
4698      <para>
4699       Tests a field for a null value.  Row and column numbers start
4700       at 0.
4701<synopsis>
4702int PQgetisnull(const PGresult *res,
4703                int row_number,
4704                int column_number);
4705</synopsis>
4706      </para>
4707
4708      <para>
4709       This function returns  1 if the field is null and 0 if it
4710       contains a non-null value.  (Note that
4711       <xref linkend="libpq-PQgetvalue"/> will return an empty string,
4712       not a null pointer, for a null field.)
4713      </para>
4714     </listitem>
4715    </varlistentry>
4716
4717    <varlistentry id="libpq-PQgetlength">
4718     <term><function>PQgetlength</function><indexterm><primary>PQgetlength</primary></indexterm></term>
4719
4720     <listitem>
4721      <para>
4722       Returns the actual length of a field value in bytes.  Row and
4723       column numbers start at 0.
4724<synopsis>
4725int PQgetlength(const PGresult *res,
4726                int row_number,
4727                int column_number);
4728</synopsis>
4729      </para>
4730
4731      <para>
4732       This is the actual data length for the particular data value,
4733       that is, the size of the object pointed to by
4734       <xref linkend="libpq-PQgetvalue"/>.  For text data format this is
4735       the same as <function>strlen()</function>.  For binary format this is
4736       essential information.  Note that one should <emphasis>not</emphasis>
4737       rely on <xref linkend="libpq-PQfsize"/> to obtain the actual data
4738       length.
4739      </para>
4740     </listitem>
4741    </varlistentry>
4742
4743    <varlistentry id="libpq-PQnparams">
4744     <term><function>PQnparams</function><indexterm><primary>PQnparams</primary></indexterm></term>
4745
4746     <listitem>
4747      <para>
4748       Returns the number of parameters of a prepared statement.
4749<synopsis>
4750int PQnparams(const PGresult *res);
4751</synopsis>
4752      </para>
4753
4754      <para>
4755       This function is only useful when inspecting the result of
4756       <xref linkend="libpq-PQdescribePrepared"/>.  For other types of results it
4757       will return zero.
4758      </para>
4759     </listitem>
4760    </varlistentry>
4761
4762    <varlistentry id="libpq-PQparamtype">
4763     <term><function>PQparamtype</function><indexterm><primary>PQparamtype</primary></indexterm></term>
4764
4765     <listitem>
4766      <para>
4767       Returns the data type of the indicated statement parameter.
4768       Parameter numbers start at 0.
4769<synopsis>
4770Oid PQparamtype(const PGresult *res, int param_number);
4771</synopsis>
4772      </para>
4773
4774      <para>
4775       This function is only useful when inspecting the result of
4776       <xref linkend="libpq-PQdescribePrepared"/>.  For other types of results it
4777       will return zero.
4778      </para>
4779     </listitem>
4780    </varlistentry>
4781
4782    <varlistentry id="libpq-PQprint">
4783     <term><function>PQprint</function><indexterm><primary>PQprint</primary></indexterm></term>
4784
4785     <listitem>
4786      <para>
4787       Prints out all the rows and,  optionally,  the column names  to
4788       the specified output stream.
4789<synopsis>
4790void PQprint(FILE *fout,      /* output stream */
4791             const PGresult *res,
4792             const PQprintOpt *po);
4793typedef struct
4794{
4795    pqbool  header;      /* print output field headings and row count */
4796    pqbool  align;       /* fill align the fields */
4797    pqbool  standard;    /* old brain dead format */
4798    pqbool  html3;       /* output HTML tables */
4799    pqbool  expanded;    /* expand tables */
4800    pqbool  pager;       /* use pager for output if needed */
4801    char    *fieldSep;   /* field separator */
4802    char    *tableOpt;   /* attributes for HTML table element */
4803    char    *caption;    /* HTML table caption */
4804    char    **fieldName; /* null-terminated array of replacement field names */
4805} PQprintOpt;
4806</synopsis>
4807      </para>
4808
4809      <para>
4810       This function was formerly used by <application>psql</application>
4811       to print query results, but this is no longer the case.  Note
4812       that it assumes all the data is in text format.
4813      </para>
4814     </listitem>
4815    </varlistentry>
4816   </variablelist>
4817  </sect2>
4818
4819  <sect2 id="libpq-exec-nonselect">
4820   <title>Retrieving Other Result Information</title>
4821
4822   <para>
4823    These functions are used to extract other information from
4824    <structname>PGresult</structname> objects.
4825   </para>
4826
4827   <variablelist>
4828    <varlistentry id="libpq-PQcmdStatus">
4829     <term><function>PQcmdStatus</function><indexterm><primary>PQcmdStatus</primary></indexterm></term>
4830
4831     <listitem>
4832      <para>
4833       Returns the command status tag from the SQL command that generated
4834       the <structname>PGresult</structname>.
4835<synopsis>
4836char *PQcmdStatus(PGresult *res);
4837</synopsis>
4838      </para>
4839
4840      <para>
4841       Commonly this is just the name of the command, but it might include
4842       additional data such as the number of rows processed. The caller
4843       should not free the result directly. It will be freed when the
4844       associated <structname>PGresult</structname> handle is passed to
4845       <xref linkend="libpq-PQclear"/>.
4846      </para>
4847     </listitem>
4848    </varlistentry>
4849
4850    <varlistentry id="libpq-PQcmdTuples">
4851     <term><function>PQcmdTuples</function><indexterm><primary>PQcmdTuples</primary></indexterm></term>
4852
4853     <listitem>
4854      <para>
4855       Returns the number of rows affected by the SQL command.
4856<synopsis>
4857char *PQcmdTuples(PGresult *res);
4858</synopsis>
4859      </para>
4860
4861      <para>
4862       This function returns a string containing the number of rows
4863       affected by the <acronym>SQL</acronym> statement that generated the
4864       <structname>PGresult</structname>. This function can only be used following
4865       the execution of a <command>SELECT</command>, <command>CREATE TABLE AS</command>,
4866       <command>INSERT</command>, <command>UPDATE</command>, <command>DELETE</command>,
4867       <command>MERGE</command>, <command>MOVE</command>, <command>FETCH</command>,
4868       or <command>COPY</command> statement, or an <command>EXECUTE</command> of a
4869       prepared query that contains an <command>INSERT</command>,
4870       <command>UPDATE</command>, <command>DELETE</command>,
4871       or <command>MERGE</command> statement.
4872       If the command that generated the <structname>PGresult</structname> was anything
4873       else, <xref linkend="libpq-PQcmdTuples"/> returns an empty string. The caller
4874       should not free the return value directly. It will be freed when
4875       the associated <structname>PGresult</structname> handle is passed to
4876       <xref linkend="libpq-PQclear"/>.
4877      </para>
4878     </listitem>
4879    </varlistentry>
4880
4881    <varlistentry id="libpq-PQoidValue">
4882     <term><function>PQoidValue</function><indexterm><primary>PQoidValue</primary></indexterm></term>
4883
4884     <listitem>
4885      <para>
4886       Returns the OID<indexterm><primary>OID</primary><secondary>in libpq</secondary></indexterm>
4887       of the inserted row, if the <acronym>SQL</acronym> command was an
4888       <command>INSERT</command> that inserted exactly one row into a table that
4889       has OIDs, or a <command>EXECUTE</command> of a prepared query containing
4890       a suitable <command>INSERT</command> statement.  Otherwise, this function
4891       returns <literal>InvalidOid</literal>. This function will also
4892       return <literal>InvalidOid</literal> if the table affected by the
4893       <command>INSERT</command> statement does not contain OIDs.
4894<synopsis>
4895Oid PQoidValue(const PGresult *res);
4896</synopsis>
4897      </para>
4898     </listitem>
4899    </varlistentry>
4900
4901    <varlistentry id="libpq-PQoidStatus">
4902     <term><function>PQoidStatus</function><indexterm><primary>PQoidStatus</primary></indexterm></term>
4903
4904     <listitem>
4905      <para>
4906       This function is deprecated in favor of
4907       <xref linkend="libpq-PQoidValue"/> and is not thread-safe.
4908       It returns a string with the OID of the inserted row, while
4909       <xref linkend="libpq-PQoidValue"/> returns the OID value.
4910<synopsis>
4911char *PQoidStatus(const PGresult *res);
4912</synopsis>
4913      </para>
4914
4915     </listitem>
4916    </varlistentry>
4917   </variablelist>
4918
4919  </sect2>
4920
4921  <sect2 id="libpq-exec-escape-string">
4922   <title>Escaping Strings for Inclusion in SQL Commands</title>
4923
4924   <indexterm zone="libpq-exec-escape-string">
4925    <primary>escaping strings</primary>
4926    <secondary>in libpq</secondary>
4927   </indexterm>
4928
4929   <variablelist>
4930    <varlistentry id="libpq-PQescapeLiteral">
4931     <term><function>PQescapeLiteral</function><indexterm><primary>PQescapeLiteral</primary></indexterm></term>
4932
4933     <listitem>
4934     <para>
4935<synopsis>
4936char *PQescapeLiteral(PGconn *conn, const char *str, size_t length);
4937</synopsis>
4938     </para>
4939
4940     <para>
4941      <xref linkend="libpq-PQescapeLiteral"/> escapes a string for
4942      use within an SQL command.  This is useful when inserting data
4943      values as literal constants in SQL commands.  Certain characters
4944      (such as quotes and backslashes) must be escaped to prevent them
4945      from being interpreted specially by the SQL parser.
4946      <xref linkend="libpq-PQescapeLiteral"/> performs this operation.
4947     </para>
4948
4949     <para>
4950      <xref linkend="libpq-PQescapeLiteral"/> returns an escaped version of the
4951      <parameter>str</parameter> parameter in memory allocated with
4952      <function>malloc()</function>.  This memory should be freed using
4953      <function>PQfreemem()</function> when the result is no longer needed.
4954      A terminating zero byte is not required, and should not be
4955      counted in <parameter>length</parameter>.  (If a terminating zero byte is found
4956      before <parameter>length</parameter> bytes are processed,
4957      <xref linkend="libpq-PQescapeLiteral"/> stops at the zero; the behavior is
4958      thus rather like <function>strncpy</function>.) The
4959      return string has all special characters replaced so that they can
4960      be properly processed by the <productname>PostgreSQL</productname>
4961      string literal parser.  A terminating zero byte is also added.  The
4962      single quotes that must surround <productname>PostgreSQL</productname>
4963      string literals are included in the result string.
4964     </para>
4965
4966     <para>
4967      On error, <xref linkend="libpq-PQescapeLiteral"/> returns <symbol>NULL</symbol> and a suitable
4968      message is stored in the <parameter>conn</parameter> object.
4969     </para>
4970
4971     <tip>
4972      <para>
4973       It is especially important to do proper escaping when handling
4974       strings that were received from an untrustworthy source.
4975       Otherwise there is a security risk: you are vulnerable to
4976       <quote>SQL injection</quote> attacks wherein unwanted SQL commands are
4977       fed to your database.
4978      </para>
4979     </tip>
4980
4981     <para>
4982      Note that it is neither necessary nor correct to do escaping when a data
4983      value is passed as a separate parameter in <xref linkend="libpq-PQexecParams"/> or
4984      its sibling routines.
4985     </para>
4986     </listitem>
4987    </varlistentry>
4988
4989    <varlistentry id="libpq-PQescapeIdentifier">
4990     <term><function>PQescapeIdentifier</function><indexterm><primary>PQescapeIdentifier</primary></indexterm></term>
4991
4992     <listitem>
4993     <para>
4994<synopsis>
4995char *PQescapeIdentifier(PGconn *conn, const char *str, size_t length);
4996</synopsis>
4997     </para>
4998
4999     <para>
5000      <xref linkend="libpq-PQescapeIdentifier"/> escapes a string for
5001      use as an SQL identifier, such as a table, column, or function name.
5002      This is useful when a user-supplied identifier might contain
5003      special characters that would otherwise not be interpreted as part
5004      of the identifier by the SQL parser, or when the identifier might
5005      contain upper case characters whose case should be preserved.
5006     </para>
5007
5008     <para>
5009      <xref linkend="libpq-PQescapeIdentifier"/> returns a version of the
5010      <parameter>str</parameter> parameter escaped as an SQL identifier
5011      in memory allocated with <function>malloc()</function>.  This memory must be
5012      freed using <function>PQfreemem()</function> when the result is no longer
5013      needed.  A terminating zero byte is not required, and should not be
5014      counted in <parameter>length</parameter>.  (If a terminating zero byte is found
5015      before <parameter>length</parameter> bytes are processed,
5016      <xref linkend="libpq-PQescapeIdentifier"/> stops at the zero; the behavior is
5017      thus rather like <function>strncpy</function>.) The
5018      return string has all special characters replaced so that it
5019      will be properly processed as an SQL identifier.  A terminating zero byte
5020      is also added.  The return string will also be surrounded by double
5021      quotes.
5022     </para>
5023
5024     <para>
5025      On error, <xref linkend="libpq-PQescapeIdentifier"/> returns <symbol>NULL</symbol> and a suitable
5026      message is stored in the <parameter>conn</parameter> object.
5027     </para>
5028
5029     <tip>
5030      <para>
5031       As with string literals, to prevent SQL injection attacks,
5032       SQL identifiers must be escaped when they are received from an
5033       untrustworthy source.
5034      </para>
5035     </tip>
5036     </listitem>
5037    </varlistentry>
5038
5039    <varlistentry id="libpq-PQescapeStringConn">
5040     <term><function>PQescapeStringConn</function><indexterm><primary>PQescapeStringConn</primary></indexterm></term>
5041
5042     <listitem>
5043     <para>
5044<synopsis>
5045size_t PQescapeStringConn(PGconn *conn,
5046                          char *to, const char *from, size_t length,
5047                          int *error);
5048</synopsis>
5049     </para>
5050
5051     <para>
5052      <xref linkend="libpq-PQescapeStringConn"/> escapes string literals, much like
5053      <xref linkend="libpq-PQescapeLiteral"/>.  Unlike <xref linkend="libpq-PQescapeLiteral"/>,
5054      the caller is responsible for providing an appropriately sized buffer.
5055      Furthermore, <xref linkend="libpq-PQescapeStringConn"/> does not generate the
5056      single quotes that must surround <productname>PostgreSQL</productname> string
5057      literals; they should be provided in the SQL command that the
5058      result is inserted into.  The parameter <parameter>from</parameter> points to
5059      the first character of the string that is to be escaped, and the
5060      <parameter>length</parameter> parameter gives the number of bytes in this
5061      string.  A terminating zero byte is not required, and should not be
5062      counted in <parameter>length</parameter>.  (If a terminating zero byte is found
5063      before <parameter>length</parameter> bytes are processed,
5064      <xref linkend="libpq-PQescapeStringConn"/> stops at the zero; the behavior is
5065      thus rather like <function>strncpy</function>.) <parameter>to</parameter> shall point
5066      to a buffer that is able to hold at least one more byte than twice
5067      the value of <parameter>length</parameter>, otherwise the behavior is undefined.
5068      Behavior is likewise undefined if the <parameter>to</parameter> and
5069      <parameter>from</parameter> strings overlap.
5070     </para>
5071
5072     <para>
5073      If the <parameter>error</parameter> parameter is not <symbol>NULL</symbol>, then
5074      <literal>*error</literal> is set to zero on success, nonzero on error.
5075      Presently the only possible error conditions involve invalid multibyte
5076      encoding in the source string.  The output string is still generated
5077      on error, but it can be expected that the server will reject it as
5078      malformed.  On error, a suitable message is stored in the
5079      <parameter>conn</parameter> object, whether or not <parameter>error</parameter> is <symbol>NULL</symbol>.
5080     </para>
5081
5082     <para>
5083      <xref linkend="libpq-PQescapeStringConn"/> returns the number of bytes written
5084      to <parameter>to</parameter>, not including the terminating zero byte.
5085     </para>
5086     </listitem>
5087    </varlistentry>
5088
5089    <varlistentry id="libpq-PQescapeString">
5090     <term><function>PQescapeString</function><indexterm><primary>PQescapeString</primary></indexterm></term>
5091
5092     <listitem>
5093     <para>
5094       <xref linkend="libpq-PQescapeString"/> is an older, deprecated version of
5095       <xref linkend="libpq-PQescapeStringConn"/>.
5096<synopsis>
5097size_t PQescapeString (char *to, const char *from, size_t length);
5098</synopsis>
5099     </para>
5100
5101     <para>
5102      The only difference from <xref linkend="libpq-PQescapeStringConn"/> is that
5103      <xref linkend="libpq-PQescapeString"/> does not take <structname>PGconn</structname>
5104      or <parameter>error</parameter> parameters.
5105      Because of this, it cannot adjust its behavior depending on the
5106      connection properties (such as character encoding) and therefore
5107      <emphasis>it might give the wrong results</emphasis>.  Also, it has no way
5108      to report error conditions.
5109     </para>
5110
5111     <para>
5112      <xref linkend="libpq-PQescapeString"/> can be used safely in
5113      client programs that work with only one <productname>PostgreSQL</productname>
5114      connection at a time (in this case it can find out what it needs to
5115      know <quote>behind the scenes</quote>).  In other contexts it is a security
5116      hazard and should be avoided in favor of
5117      <xref linkend="libpq-PQescapeStringConn"/>.
5118     </para>
5119     </listitem>
5120    </varlistentry>
5121
5122    <varlistentry id="libpq-PQescapeByteaConn">
5123     <term><function>PQescapeByteaConn</function><indexterm><primary>PQescapeByteaConn</primary></indexterm></term>
5124
5125     <listitem>
5126     <para>
5127       Escapes binary data for use within an SQL command with the type
5128       <type>bytea</type>.  As with <xref linkend="libpq-PQescapeStringConn"/>,
5129       this is only used when inserting data directly into an SQL command string.
5130<synopsis>
5131unsigned char *PQescapeByteaConn(PGconn *conn,
5132                                 const unsigned char *from,
5133                                 size_t from_length,
5134                                 size_t *to_length);
5135</synopsis>
5136      </para>
5137
5138      <para>
5139       Certain byte values must be escaped when used as part of a
5140       <type>bytea</type> literal in an <acronym>SQL</acronym> statement.
5141       <xref linkend="libpq-PQescapeByteaConn"/> escapes bytes using
5142       either hex encoding or backslash escaping.  See <xref
5143       linkend="datatype-binary"/> for more information.
5144      </para>
5145
5146      <para>
5147       The <parameter>from</parameter> parameter points to the first
5148       byte of the string that is to be escaped, and the
5149       <parameter>from_length</parameter> parameter gives the number of
5150       bytes in this binary string.  (A terminating zero byte is
5151       neither necessary nor counted.)  The <parameter>to_length</parameter>
5152       parameter points to a variable that will hold the resultant
5153       escaped string length. This result string length includes the terminating
5154       zero byte of the result.
5155      </para>
5156
5157      <para>
5158       <xref linkend="libpq-PQescapeByteaConn"/> returns an escaped version of the
5159       <parameter>from</parameter> parameter binary string in memory
5160       allocated with <function>malloc()</function>.  This memory should be freed using
5161       <function>PQfreemem()</function> when the result is no longer needed.  The
5162       return string has all special characters replaced so that they can
5163       be properly processed by the <productname>PostgreSQL</productname>
5164       string literal parser, and the <type>bytea</type> input function. A
5165       terminating zero byte is also added.  The single quotes that must
5166       surround <productname>PostgreSQL</productname> string literals are
5167       not part of the result string.
5168      </para>
5169
5170      <para>
5171       On error, a null pointer is returned, and a suitable error message
5172       is stored in the <parameter>conn</parameter> object.  Currently, the only
5173       possible error is insufficient memory for the result string.
5174      </para>
5175     </listitem>
5176    </varlistentry>
5177
5178    <varlistentry id="libpq-PQescapeBytea">
5179     <term><function>PQescapeBytea</function><indexterm><primary>PQescapeBytea</primary></indexterm></term>
5180
5181     <listitem>
5182      <para>
5183       <xref linkend="libpq-PQescapeBytea"/> is an older, deprecated version of
5184       <xref linkend="libpq-PQescapeByteaConn"/>.
5185<synopsis>
5186unsigned char *PQescapeBytea(const unsigned char *from,
5187                             size_t from_length,
5188                             size_t *to_length);
5189</synopsis>
5190      </para>
5191
5192      <para>
5193       The only difference from <xref linkend="libpq-PQescapeByteaConn"/> is that
5194       <xref linkend="libpq-PQescapeBytea"/> does not take a <structname>PGconn</structname>
5195       parameter.  Because of this, <xref linkend="libpq-PQescapeBytea"/> can
5196       only be used safely in client programs that use a single
5197       <productname>PostgreSQL</productname> connection at a time (in this case
5198       it can find out what it needs to know <quote>behind the
5199       scenes</quote>).  It <emphasis>might give the wrong results</emphasis> if
5200       used in programs that use multiple database connections (use
5201       <xref linkend="libpq-PQescapeByteaConn"/> in such cases).
5202      </para>
5203     </listitem>
5204    </varlistentry>
5205
5206    <varlistentry id="libpq-PQunescapeBytea">
5207     <term><function>PQunescapeBytea</function><indexterm><primary>PQunescapeBytea</primary></indexterm></term>
5208
5209     <listitem>
5210      <para>
5211       Converts a string representation of binary data into binary data
5212       &mdash; the reverse of <xref linkend="libpq-PQescapeBytea"/>.  This
5213       is needed when retrieving <type>bytea</type> data in text format,
5214       but not when retrieving it in binary format.
5215
5216<synopsis>
5217unsigned char *PQunescapeBytea(const unsigned char *from, size_t *to_length);
5218</synopsis>
5219      </para>
5220
5221      <para>
5222       The <parameter>from</parameter> parameter points to a string
5223       such as might be returned by <xref linkend="libpq-PQgetvalue"/> when applied
5224       to a <type>bytea</type> column. <xref linkend="libpq-PQunescapeBytea"/>
5225       converts this string representation into its binary representation.
5226       It returns a pointer to a buffer allocated with
5227       <function>malloc()</function>, or <symbol>NULL</symbol> on error, and puts the size of
5228       the buffer in <parameter>to_length</parameter>. The result must be
5229       freed using <xref linkend="libpq-PQfreemem"/> when it is no longer needed.
5230      </para>
5231
5232      <para>
5233       This conversion is not exactly the inverse of
5234       <xref linkend="libpq-PQescapeBytea"/>, because the string is not expected
5235       to be <quote>escaped</quote> when received from <xref linkend="libpq-PQgetvalue"/>.
5236       In particular this means there is no need for string quoting considerations,
5237       and so no need for a <structname>PGconn</structname> parameter.
5238      </para>
5239     </listitem>
5240    </varlistentry>
5241   </variablelist>
5242
5243  </sect2>
5244
5245 </sect1>
5246
5247 <sect1 id="libpq-async">
5248  <title>Asynchronous Command Processing</title>
5249
5250  <indexterm zone="libpq-async">
5251   <primary>nonblocking connection</primary>
5252  </indexterm>
5253
5254  <para>
5255   The <xref linkend="libpq-PQexec"/> function is adequate for submitting
5256   commands in normal, synchronous applications.  It has a few
5257   deficiencies, however, that can be of importance to some users:
5258
5259   <itemizedlist>
5260    <listitem>
5261     <para>
5262      <xref linkend="libpq-PQexec"/> waits for the command to be completed.
5263      The application might have other work to do (such as maintaining a
5264      user interface), in which case it won't want to block waiting for
5265      the response.
5266     </para>
5267    </listitem>
5268
5269    <listitem>
5270     <para>
5271      Since the execution of the client application is suspended while it
5272      waits for the result, it is hard for the application to decide that
5273      it would like to try to cancel the ongoing command.  (It can be done
5274      from a signal handler, but not otherwise.)
5275     </para>
5276    </listitem>
5277
5278    <listitem>
5279     <para>
5280      <xref linkend="libpq-PQexec"/> can return only one
5281      <structname>PGresult</structname> structure.  If the submitted command
5282      string contains multiple <acronym>SQL</acronym> commands, all but
5283      the last <structname>PGresult</structname> are discarded by
5284      <xref linkend="libpq-PQexec"/>.
5285     </para>
5286    </listitem>
5287
5288    <listitem>
5289     <para>
5290      <xref linkend="libpq-PQexec"/> always collects the command's entire result,
5291      buffering it in a single <structname>PGresult</structname>.  While
5292      this simplifies error-handling logic for the application, it can be
5293      impractical for results containing many rows.
5294     </para>
5295    </listitem>
5296   </itemizedlist>
5297  </para>
5298
5299  <para>
5300   Applications that do not like these limitations can instead use the
5301   underlying functions that <xref linkend="libpq-PQexec"/> is built from:
5302   <xref linkend="libpq-PQsendQuery"/> and <xref linkend="libpq-PQgetResult"/>.
5303   There are also
5304   <xref linkend="libpq-PQsendQueryParams"/>,
5305   <xref linkend="libpq-PQsendPrepare"/>,
5306   <xref linkend="libpq-PQsendQueryPrepared"/>,
5307   <xref linkend="libpq-PQsendDescribePrepared"/>,
5308   <xref linkend="libpq-PQsendDescribePortal"/>,
5309   <xref linkend="libpq-PQsendClosePrepared"/>, and
5310   <xref linkend="libpq-PQsendClosePortal"/>,
5311   which can be used with <xref linkend="libpq-PQgetResult"/> to duplicate
5312   the functionality of
5313   <xref linkend="libpq-PQexecParams"/>,
5314   <xref linkend="libpq-PQprepare"/>,
5315   <xref linkend="libpq-PQexecPrepared"/>,
5316   <xref linkend="libpq-PQdescribePrepared"/>,
5317   <xref linkend="libpq-PQdescribePortal"/>,
5318   <xref linkend="libpq-PQclosePrepared"/>, and
5319   <xref linkend="libpq-PQclosePortal"/>
5320   respectively.
5321
5322   <variablelist>
5323    <varlistentry id="libpq-PQsendQuery">
5324     <term><function>PQsendQuery</function><indexterm><primary>PQsendQuery</primary></indexterm></term>
5325
5326     <listitem>
5327      <para>
5328       Submits a command to the server without waiting for the result(s).
5329       1 is returned if the command was successfully dispatched and 0 if
5330       not (in which case, use <xref linkend="libpq-PQerrorMessage"/> to get more
5331       information about the failure).
5332<synopsis>
5333int PQsendQuery(PGconn *conn, const char *command);
5334</synopsis>
5335
5336       After successfully calling <xref linkend="libpq-PQsendQuery"/>, call
5337       <xref linkend="libpq-PQgetResult"/> one or more times to obtain the
5338       results.  <xref linkend="libpq-PQsendQuery"/> cannot be called again
5339       (on the same connection) until <xref linkend="libpq-PQgetResult"/>
5340       has returned a null pointer, indicating that the command is done.
5341      </para>
5342
5343      <para>
5344       In pipeline mode, this function is disallowed.
5345      </para>
5346     </listitem>
5347    </varlistentry>
5348
5349    <varlistentry id="libpq-PQsendQueryParams">
5350     <term><function>PQsendQueryParams</function><indexterm><primary>PQsendQueryParams</primary></indexterm></term>
5351
5352     <listitem>
5353      <para>
5354       Submits a command and separate parameters to the server without
5355       waiting for the result(s).
5356<synopsis>
5357int PQsendQueryParams(PGconn *conn,
5358                      const char *command,
5359                      int nParams,
5360                      const Oid *paramTypes,
5361                      const char * const *paramValues,
5362                      const int *paramLengths,
5363                      const int *paramFormats,
5364                      int resultFormat);
5365</synopsis>
5366
5367       This is equivalent to <xref linkend="libpq-PQsendQuery"/> except that
5368       query parameters can be specified separately from the query string.
5369       The function's parameters are handled identically to
5370       <xref linkend="libpq-PQexecParams"/>.  Like
5371       <xref linkend="libpq-PQexecParams"/>, it allows only one command in the
5372       query string.
5373      </para>
5374     </listitem>
5375    </varlistentry>
5376
5377    <varlistentry id="libpq-PQsendPrepare">
5378     <term><function>PQsendPrepare</function><indexterm><primary>PQsendPrepare</primary></indexterm></term>
5379
5380     <listitem>
5381      <para>
5382       Sends a request to create a prepared statement with the given
5383       parameters, without waiting for completion.
5384<synopsis>
5385int PQsendPrepare(PGconn *conn,
5386                  const char *stmtName,
5387                  const char *query,
5388                  int nParams,
5389                  const Oid *paramTypes);
5390</synopsis>
5391
5392       This is an asynchronous version of <xref linkend="libpq-PQprepare"/>: it
5393       returns 1 if it was able to dispatch the request, and 0 if not.
5394       After a successful call, call <xref linkend="libpq-PQgetResult"/> to
5395       determine whether the server successfully created the prepared
5396       statement.  The function's parameters are handled identically to
5397       <xref linkend="libpq-PQprepare"/>.
5398      </para>
5399     </listitem>
5400    </varlistentry>
5401
5402    <varlistentry id="libpq-PQsendQueryPrepared">
5403     <term><function>PQsendQueryPrepared</function><indexterm><primary>PQsendQueryPrepared</primary></indexterm></term>
5404
5405     <listitem>
5406      <para>
5407       Sends a request to execute a prepared statement with given
5408       parameters, without waiting for the result(s).
5409<synopsis>
5410int PQsendQueryPrepared(PGconn *conn,
5411                        const char *stmtName,
5412                        int nParams,
5413                        const char * const *paramValues,
5414                        const int *paramLengths,
5415                        const int *paramFormats,
5416                        int resultFormat);
5417</synopsis>
5418
5419       This is similar to <xref linkend="libpq-PQsendQueryParams"/>, but
