5420       the command to be executed is specified by naming a
5421       previously-prepared statement, instead of giving a query string.
5422       The function's parameters are handled identically to
5423       <xref linkend="libpq-PQexecPrepared"/>.
5424      </para>
5425     </listitem>
5426    </varlistentry>
5427
5428    <varlistentry id="libpq-PQsendDescribePrepared">
5429     <term><function>PQsendDescribePrepared</function><indexterm><primary>PQsendDescribePrepared</primary></indexterm></term>
5430
5431     <listitem>
5432      <para>
5433       Submits a request to obtain information about the specified
5434       prepared statement, without waiting for completion.
5435<synopsis>
5436int PQsendDescribePrepared(PGconn *conn, const char *stmtName);
5437</synopsis>
5438
5439       This is an asynchronous version of <xref linkend="libpq-PQdescribePrepared"/>:
5440       it returns 1 if it was able to dispatch the request, and 0 if not.
5441       After a successful call, call <xref linkend="libpq-PQgetResult"/> to
5442       obtain the results.  The function's parameters are handled
5443       identically to <xref linkend="libpq-PQdescribePrepared"/>.
5444      </para>
5445     </listitem>
5446    </varlistentry>
5447
5448    <varlistentry id="libpq-PQsendDescribePortal">
5449     <term><function>PQsendDescribePortal</function><indexterm><primary>PQsendDescribePortal</primary></indexterm></term>
5450
5451     <listitem>
5452      <para>
5453       Submits a request to obtain information about the specified
5454       portal, without waiting for completion.
5455<synopsis>
5456int PQsendDescribePortal(PGconn *conn, const char *portalName);
5457</synopsis>
5458
5459       This is an asynchronous version of <xref linkend="libpq-PQdescribePortal"/>:
5460       it returns 1 if it was able to dispatch the request, and 0 if not.
5461       After a successful call, call <xref linkend="libpq-PQgetResult"/> to
5462       obtain the results.  The function's parameters are handled
5463       identically to <xref linkend="libpq-PQdescribePortal"/>.
5464      </para>
5465     </listitem>
5466    </varlistentry>
5467
5468    <varlistentry id="libpq-PQsendClosePrepared">
5469     <term><function>PQsendClosePrepared</function><indexterm><primary>PQsendClosePrepared</primary></indexterm></term>
5470
5471     <listitem>
5472      <para>
5473       Submits a request to close the specified prepared statement, without
5474       waiting for completion.
5475<synopsis>
5476int PQsendClosePrepared(PGconn *conn, const char *stmtName);
5477</synopsis>
5478
5479       This is an asynchronous version of <xref linkend="libpq-PQclosePrepared"/>:
5480       it returns 1 if it was able to dispatch the request, and 0 if not.
5481       After a successful call, call <xref linkend="libpq-PQgetResult"/> to
5482       obtain the results.  The function's parameters are handled
5483       identically to <xref linkend="libpq-PQclosePrepared"/>.
5484      </para>
5485     </listitem>
5486    </varlistentry>
5487
5488    <varlistentry id="libpq-PQsendClosePortal">
5489     <term><function>PQsendClosePortal</function><indexterm><primary>PQsendClosePortal</primary></indexterm></term>
5490
5491     <listitem>
5492      <para>
5493       Submits a request to close specified portal, without waiting for
5494       completion.
5495<synopsis>
5496int PQsendClosePortal(PGconn *conn, const char *portalName);
5497</synopsis>
5498
5499       This is an asynchronous version of <xref linkend="libpq-PQclosePortal"/>:
5500       it returns 1 if it was able to dispatch the request, and 0 if not.
5501       After a successful call, call <xref linkend="libpq-PQgetResult"/> to
5502       obtain the results.  The function's parameters are handled
5503       identically to <xref linkend="libpq-PQclosePortal"/>.
5504      </para>
5505     </listitem>
5506    </varlistentry>
5507
5508    <varlistentry id="libpq-PQgetResult">
5509     <term><function>PQgetResult</function><indexterm><primary>PQgetResult</primary></indexterm></term>
5510
5511     <listitem>
5512      <para>
5513       Waits for the next result from a prior
5514       <xref linkend="libpq-PQsendQuery"/>,
5515       <xref linkend="libpq-PQsendQueryParams"/>,
5516       <xref linkend="libpq-PQsendPrepare"/>,
5517       <xref linkend="libpq-PQsendQueryPrepared"/>,
5518       <xref linkend="libpq-PQsendDescribePrepared"/>,
5519       <xref linkend="libpq-PQsendDescribePortal"/>,
5520       <xref linkend="libpq-PQsendClosePrepared"/>,
5521       <xref linkend="libpq-PQsendClosePortal"/>,
5522       <xref linkend="libpq-PQsendPipelineSync"/>, or
5523       <xref linkend="libpq-PQpipelineSync"/>
5524       call, and returns it.
5525       A null pointer is returned when the command is complete and there
5526       will be no more results.
5527<synopsis>
5528PGresult *PQgetResult(PGconn *conn);
5529</synopsis>
5530      </para>
5531
5532      <para>
5533       <xref linkend="libpq-PQgetResult"/> must be called repeatedly until
5534       it returns a null pointer, indicating that the command is done.
5535       (If called when no command is active,
5536       <xref linkend="libpq-PQgetResult"/> will just return a null pointer
5537       at once.) Each non-null result from
5538       <xref linkend="libpq-PQgetResult"/> should be processed using the
5539       same <structname>PGresult</structname> accessor functions previously
5540       described.  Don't forget to free each result object with
5541       <xref linkend="libpq-PQclear"/> when done with it.  Note that
5542       <xref linkend="libpq-PQgetResult"/> will block only if a command is
5543       active and the necessary response data has not yet been read by
5544       <xref linkend="libpq-PQconsumeInput"/>.
5545      </para>
5546
5547      <para>
5548       In pipeline mode, <function>PQgetResult</function> will return normally
5549       unless an error occurs; for any subsequent query sent after the one
5550       that caused the error until (and excluding) the next synchronization point,
5551       a special result of type <literal>PGRES_PIPELINE_ABORTED</literal> will
5552       be returned, and a null pointer will be returned after it.
5553       When the pipeline synchronization point is reached, a result of type
5554       <literal>PGRES_PIPELINE_SYNC</literal> will be returned.
5555       The result of the next query after the synchronization point follows
5556       immediately (that is, no null pointer is returned after
5557       the synchronization point).
5558      </para>
5559
5560      <note>
5561       <para>
5562        Even when <xref linkend="libpq-PQresultStatus"/> indicates a fatal
5563        error, <xref linkend="libpq-PQgetResult"/> should be called until it
5564        returns a null pointer, to allow <application>libpq</application> to
5565        process the error information completely.
5566       </para>
5567      </note>
5568     </listitem>
5569    </varlistentry>
5570   </variablelist>
5571  </para>
5572
5573  <para>
5574   Using <xref linkend="libpq-PQsendQuery"/> and
5575   <xref linkend="libpq-PQgetResult"/> solves one of
5576   <xref linkend="libpq-PQexec"/>'s problems:  If a command string contains
5577   multiple <acronym>SQL</acronym> commands, the results of those commands
5578   can be obtained individually.  (This allows a simple form of overlapped
5579   processing, by the way: the client can be handling the results of one
5580   command while the server is still working on later queries in the same
5581   command string.)
5582  </para>
5583
5584  <para>
5585   Another frequently-desired feature that can be obtained with
5586   <xref linkend="libpq-PQsendQuery"/> and <xref linkend="libpq-PQgetResult"/>
5587   is retrieving large query results a limited number of rows at a time.
5588   This is discussed
5589   in <xref linkend="libpq-single-row-mode"/>.
5590  </para>
5591
5592  <para>
5593   By itself, calling <xref linkend="libpq-PQgetResult"/>
5594   will still cause the client to block until the server completes the
5595   next <acronym>SQL</acronym> command.  This can be avoided by proper
5596   use of two more functions:
5597
5598   <variablelist>
5599    <varlistentry id="libpq-PQconsumeInput">
5600     <term><function>PQconsumeInput</function><indexterm><primary>PQconsumeInput</primary></indexterm>
5601     </term>
5602
5603     <listitem>
5604      <para>
5605       If input is available from the server, consume it.
5606<synopsis>
5607int PQconsumeInput(PGconn *conn);
5608</synopsis>
5609      </para>
5610
5611      <para>
5612       <xref linkend="libpq-PQconsumeInput"/> normally returns 1 indicating
5613       <quote>no error</quote>, but returns 0 if there was some kind of
5614       trouble (in which case <xref linkend="libpq-PQerrorMessage"/> can be
5615       consulted).  Note that the result does not say whether any input
5616       data was actually collected. After calling
5617       <xref linkend="libpq-PQconsumeInput"/>, the application can check
5618       <xref linkend="libpq-PQisBusy"/> and/or
5619       <function>PQnotifies</function> to see if their state has changed.
5620      </para>
5621
5622      <para>
5623       <xref linkend="libpq-PQconsumeInput"/> can be called even if the
5624       application is not prepared to deal with a result or notification
5625       just yet.  The function will read available data and save it in
5626       a buffer, thereby causing a <function>select()</function>
5627       read-ready indication to go away.  The application can thus use
5628       <xref linkend="libpq-PQconsumeInput"/> to clear the
5629       <function>select()</function> condition immediately, and then
5630       examine the results at leisure.
5631      </para>
5632     </listitem>
5633    </varlistentry>
5634
5635    <varlistentry id="libpq-PQisBusy">
5636     <term><function>PQisBusy</function><indexterm><primary>PQisBusy</primary></indexterm></term>
5637
5638     <listitem>
5639      <para>
5640       Returns 1 if a command is busy, that is,
5641       <xref linkend="libpq-PQgetResult"/> would block waiting for input.
5642       A 0 return indicates that <xref linkend="libpq-PQgetResult"/> can be
5643       called with assurance of not blocking.
5644<synopsis>
5645int PQisBusy(PGconn *conn);
5646</synopsis>
5647      </para>
5648
5649      <para>
5650       <xref linkend="libpq-PQisBusy"/> will not itself attempt to read data
5651       from the server; therefore <xref linkend="libpq-PQconsumeInput"/>
5652       must be invoked first, or the busy state will never end.
5653      </para>
5654     </listitem>
5655    </varlistentry>
5656   </variablelist>
5657  </para>
5658
5659  <para>
5660   A typical application using these functions will have a main loop that
5661   uses <function>select()</function> or <function>poll()</function> to wait for
5662   all the conditions that it must respond to.  One of the conditions
5663   will be input available from the server, which in terms of
5664   <function>select()</function> means readable data on the file
5665   descriptor identified by <xref linkend="libpq-PQsocket"/>.  When the main
5666   loop detects input ready, it should call
5667   <xref linkend="libpq-PQconsumeInput"/> to read the input.  It can then
5668   call <xref linkend="libpq-PQisBusy"/>, followed by
5669   <xref linkend="libpq-PQgetResult"/> if <xref linkend="libpq-PQisBusy"/>
5670   returns false (0).  It can also call <function>PQnotifies</function>
5671   to detect <command>NOTIFY</command> messages (see <xref
5672   linkend="libpq-notify"/>).
5673  </para>
5674
5675  <para>
5676   A client that uses
5677   <xref linkend="libpq-PQsendQuery"/>/<xref linkend="libpq-PQgetResult"/>
5678   can also attempt to cancel a command that is still being processed
5679   by the server; see <xref linkend="libpq-cancel"/>.  But regardless of
5680   the return value of <xref linkend="libpq-PQcancelBlocking"/>, the application
5681   must continue with the normal result-reading sequence using
5682   <xref linkend="libpq-PQgetResult"/>.  A successful cancellation will
5683   simply cause the command to terminate sooner than it would have
5684   otherwise.
5685  </para>
5686
5687  <para>
5688   By using the functions described above, it is possible to avoid
5689   blocking while waiting for input from the database server.  However,
5690   it is still possible that the application will block waiting to send
5691   output to the server.  This is relatively uncommon but can happen if
5692   very long SQL commands or data values are sent.  (It is much more
5693   probable if the application sends data via <command>COPY IN</command>,
5694   however.)  To prevent this possibility and achieve completely
5695   nonblocking database operation, the following additional functions
5696   can be used.
5697
5698   <variablelist>
5699    <varlistentry id="libpq-PQsetnonblocking">
5700     <term><function>PQsetnonblocking</function><indexterm><primary>PQsetnonblocking</primary></indexterm></term>
5701
5702     <listitem>
5703      <para>
5704       Sets the nonblocking status of the connection.
5705<synopsis>
5706int PQsetnonblocking(PGconn *conn, int arg);
5707</synopsis>
5708      </para>
5709
5710      <para>
5711       Sets the state of the connection to nonblocking if
5712       <parameter>arg</parameter> is 1, or blocking if
5713       <parameter>arg</parameter> is 0.  Returns 0 if OK, -1 if error.
5714      </para>
5715
5716      <para>
5717       In the nonblocking state, successful calls to
5718       <xref linkend="libpq-PQsendQuery"/>, <xref linkend="libpq-PQputline"/>,
5719       <xref linkend="libpq-PQputnbytes"/>, <xref linkend="libpq-PQputCopyData"/>,
5720       and <xref linkend="libpq-PQendcopy"/> will not block;  their changes
5721       are stored in the local output buffer until they are flushed.
5722       Unsuccessful calls will return an error and must be retried.
5723      </para>
5724
5725      <para>
5726       Note that <xref linkend="libpq-PQexec"/> does not honor nonblocking
5727       mode; if it is called, it will act in blocking fashion anyway.
5728      </para>
5729     </listitem>
5730    </varlistentry>
5731
5732    <varlistentry id="libpq-PQisnonblocking">
5733     <term><function>PQisnonblocking</function><indexterm><primary>PQisnonblocking</primary></indexterm></term>
5734
5735     <listitem>
5736      <para>
5737       Returns the blocking status of the database connection.
5738<synopsis>
5739int PQisnonblocking(const PGconn *conn);
5740</synopsis>
5741      </para>
5742
5743      <para>
5744       Returns 1 if the connection is set to nonblocking mode and 0 if
5745       blocking.
5746      </para>
5747     </listitem>
5748    </varlistentry>
5749
5750    <varlistentry id="libpq-PQflush">
5751     <term><function>PQflush</function><indexterm><primary>PQflush</primary></indexterm></term>
5752
5753      <listitem>
5754       <para>
5755       Attempts to flush any queued output data to the server.  Returns
5756       0 if successful (or if the send queue is empty), -1 if it failed
5757       for some reason, or 1 if it was unable to send all the data in
5758       the send queue yet (this case can only occur if the connection
5759       is nonblocking).
5760<synopsis>
5761int PQflush(PGconn *conn);
5762</synopsis>
5763      </para>
5764     </listitem>
5765    </varlistentry>
5766   </variablelist>
5767  </para>
5768
5769  <para>
5770   After sending any command or data on a nonblocking connection, call
5771   <xref linkend="libpq-PQflush"/>.  If it returns 1, wait for the socket
5772   to become read- or write-ready.  If it becomes write-ready, call
5773   <xref linkend="libpq-PQflush"/> again.  If it becomes read-ready, call
5774   <xref linkend="libpq-PQconsumeInput"/>, then call
5775   <xref linkend="libpq-PQflush"/> again.  Repeat until
5776   <xref linkend="libpq-PQflush"/> returns 0.  (It is necessary to check for
5777   read-ready and drain the input with <xref linkend="libpq-PQconsumeInput"/>,
5778   because the server can block trying to send us data, e.g., NOTICE
5779   messages, and won't read our data until we read its.)  Once
5780   <xref linkend="libpq-PQflush"/> returns 0, wait for the socket to be
5781   read-ready and then read the response as described above.
5782  </para>
5783
5784 </sect1>
5785
5786 <sect1 id="libpq-pipeline-mode">
5787  <title>Pipeline Mode</title>
5788
5789  <indexterm zone="libpq-pipeline-mode">
5790   <primary>libpq</primary>
5791   <secondary>pipeline mode</secondary>
5792  </indexterm>
5793
5794  <indexterm zone="libpq-pipeline-mode">
5795   <primary>pipelining</primary>
5796   <secondary>in libpq</secondary>
5797  </indexterm>
5798
5799  <indexterm zone="libpq-pipeline-mode">
5800   <primary>batch mode</primary>
5801   <secondary>in libpq</secondary>
5802  </indexterm>
5803
5804  <para>
5805   <application>libpq</application> pipeline mode allows applications to
5806   send a query without having to read the result of the previously
5807   sent query.  Taking advantage of the pipeline mode, a client will wait
5808   less for the server, since multiple queries/results can be
5809   sent/received in a single network transaction.
5810  </para>
5811
5812  <para>
5813   While pipeline mode provides a significant performance boost, writing
5814   clients using the pipeline mode is more complex because it involves
5815   managing a queue of pending queries and finding which result
5816   corresponds to which query in the queue.
5817  </para>
5818
5819  <para>
5820   Pipeline mode also generally consumes more memory on both the client and server,
5821   though careful and aggressive management of the send/receive queue can mitigate
5822   this.  This applies whether or not the connection is in blocking or non-blocking
5823   mode.
5824  </para>
5825
5826  <para>
5827   While <application>libpq</application>'s pipeline API was introduced in
5828   <productname>PostgreSQL</productname> 14, it is a client-side feature
5829   which doesn't require special server support and works on any server
5830   that supports the v3 extended query protocol.  For more information see
5831   <xref linkend="protocol-flow-pipelining"/>.
5832  </para>
5833
5834  <sect2 id="libpq-pipeline-using">
5835   <title>Using Pipeline Mode</title>
5836
5837   <para>
5838    To issue pipelines, the application must switch the connection
5839    into pipeline mode,
5840    which is done with <xref linkend="libpq-PQenterPipelineMode"/>.
5841    <xref linkend="libpq-PQpipelineStatus"/> can be used
5842    to test whether pipeline mode is active.
5843    In pipeline mode, only <link linkend="libpq-async">asynchronous operations</link>
5844    that utilize the extended query protocol
5845    are permitted, command strings containing multiple SQL commands are
5846    disallowed, and so is <literal>COPY</literal>.
5847    Using synchronous command execution functions
5848    such as <function>PQfn</function>,
5849    <function>PQexec</function>,
5850    <function>PQexecParams</function>,
5851    <function>PQprepare</function>,
5852    <function>PQexecPrepared</function>,
5853    <function>PQdescribePrepared</function>,
5854    <function>PQdescribePortal</function>,
5855    <function>PQclosePrepared</function>,
5856    <function>PQclosePortal</function>,
5857    is an error condition.
5858    <function>PQsendQuery</function> is
5859    also disallowed, because it uses the simple query protocol.
5860    Once all dispatched commands have had their results processed, and
5861    the end pipeline result has been consumed, the application may return
5862    to non-pipelined mode with <xref linkend="libpq-PQexitPipelineMode"/>.
5863   </para>
5864
5865   <note>
5866    <para>
5867     It is best to use pipeline mode with <application>libpq</application> in
5868     <link linkend="libpq-PQsetnonblocking">non-blocking mode</link>. If used
5869     in blocking mode it is possible for a client/server deadlock to occur.
5870      <footnote>
5871       <para>
5872        The client will block trying to send queries to the server, but the
5873        server will block trying to send results to the client from queries
5874        it has already processed. This only occurs when the client sends
5875        enough queries to fill both its output buffer and the server's receive
5876        buffer before it switches to processing input from the server,
5877        but it's hard to predict exactly when that will happen.
5878       </para>
5879      </footnote>
5880    </para>
5881   </note>
5882
5883   <sect3 id="libpq-pipeline-sending">
5884    <title>Issuing Queries</title>
5885
5886    <para>
5887     After entering pipeline mode, the application dispatches requests using
5888     <xref linkend="libpq-PQsendQueryParams"/>
5889     or its prepared-query sibling
5890     <xref linkend="libpq-PQsendQueryPrepared"/>.
5891     These requests are queued on the client-side until flushed to the server;
5892     this occurs when <xref linkend="libpq-PQpipelineSync"/> is used to
5893     establish a synchronization point in the pipeline,
5894     or when <xref linkend="libpq-PQflush"/> is called.
5895     The functions <xref linkend="libpq-PQsendPrepare"/>,
5896     <xref linkend="libpq-PQsendDescribePrepared"/>,
5897     <xref linkend="libpq-PQsendDescribePortal"/>,
5898     <xref linkend="libpq-PQsendClosePrepared"/>, and
5899     <xref linkend="libpq-PQsendClosePortal"/> also work in pipeline mode.
5900     Result processing is described below.
5901    </para>
5902
5903    <para>
5904     The server executes statements, and returns results, in the order the
5905     client sends them.  The server will begin executing the commands in the
5906     pipeline immediately, not waiting for the end of the pipeline.
5907     Note that results are buffered on the server side; the server flushes
5908     that buffer when a synchronization point is established with either
5909     <function>PQpipelineSync</function> or
5910     <function>PQsendPipelineSync</function>, or when
5911     <function>PQsendFlushRequest</function> is called.
5912     If any statement encounters an error, the server aborts the current
5913     transaction and does not execute any subsequent command in the queue
5914     until the next synchronization point;
5915     a <literal>PGRES_PIPELINE_ABORTED</literal> result is produced for
5916     each such command.
5917     (This remains true even if the commands in the pipeline would rollback
5918     the transaction.)
5919     Query processing resumes after the synchronization point.
5920    </para>
5921
5922    <para>
5923     It's fine for one operation to depend on the results of a
5924     prior one; for example, one query may define a table that the next
5925     query in the same pipeline uses. Similarly, an application may
5926     create a named prepared statement and execute it with later
5927     statements in the same pipeline.
5928    </para>
5929   </sect3>
5930
5931   <sect3 id="libpq-pipeline-results">
5932    <title>Processing Results</title>
5933
5934    <para>
5935     To process the result of one query in a pipeline, the application calls
5936     <function>PQgetResult</function> repeatedly and handles each result
5937     until <function>PQgetResult</function> returns null.
5938     The result from the next query in the pipeline may then be retrieved using
5939     <function>PQgetResult</function> again and the cycle repeated.
5940     The application handles individual statement results as normal.
5941     When the results of all the queries in the pipeline have been
5942     returned, <function>PQgetResult</function> returns a result
5943     containing the status value <literal>PGRES_PIPELINE_SYNC</literal>
5944    </para>
5945
5946    <para>
5947     The client may choose to defer result processing until the complete
5948     pipeline has been sent, or interleave that with sending further
5949     queries in the pipeline; see <xref linkend="libpq-pipeline-interleave"/>.
5950    </para>
5951
5952    <para>
5953     <function>PQgetResult</function> behaves the same as for normal
5954     asynchronous processing except that it may contain the new
5955     <type>PGresult</type> types <literal>PGRES_PIPELINE_SYNC</literal>
5956     and <literal>PGRES_PIPELINE_ABORTED</literal>.
5957     <literal>PGRES_PIPELINE_SYNC</literal> is reported exactly once for each
5958     <function>PQpipelineSync</function> or
5959     <function>PQsendPipelineSync</function> at the corresponding point
5960     in the pipeline.
5961     <literal>PGRES_PIPELINE_ABORTED</literal> is emitted in place of a normal
5962     query result for the first error and all subsequent results
5963     until the next <literal>PGRES_PIPELINE_SYNC</literal>;
5964     see <xref linkend="libpq-pipeline-errors"/>.
5965    </para>
5966
5967    <para>
5968     <function>PQisBusy</function>, <function>PQconsumeInput</function>, etc
5969     operate as normal when processing pipeline results.  In particular,
5970     a call to <function>PQisBusy</function> in the middle of a pipeline
5971     returns 0 if the results for all the queries issued so far have been
5972     consumed.
5973    </para>
5974
5975    <para>
5976     <application>libpq</application> does not provide any information to the
5977     application about the query currently being processed (except that
5978     <function>PQgetResult</function> returns null to indicate that we start
5979     returning the results of next query). The application must keep track
5980     of the order in which it sent queries, to associate them with their
5981     corresponding results.
5982     Applications will typically use a state machine or a FIFO queue for this.
5983    </para>
5984
5985   </sect3>
5986
5987   <sect3 id="libpq-pipeline-errors">
5988    <title>Error Handling</title>
5989
5990    <para>
5991     From the client's perspective, after <function>PQresultStatus</function>
5992     returns <literal>PGRES_FATAL_ERROR</literal>,
5993     the pipeline is flagged as aborted.
5994     <function>PQresultStatus</function> will report a
5995     <literal>PGRES_PIPELINE_ABORTED</literal> result for each remaining queued
5996     operation in an aborted pipeline. The result for
5997     <function>PQpipelineSync</function> or
5998     <function>PQsendPipelineSync</function> is reported as
5999     <literal>PGRES_PIPELINE_SYNC</literal> to signal the end of the aborted pipeline
6000     and resumption of normal result processing.
6001    </para>
6002
6003    <para>
6004     The client <emphasis>must</emphasis> process results with
6005     <function>PQgetResult</function> during error recovery.
6006    </para>
6007
6008    <para>
6009     If the pipeline used an implicit transaction, then operations that have
6010     already executed are rolled back and operations that were queued to follow
6011     the failed operation are skipped entirely. The same behavior holds if the
6012     pipeline starts and commits a single explicit transaction (i.e. the first
6013     statement is <literal>BEGIN</literal> and the last is
6014     <literal>COMMIT</literal>) except that the session remains in an aborted
6015     transaction state at the end of the pipeline. If a pipeline contains
6016     <emphasis>multiple explicit transactions</emphasis>, all transactions that
6017     committed prior to the error remain committed, the currently in-progress
6018     transaction is aborted, and all subsequent operations are skipped completely,
6019     including subsequent transactions.  If a pipeline synchronization point
6020     occurs with an explicit transaction block in aborted state, the next pipeline
6021     will become aborted immediately unless the next command puts the transaction
6022     in normal mode with <command>ROLLBACK</command>.
6023    </para>
6024
6025    <note>
6026     <para>
6027      The client must not assume that work is committed when it
6028      <emphasis>sends</emphasis> a <literal>COMMIT</literal> &mdash; only when the
6029      corresponding result is received to confirm the commit is complete.
6030      Because errors arrive asynchronously, the application needs to be able to
6031      restart from the last <emphasis>received</emphasis> committed change and
6032      resend work done after that point if something goes wrong.
6033     </para>
6034    </note>
6035   </sect3>
6036
6037   <sect3 id="libpq-pipeline-interleave">
6038    <title>Interleaving Result Processing and Query Dispatch</title>
6039
6040    <para>
6041     To avoid deadlocks on large pipelines the client should be structured
6042     around a non-blocking event loop using operating system facilities
6043     such as <function>select</function>, <function>poll</function>,
6044     <function>WaitForMultipleObjectEx</function>, etc.
6045    </para>
6046
6047    <para>
6048     The client application should generally maintain a queue of work
6049     remaining to be dispatched and a queue of work that has been dispatched
6050     but not yet had its results processed. When the socket is writable
6051     it should dispatch more work. When the socket is readable it should
6052     read results and process them, matching them up to the next entry in
6053     its corresponding results queue.  Based on available memory, results from the
6054     socket should be read frequently: there's no need to wait until the
6055     pipeline end to read the results.  Pipelines should be scoped to logical
6056     units of work, usually (but not necessarily) one transaction per pipeline.
6057     There's no need to exit pipeline mode and re-enter it between pipelines,
6058     or to wait for one pipeline to finish before sending the next.
6059    </para>
6060
6061    <para>
6062     An example using <function>select()</function> and a simple state
6063     machine to track sent and received work is in
6064     <filename>src/test/modules/libpq_pipeline/libpq_pipeline.c</filename>
6065     in the PostgreSQL source distribution.
6066    </para>
6067   </sect3>
6068  </sect2>
6069
6070  <sect2 id="libpq-pipeline-functions">
6071   <title>Functions Associated with Pipeline Mode</title>
6072
6073   <variablelist>
6074
6075    <varlistentry id="libpq-PQpipelineStatus">
6076     <term><function>PQpipelineStatus</function><indexterm><primary>PQpipelineStatus</primary></indexterm></term>
6077
6078     <listitem>
6079      <para>
6080      Returns the current pipeline mode status of the
6081      <application>libpq</application> connection.
6082<synopsis>
6083PGpipelineStatus PQpipelineStatus(const PGconn *conn);
6084</synopsis>
6085      </para>
6086
6087      <para>
6088       <function>PQpipelineStatus</function> can return one of the following values:
6089
6090       <variablelist>
6091        <varlistentry>
6092         <term>
6093          <literal>PQ_PIPELINE_ON</literal>
6094         </term>
6095         <listitem>
6096          <para>
6097           The <application>libpq</application> connection is in
6098           pipeline mode.
6099          </para>
6100         </listitem>
6101        </varlistentry>
6102
6103        <varlistentry>
6104         <term>
6105          <literal>PQ_PIPELINE_OFF</literal>
6106         </term>
6107         <listitem>
6108          <para>
6109           The <application>libpq</application> connection is
6110           <emphasis>not</emphasis> in pipeline mode.
6111          </para>
6112         </listitem>
6113        </varlistentry>
6114
6115        <varlistentry>
6116         <term>
6117          <literal>PQ_PIPELINE_ABORTED</literal>
6118         </term>
6119         <listitem>
6120          <para>
6121           The <application>libpq</application> connection is in pipeline
6122           mode and an error occurred while processing the current pipeline.
6123           The aborted flag is cleared when <function>PQgetResult</function>
6124           returns a result of type <literal>PGRES_PIPELINE_SYNC</literal>.
6125          </para>
6126         </listitem>
6127        </varlistentry>
6128
6129       </variablelist>
6130      </para>
6131     </listitem>
6132    </varlistentry>
6133
6134    <varlistentry id="libpq-PQenterPipelineMode">
6135     <term><function>PQenterPipelineMode</function><indexterm><primary>PQenterPipelineMode</primary></indexterm></term>
6136
6137     <listitem>
6138      <para>
6139      Causes a connection to enter pipeline mode if it is currently idle or
6140      already in pipeline mode.
6141
6142<synopsis>
6143int PQenterPipelineMode(PGconn *conn);
6144</synopsis>
6145
6146      </para>
6147      <para>
6148       Returns 1 for success.
6149       Returns 0 and has no effect if the connection is not currently
6150       idle, i.e., it has a result ready, or it is waiting for more
6151       input from the server, etc.
6152       This function does not actually send anything to the server,
6153       it just changes the <application>libpq</application> connection
6154       state.
6155      </para>
6156     </listitem>
6157    </varlistentry>
6158
6159    <varlistentry id="libpq-PQexitPipelineMode">
6160     <term><function>PQexitPipelineMode</function><indexterm><primary>PQexitPipelineMode</primary></indexterm></term>
6161
6162     <listitem>
6163      <para>
6164       Causes a connection to exit pipeline mode if it is currently in pipeline mode
6165       with an empty queue and no pending results.
6166<synopsis>
6167int PQexitPipelineMode(PGconn *conn);
6168</synopsis>
6169      </para>
6170      <para>
6171       Returns 1 for success.  Returns 1 and takes no action if not in
6172       pipeline mode. If the current statement isn't finished processing,
6173       or <function>PQgetResult</function> has not been called to collect
6174       results from all previously sent query, returns 0 (in which case,
6175       use <xref linkend="libpq-PQerrorMessage"/> to get more information
6176       about the failure).
6177      </para>
6178     </listitem>
6179    </varlistentry>
6180
6181    <varlistentry id="libpq-PQpipelineSync">
6182     <term><function>PQpipelineSync</function><indexterm><primary>PQpipelineSync</primary></indexterm></term>
6183
6184     <listitem>
6185      <para>
6186       Marks a synchronization point in a pipeline by sending a
6187       <link linkend="protocol-flow-ext-query">sync message</link>
6188       and flushing the send buffer. This serves as
6189       the delimiter of an implicit transaction and an error recovery
6190       point; see <xref linkend="libpq-pipeline-errors"/>.
6191
6192<synopsis>
6193int PQpipelineSync(PGconn *conn);
6194</synopsis>
6195      </para>
6196      <para>
6197       Returns 1 for success. Returns 0 if the connection is not in
6198       pipeline mode or sending a
6199       <link linkend="protocol-flow-ext-query">sync message</link>
6200       failed.
6201      </para>
6202     </listitem>
6203    </varlistentry>
6204
6205    <varlistentry id="libpq-PQsendPipelineSync">
6206     <term><function>PQsendPipelineSync</function><indexterm><primary>PQsendPipelineSync</primary></indexterm></term>
6207
6208     <listitem>
6209      <para>
6210       Marks a synchronization point in a pipeline by sending a
6211       <link linkend="protocol-flow-ext-query">sync message</link>
6212       without flushing the send buffer. This serves as
6213       the delimiter of an implicit transaction and an error recovery
6214       point; see <xref linkend="libpq-pipeline-errors"/>.
6215
6216<synopsis>
6217int PQsendPipelineSync(PGconn *conn);
6218</synopsis>
6219      </para>
6220      <para>
6221       Returns 1 for success. Returns 0 if the connection is not in
6222       pipeline mode or sending a
6223       <link linkend="protocol-flow-ext-query">sync message</link>
6224       failed.
6225       Note that the message is not itself flushed to the server automatically;
6226       use <function>PQflush</function> if necessary.
6227      </para>
6228     </listitem>
6229    </varlistentry>
6230
6231    <varlistentry id="libpq-PQsendFlushRequest">
6232     <term><function>PQsendFlushRequest</function><indexterm><primary>PQsendFlushRequest</primary></indexterm></term>
6233
6234     <listitem>
6235      <para>
6236       Sends a request for the server to flush its output buffer.
6237<synopsis>
6238int PQsendFlushRequest(PGconn *conn);
6239</synopsis>
6240      </para>
6241
6242      <para>
6243       Returns 1 for success.  Returns 0 on any failure.
6244      </para>
6245      <para>
6246       The server flushes its output buffer automatically as a result of
6247       <function>PQpipelineSync</function> being called, or
6248       on any request when not in pipeline mode; this function is useful
6249       to cause the server to flush its output buffer in pipeline mode
6250       without establishing a synchronization point.
6251       Note that the request is not itself flushed to the server automatically;
6252       use <function>PQflush</function> if necessary.
6253      </para>
6254     </listitem>
6255    </varlistentry>
6256   </variablelist>
6257  </sect2>
6258
6259  <sect2 id="libpq-pipeline-tips">
6260   <title>When to Use Pipeline Mode</title>
6261
6262   <para>
6263    Much like asynchronous query mode, there is no meaningful performance
6264    overhead when using pipeline mode. It increases client application complexity,
6265    and extra caution is required to prevent client/server deadlocks, but
6266    pipeline mode can offer considerable performance improvements, in exchange for
6267    increased memory usage from leaving state around longer.
6268   </para>
6269
6270   <para>
6271    Pipeline mode is most useful when the server is distant, i.e., network latency
6272    (<quote>ping time</quote>) is high, and also when many small operations
6273    are being performed in rapid succession.  There is usually less benefit
6274    in using pipelined commands when each query takes many multiples of the client/server
6275    round-trip time to execute.  A 100-statement operation run on a server
6276    300 ms round-trip-time away would take 30 seconds in network latency alone
6277    without pipelining; with pipelining it may spend as little as 0.3 s waiting for
6278    results from the server.
6279   </para>
6280
6281   <para>
6282    Use pipelined commands when your application does lots of small
6283    <literal>INSERT</literal>, <literal>UPDATE</literal> and
6284    <literal>DELETE</literal> operations that can't easily be transformed
6285    into operations on sets, or into a <literal>COPY</literal> operation.
6286   </para>
6287
6288   <para>
6289    Pipeline mode is not useful when information from one operation is required by
6290    the client to produce the next operation. In such cases, the client
6291    would have to introduce a synchronization point and wait for a full client/server
6292    round-trip to get the results it needs. However, it's often possible to
6293    adjust the client design to exchange the required information server-side.
6294    Read-modify-write cycles are especially good candidates; for example:
6295<programlisting>
6296BEGIN;
6297SELECT x FROM mytable WHERE id = 42 FOR UPDATE;
6298-- result: x=2
6299-- client adds 1 to x:
6300UPDATE mytable SET x = 3 WHERE id = 42;
6301COMMIT;
6302</programlisting>
6303    could be much more efficiently done with:
6304<programlisting>
6305UPDATE mytable SET x = x + 1 WHERE id = 42;
6306</programlisting>
6307   </para>
6308
6309   <para>
6310    Pipelining is less useful, and more complex, when a single pipeline contains
6311    multiple transactions (see <xref linkend="libpq-pipeline-errors"/>).
6312   </para>
6313  </sect2>
6314 </sect1>
6315
6316 <!-- keep this not-too-apropos sect1 ID for stability of doc URLs -->
6317 <sect1 id="libpq-single-row-mode">
6318  <title>Retrieving Query Results in Chunks</title>
6319
6320  <indexterm zone="libpq-single-row-mode">
6321   <primary>libpq</primary>
6322   <secondary>single-row mode</secondary>
6323  </indexterm>
6324
6325  <indexterm zone="libpq-single-row-mode">
6326   <primary>libpq</primary>
6327   <secondary>chunked mode</secondary>
6328  </indexterm>
6329
6330  <para>
6331   Ordinarily, <application>libpq</application> collects an SQL command's
6332   entire result and returns it to the application as a single
6333   <structname>PGresult</structname>.  This can be unworkable for commands
6334   that return a large number of rows.  For such cases, applications can use
6335   <xref linkend="libpq-PQsendQuery"/> and <xref linkend="libpq-PQgetResult"/> in
6336   <firstterm>single-row mode</firstterm> or <firstterm>chunked
6337   mode</firstterm>.  In these modes, result row(s) are returned to the
6338   application as they are received from the server, one at a time for
6339   single-row mode or in groups for chunked mode.
6340  </para>
6341
6342  <para>
6343   To enter one of these modes, call <xref linkend="libpq-PQsetSingleRowMode"/>
6344    or <xref linkend="libpq-PQsetChunkedRowsMode"/>
6345   immediately after a successful call of <xref linkend="libpq-PQsendQuery"/>
6346   (or a sibling function).  This mode selection is effective only for the
6347   currently executing query.  Then call <xref linkend="libpq-PQgetResult"/>
6348   repeatedly, until it returns null, as documented in <xref
6349   linkend="libpq-async"/>.  If the query returns any rows, they are returned
6350   as one or more <structname>PGresult</structname> objects, which look like
6351   normal query results except for having status code
6352   <literal>PGRES_SINGLE_TUPLE</literal> for single-row mode or
6353   <literal>PGRES_TUPLES_CHUNK</literal> for chunked mode, instead of
6354   <literal>PGRES_TUPLES_OK</literal>.  There is exactly one result row in
6355   each <literal>PGRES_SINGLE_TUPLE</literal> object, while
6356   a <literal>PGRES_TUPLES_CHUNK</literal> object contains at least one
6357   row but not more than the specified number of rows per chunk.
6358   After the last row, or immediately if
6359   the query returns zero rows, a zero-row object with status
6360   <literal>PGRES_TUPLES_OK</literal> is returned; this is the signal that no
6361   more rows will arrive.  (But note that it is still necessary to continue
6362   calling <xref linkend="libpq-PQgetResult"/> until it returns null.)  All of
6363   these <structname>PGresult</structname> objects will contain the same row
6364   description data (column names, types, etc.) that an ordinary
6365   <structname>PGresult</structname> object for the query would have.
6366   Each object should be freed with <xref linkend="libpq-PQclear"/> as usual.
6367  </para>
6368
6369  <para>
6370   When using pipeline mode, single-row or chunked mode needs to be
6371   activated for each query in the pipeline before retrieving results for
6372   that query with <function>PQgetResult</function>.
6373   See <xref linkend="libpq-pipeline-mode"/> for more information.
6374  </para>
6375
6376  <para>
6377   <variablelist>
6378    <varlistentry id="libpq-PQsetSingleRowMode">
6379     <term><function>PQsetSingleRowMode</function><indexterm><primary>PQsetSingleRowMode</primary></indexterm></term>
6380
6381     <listitem>
6382      <para>
6383       Select single-row mode for the currently-executing query.
6384
6385<synopsis>
6386int PQsetSingleRowMode(PGconn *conn);
6387</synopsis>
6388      </para>
6389
6390      <para>
6391       This function can only be called immediately after
6392       <xref linkend="libpq-PQsendQuery"/> or one of its sibling functions,
6393       before any other operation on the connection such as
6394       <xref linkend="libpq-PQconsumeInput"/> or
6395       <xref linkend="libpq-PQgetResult"/>.  If called at the correct time,
6396       the function activates single-row mode for the current query and
6397       returns 1.  Otherwise the mode stays unchanged and the function
6398       returns 0.  In any case, the mode reverts to normal after
6399       completion of the current query.
6400      </para>
6401     </listitem>
6402    </varlistentry>
6403
6404    <varlistentry id="libpq-PQsetChunkedRowsMode">
6405     <term><function>PQsetChunkedRowsMode</function><indexterm><primary>PQsetChunkedRowsMode</primary></indexterm></term>
6406
6407     <listitem>
6408      <para>
6409       Select chunked mode for the currently-executing query.
6410
6411<synopsis>
6412int PQsetChunkedRowsMode(PGconn *conn, int chunkSize);
6413</synopsis>
6414      </para>
6415
6416      <para>
6417       This function is similar to
6418       <xref linkend="libpq-PQsetSingleRowMode"/>, except that it
6419       specifies retrieval of up to <replaceable>chunkSize</replaceable> rows
6420       per <structname>PGresult</structname>, not necessarily just one row.
6421       This function can only be called immediately after
6422       <xref linkend="libpq-PQsendQuery"/> or one of its sibling functions,
6423       before any other operation on the connection such as
6424       <xref linkend="libpq-PQconsumeInput"/> or
6425       <xref linkend="libpq-PQgetResult"/>.  If called at the correct time,
6426       the function activates chunked mode for the current query and
6427       returns 1.  Otherwise the mode stays unchanged and the function
6428       returns 0.  In any case, the mode reverts to normal after
6429       completion of the current query.
6430      </para>
6431     </listitem>
6432    </varlistentry>
6433   </variablelist>
6434  </para>
6435
6436  <caution>
6437   <para>
6438    While processing a query, the server may return some rows and then
6439    encounter an error, causing the query to be aborted.  Ordinarily,
6440    <application>libpq</application> discards any such rows and reports only the
6441    error.  But in single-row or chunked mode, some rows may have already
6442    been returned to the application. Hence, the application will see some
6443    <literal>PGRES_SINGLE_TUPLE</literal> or <literal>PGRES_TUPLES_CHUNK</literal>
6444    <structname>PGresult</structname>
6445    objects followed by a <literal>PGRES_FATAL_ERROR</literal> object.  For
6446    proper transactional behavior, the application must be designed to
6447    discard or undo whatever has been done with the previously-processed
6448    rows, if the query ultimately fails.
6449   </para>
6450  </caution>
6451
6452 </sect1>
6453
6454 <sect1 id="libpq-cancel">
6455  <title>Canceling Queries in Progress</title>
6456
6457  <indexterm zone="libpq-cancel">
6458   <primary>canceling SQL queries</primary>
6459  </indexterm>
6460  <indexterm zone="libpq-cancel">
6461   <primary>query cancellation</primary>
6462  </indexterm>
6463
6464  <sect2 id="libpq-cancel-functions">
6465   <title>Functions for Sending Cancel Requests</title>
6466   <variablelist>
6467    <varlistentry id="libpq-PQcancelCreate">
6468     <term><function>PQcancelCreate</function><indexterm><primary>PQcancelCreate</primary></indexterm></term>
6469
6470     <listitem>
6471      <para>
6472       Prepares a connection over which a cancel request can be sent.
6473<synopsis>
6474PGcancelConn *PQcancelCreate(PGconn *conn);
6475</synopsis>
6476      </para>
6477
6478      <para>
6479       <xref linkend="libpq-PQcancelCreate"/> creates a
6480       <structname>PGcancelConn</structname><indexterm><primary>PGcancelConn</primary></indexterm>
6481       object, but it won't instantly start sending a cancel request over this
6482       connection. A cancel request can be sent over this connection in a
6483       blocking manner using <xref linkend="libpq-PQcancelBlocking"/> and in a
6484       non-blocking manner using <xref linkend="libpq-PQcancelStart"/>.
6485       The return value can be passed to <xref linkend="libpq-PQcancelStatus"/>
6486       to check if the <structname>PGcancelConn</structname> object was
6487       created successfully. The <structname>PGcancelConn</structname> object
6488       is an opaque structure that is not meant to be accessed directly by the
6489       application. This <structname>PGcancelConn</structname> object can be
6490       used to cancel the query that's running on the original connection in a
6491       thread-safe way.
6492      </para>
6493
6494      <para>
6495       Many connection parameters of the original client will be reused when
6496       setting up the connection for the cancel request. Importantly, if the
6497       original connection requires encryption of the connection and/or
6498       verification of the target host (using <literal>sslmode</literal> or
6499       <literal>gssencmode</literal>), then the connection for the cancel
6500       request is made with these same requirements. Any connection options
6501       that are only used during authentication or after authentication of the
6502       client are ignored though, because cancellation requests do not require
6503       authentication and the connection is closed right after the cancellation
6504       request is submitted.
6505      </para>
6506
6507      <para>
6508       Note that when <function>PQcancelCreate</function> returns a non-null
6509       pointer, you must call <xref linkend="libpq-PQcancelFinish"/> when you
6510       are finished with it, in order to dispose of the structure and any
6511       associated memory blocks. This must be done even if the cancel request
6512       failed or was abandoned.
6513      </para>
6514     </listitem>
6515    </varlistentry>
6516
6517    <varlistentry id="libpq-PQcancelBlocking">
6518     <term><function>PQcancelBlocking</function><indexterm><primary>PQcancelBlocking</primary></indexterm></term>
6519
6520     <listitem>
6521      <para>
6522       Requests that the server abandons processing of the current command
6523       in a blocking manner.
6524<synopsis>
6525int PQcancelBlocking(PGcancelConn *cancelConn);
6526</synopsis>
6527      </para>
6528
6529      <para>
6530       The request is made over the given <structname>PGcancelConn</structname>,
6531       which needs to be created with <xref linkend="libpq-PQcancelCreate"/>.
6532       The return value of <xref linkend="libpq-PQcancelBlocking"/>
6533       is 1 if the cancel request was successfully
6534       dispatched and 0 if not. If it was unsuccessful, the error message can be
6535       retrieved using <xref linkend="libpq-PQcancelErrorMessage"/>.
6536      </para>
6537
6538      <para>
6539       Successful dispatch of the cancellation is no guarantee that the request
6540       will have any effect, however. If the cancellation is effective, the
6541       command being canceled will terminate early and return an error result.
6542       If the cancellation fails (say, because the server was already done
6543       processing the command), then there will be no visible result at all.
6544      </para>
6545
6546     </listitem>
6547    </varlistentry>
6548
6549    <varlistentry id="libpq-PQcancelStart">
6550     <term><function>PQcancelStart</function><indexterm><primary>PQcancelStart</primary></indexterm></term>
6551     <term><function>PQcancelPoll</function><indexterm><primary>PQcancelPoll</primary></indexterm></term>
6552
6553     <listitem>
6554      <para>
6555       Requests that the server abandons processing of the current command
6556       in a non-blocking manner.
6557<synopsis>
6558int PQcancelStart(PGcancelConn *cancelConn);
6559
6560PostgresPollingStatusType PQcancelPoll(PGcancelConn *cancelConn);
6561</synopsis>
6562      </para>
6563
6564      <para>
6565       The request is made over the given <structname>PGcancelConn</structname>,
6566       which needs to be created with <xref linkend="libpq-PQcancelCreate"/>.
6567       The return value of <xref linkend="libpq-PQcancelStart"/>
6568       is 1 if the cancellation request could be started and 0 if not.
6569       If it was unsuccessful, the error message can be
6570       retrieved using <xref linkend="libpq-PQcancelErrorMessage"/>.
6571      </para>
6572
6573      <para>
6574       If <function>PQcancelStart</function> succeeds, the next stage
6575       is to poll <application>libpq</application> so that it can proceed with
6576       the cancel connection sequence.
6577       Use <xref linkend="libpq-PQcancelSocket"/> to obtain the descriptor of the
6578       socket underlying the database connection.
6579       (Caution: do not assume that the socket remains the same
6580       across <function>PQcancelPoll</function> calls.)
6581       Loop thus: If <function>PQcancelPoll(cancelConn)</function> last returned
6582       <symbol>PGRES_POLLING_READING</symbol>, wait until the socket is ready to
6583       read (as indicated by <function>select()</function>,
6584       <function>poll()</function>, or similar system function).
6585       Then call <function>PQcancelPoll(cancelConn)</function> again.
6586       Conversely, if <function>PQcancelPoll(cancelConn)</function> last returned
6587       <symbol>PGRES_POLLING_WRITING</symbol>, wait until the socket is ready
6588       to write, then call <function>PQcancelPoll(cancelConn)</function> again.
6589       On the first iteration, i.e., if you have yet to call
6590       <function>PQcancelPoll(cancelConn)</function>, behave as if it last returned
6591       <symbol>PGRES_POLLING_WRITING</symbol>.  Continue this loop until
6592       <function>PQcancelPoll(cancelConn)</function> returns
6593       <symbol>PGRES_POLLING_FAILED</symbol>, indicating the connection procedure
6594       has failed, or <symbol>PGRES_POLLING_OK</symbol>, indicating cancel
6595       request was successfully dispatched.
6596      </para>
6597
6598      <para>
6599       Successful dispatch of the cancellation is no guarantee that the request
6600       will have any effect, however. If the cancellation is effective, the
6601       command being canceled will terminate early and return an error result.
6602       If the cancellation fails (say, because the server was already done
6603       processing the command), then there will be no visible result at all.
6604      </para>
6605
6606      <para>
6607       At any time during connection, the status of the connection can be
6608       checked by calling <xref linkend="libpq-PQcancelStatus"/>.
6609       If this call returns <symbol>CONNECTION_BAD</symbol>, then
6610       the cancel procedure has failed; if the call returns
6611       <symbol>CONNECTION_OK</symbol>, then cancel request was
6612       successfully dispatched.
6613       Both of these states are equally detectable from the return value of
6614       <function>PQcancelPoll</function>, described above.
6615       Other states might also occur during (and only during) an asynchronous
6616       connection procedure.
6617       These indicate the current stage of the connection procedure and might
6618       be useful to provide feedback to the user for example.
6619       These statuses are:
6620
6621       <variablelist>
6622        <varlistentry id="libpq-cancel-connection-allocated">
6623         <term><symbol>CONNECTION_ALLOCATED</symbol></term>
6624         <listitem>
6625          <para>
6626           Waiting for a call to <xref linkend="libpq-PQcancelStart"/> or
6627           <xref linkend="libpq-PQcancelBlocking"/>, to actually open the
6628           socket. This is the connection state right after
6629           calling <xref linkend="libpq-PQcancelCreate"/>
6630           or <xref linkend="libpq-PQcancelReset"/>. No connection to the
6631           server has been initiated yet at this point. To actually start
6632           sending the cancel request use <xref linkend="libpq-PQcancelStart"/> or
6633           <xref linkend="libpq-PQcancelBlocking"/>.
6634          </para>
6635         </listitem>
6636        </varlistentry>
6637
6638        <varlistentry id="libpq-cancel-connection-started">
6639         <term><symbol>CONNECTION_STARTED</symbol></term>
6640         <listitem>
6641          <para>
6642           Waiting for connection to be made.
6643          </para>
6644         </listitem>
6645        </varlistentry>
6646
6647        <varlistentry id="libpq-cancel-connection-made">
6648         <term><symbol>CONNECTION_MADE</symbol></term>
6649         <listitem>
6650          <para>
6651           Connection OK; waiting to send.
6652          </para>
6653         </listitem>
6654        </varlistentry>
6655
6656        <varlistentry id="libpq-cancel-connection-awaiting-response">
6657         <term><symbol>CONNECTION_AWAITING_RESPONSE</symbol></term>
6658         <listitem>
6659          <para>
6660           Waiting for a response from the server.
6661          </para>
6662         </listitem>
6663        </varlistentry>
6664
6665        <varlistentry id="libpq-cancel-connection-ssl-startup">
6666         <term><symbol>CONNECTION_SSL_STARTUP</symbol></term>
6667         <listitem>
6668          <para>
6669           Negotiating SSL encryption.
6670          </para>
6671         </listitem>
6672        </varlistentry>
6673
6674        <varlistentry id="libpq-cancel-connection-gss-startup">
6675         <term><symbol>CONNECTION_GSS_STARTUP</symbol></term>
6676         <listitem>
6677          <para>
6678           Negotiating GSS encryption.
6679          </para>
6680         </listitem>
6681        </varlistentry>
6682       </variablelist>
6683
6684       Note that, although these constants will remain (in order to maintain
6685       compatibility), an application should never rely upon these occurring in a
6686       particular order, or at all, or on the status always being one of these
6687       documented values. An application might do something like this:
6688<programlisting>
6689switch(PQcancelStatus(conn))
6690{
6691        case CONNECTION_STARTED:
6692            feedback = "Connecting...";
6693            break;
6694
6695        case CONNECTION_MADE:
6696            feedback = "Connected to server...";
6697            break;
6698.
6699.
6700.
6701        default:
6702            feedback = "Connecting...";
6703}
6704</programlisting>
6705      </para>
6706
6707      <para>
6708       The <literal>connect_timeout</literal> connection parameter is ignored
6709       when using <function>PQcancelPoll</function>; it is the application's
6710       responsibility to decide whether an excessive amount of time has elapsed.
6711       Otherwise, <function>PQcancelStart</function> followed by a
6712       <function>PQcancelPoll</function> loop is equivalent to
6713       <xref linkend="libpq-PQcancelBlocking"/>.
6714      </para>
6715
6716     </listitem>
6717    </varlistentry>
6718
6719    <varlistentry id="libpq-PQcancelStatus">
6720     <term><function>PQcancelStatus</function><indexterm><primary>PQcancelStatus</primary></indexterm></term>
6721
6722     <listitem>
6723      <para>
6724       Returns the status of the cancel connection.
6725<synopsis>
6726ConnStatusType PQcancelStatus(const PGcancelConn *cancelConn);
6727</synopsis>
6728      </para>
6729
6730      <para>
6731       The status can be one of a number of values.  However, only three of
6732       these are seen outside of an asynchronous cancel procedure:
6733       <symbol>CONNECTION_ALLOCATED</symbol>,
6734       <symbol>CONNECTION_OK</symbol> and
6735       <symbol>CONNECTION_BAD</symbol>. The initial state of a
6736       <function>PGcancelConn</function> that's successfully created using
6737       <xref linkend="libpq-PQcancelCreate"/> is <symbol>CONNECTION_ALLOCATED</symbol>.
6738       A cancel request that was successfully dispatched
6739       has the status <symbol>CONNECTION_OK</symbol>.  A failed
6740       cancel attempt is signaled by status
6741       <symbol>CONNECTION_BAD</symbol>.  An OK status will
6742       remain so until <xref linkend="libpq-PQcancelFinish"/> or
6743       <xref linkend="libpq-PQcancelReset"/> is called.
6744      </para>
6745
6746      <para>
6747       See the entry for <xref linkend="libpq-PQcancelStart"/> with regards
6748       to other status codes that might be returned.
6749      </para>
6750
6751      <para>
6752       Successful dispatch of the cancellation is no guarantee that the request
6753       will have any effect, however. If the cancellation is effective, the
6754       command being canceled will terminate early and return an error result.
6755       If the cancellation fails (say, because the server was already done
6756       processing the command), then there will be no visible result at all.
6757      </para>
6758
6759     </listitem>
6760    </varlistentry>
6761
6762    <varlistentry id="libpq-PQcancelSocket">
6763     <term><function>PQcancelSocket</function><indexterm><primary>PQcancelSocket</primary></indexterm></term>
6764
6765     <listitem>
6766      <para>
6767       Obtains the file descriptor number of the cancel connection socket to
6768       the server.
6769<synopsis>
6770int PQcancelSocket(const PGcancelConn *cancelConn);
6771</synopsis>
6772      </para>
6773
6774      <para>
6775       A valid descriptor will be greater than or equal to 0;
6776       a result of -1 indicates that no server connection is currently open.
6777       This might change as a result of calling any of the functions
6778       in this section on the <structname>PGcancelConn</structname>
6779       (except for <xref linkend="libpq-PQcancelErrorMessage"/> and
6780       <function>PQcancelSocket</function> itself).
6781      </para>
6782     </listitem>
6783    </varlistentry>
6784
6785    <varlistentry id="libpq-PQcancelErrorMessage">
6786     <term>
6787      <function>PQcancelErrorMessage</function><indexterm><primary>PQcancelErrorMessage</primary></indexterm>
6788      <indexterm><primary>error message</primary><secondary>in <structname>PGcancelConn</structname></secondary></indexterm>
6789     </term>
6790
6791     <listitem>
6792      <para>
6793       Returns the error message most recently generated by an
6794       operation on the cancel connection.
6795<synopsis>
6796char *PQcancelErrorMessage(const PGcancelConn *cancelconn);
6797</synopsis>
6798      </para>
6799
6800      <para>
6801       Nearly all <application>libpq</application> functions that take a
6802       <structname>PGcancelConn</structname> will set a message for
6803       <xref linkend="libpq-PQcancelErrorMessage"/> if they fail.
6804       Note that by <application>libpq</application> convention,
6805       a nonempty <xref linkend="libpq-PQcancelErrorMessage"/> result
6806       can consist of multiple lines, and will include a trailing newline.
6807       The caller should not free the result directly.
6808       It will be freed when the associated
6809       <structname>PGcancelConn</structname> handle is passed to
6810       <xref linkend="libpq-PQcancelFinish"/>.  The result string should not be
6811       expected to remain the same across operations on the
6812       <literal>PGcancelConn</literal> structure.
6813      </para>
6814     </listitem>
6815    </varlistentry>
6816
6817    <varlistentry id="libpq-PQcancelFinish">
6818     <term><function>PQcancelFinish</function><indexterm><primary>PQcancelFinish</primary></indexterm></term>
6819     <listitem>
6820      <para>
6821       Closes the cancel connection (if it did not finish sending the
6822       cancel request yet). Also frees memory used by the
6823       <structname>PGcancelConn</structname> object.
6824<synopsis>
6825void PQcancelFinish(PGcancelConn *cancelConn);
6826</synopsis>
6827      </para>
6828
6829      <para>
6830       Note that even if the cancel attempt fails (as
6831       indicated by <xref linkend="libpq-PQcancelStatus"/>), the
6832       application should call <xref linkend="libpq-PQcancelFinish"/>
6833       to free the memory used by the <structname>PGcancelConn</structname>
6834       object.
6835       The <structname>PGcancelConn</structname> pointer must not be used
6836       again after <xref linkend="libpq-PQcancelFinish"/> has been called.
6837      </para>
6838     </listitem>
6839    </varlistentry>
6840
6841    <varlistentry id="libpq-PQcancelReset">
6842     <term><function>PQcancelReset</function><indexterm><primary>PQcancelReset</primary></indexterm></term>
6843     <listitem>
6844      <para>
6845       Resets the <symbol>PGcancelConn</symbol> so it can be reused for a new
6846       cancel connection.
6847<synopsis>
6848void PQcancelReset(PGcancelConn *cancelConn);
6849</synopsis>
6850      </para>
6851
6852      <para>
6853       If the <symbol>PGcancelConn</symbol> is currently used to send a cancel
6854       request, then this connection is closed. It will then prepare the
6855       <symbol>PGcancelConn</symbol> object such that it can be used to send a
6856       new cancel request.
6857      </para>
6858
6859      <para>
6860       This can be used to create one <structname>PGcancelConn</structname>
6861       for a <structname>PGconn</structname> and reuse it multiple times
6862       throughout the lifetime of the original <structname>PGconn</structname>.
6863      </para>
6864     </listitem>
6865    </varlistentry>
6866   </variablelist>
6867  </sect2>
6868
6869  <sect2 id="libpq-cancel-deprecated">
6870   <title>Obsolete Functions for Sending Cancel Requests</title>
6871
6872   <para>
6873    These functions represent older methods of sending cancel requests.
6874    Although they still work, they are deprecated due to not sending the cancel
6875    requests in an encrypted manner, even when the original connection
6876    specified <literal>sslmode</literal> or <literal>gssencmode</literal> to
6877    require encryption. Thus these older methods are heavily discouraged from
6878    being used in new code, and it is recommended to change existing code to
6879    use the new functions instead.
6880   </para>
6881
6882   <variablelist>
6883    <varlistentry id="libpq-PQgetCancel">
6884     <term><function>PQgetCancel</function><indexterm><primary>PQgetCancel</primary></indexterm></term>
6885
6886     <listitem>
6887      <para>
6888       Creates a data structure containing the information needed to cancel
6889       a command using <xref linkend="libpq-PQcancel"/>.
6890<synopsis>
6891PGcancel *PQgetCancel(PGconn *conn);
6892</synopsis>
6893      </para>
6894
6895      <para>
6896       <xref linkend="libpq-PQgetCancel"/> creates a
6897       <structname>PGcancel</structname><indexterm><primary>PGcancel</primary></indexterm>
6898       object given a <structname>PGconn</structname> connection object.
6899       It will return <symbol>NULL</symbol> if the given <parameter>conn</parameter>
6900       is <symbol>NULL</symbol> or an invalid connection.
6901       The <structname>PGcancel</structname> object is an opaque
6902       structure that is not meant to be accessed directly by the
6903       application; it can only be passed to <xref linkend="libpq-PQcancel"/>
6904       or <xref linkend="libpq-PQfreeCancel"/>.
6905      </para>
6906     </listitem>
6907    </varlistentry>
6908
6909    <varlistentry id="libpq-PQfreeCancel">
6910     <term><function>PQfreeCancel</function><indexterm><primary>PQfreeCancel</primary></indexterm></term>
6911
6912     <listitem>
6913      <para>
6914       Frees a data structure created by <xref linkend="libpq-PQgetCancel"/>.
6915<synopsis>
6916void PQfreeCancel(PGcancel *cancel);
6917</synopsis>
6918      </para>
6919
6920      <para>
6921       <xref linkend="libpq-PQfreeCancel"/> frees a data object previously created
6922       by <xref linkend="libpq-PQgetCancel"/>.
6923      </para>
6924     </listitem>
6925    </varlistentry>
6926
6927    <varlistentry id="libpq-PQcancel">
6928     <term><function>PQcancel</function><indexterm><primary>PQcancel</primary></indexterm></term>
6929
6930     <listitem>
6931      <para>
6932       <xref linkend="libpq-PQcancel"/> is a deprecated and insecure
6933       variant of <xref linkend="libpq-PQcancelBlocking"/>, but one that can be
6934       used safely from within a signal handler.
6935<synopsis>
6936int PQcancel(PGcancel *cancel, char *errbuf, int errbufsize);
6937</synopsis>
6938      </para>
6939
6940      <para>
6941       <xref linkend="libpq-PQcancel"/> only exists because of backwards
6942       compatibility reasons. <xref linkend="libpq-PQcancelBlocking"/> should be
6943       used instead. The only benefit that <xref linkend="libpq-PQcancel"/> has
6944       is that it can be safely invoked from a signal handler, if the
6945       <parameter>errbuf</parameter> is a local variable in the signal handler.
6946       However, this is generally not considered a big enough benefit to be
6947       worth the security issues that this function has.
6948      </para>
6949
6950      <para>
6951       The <structname>PGcancel</structname> object is read-only as far as
6952       <xref linkend="libpq-PQcancel"/> is concerned, so it can also be invoked
6953       from a thread that is separate from the one manipulating the
6954       <structname>PGconn</structname> object.
6955      </para>
6956
6957      <para>
6958       The return value of <xref linkend="libpq-PQcancel"/> is 1 if the
6959       cancel request was successfully dispatched and 0 if not.
6960       If not, <parameter>errbuf</parameter> is filled with an explanatory
6961       error message.
6962       <parameter>errbuf</parameter> must be a char array of size
6963       <parameter>errbufsize</parameter> (the recommended size is 256 bytes).
6964      </para>
6965     </listitem>
6966    </varlistentry>
6967   </variablelist>
6968
6969   <variablelist>
6970    <varlistentry id="libpq-PQrequestCancel">
6971     <term><function>PQrequestCancel</function><indexterm><primary>PQrequestCancel</primary></indexterm></term>
6972
6973     <listitem>
6974      <para>
6975       <xref linkend="libpq-PQrequestCancel"/> is a deprecated and insecure
6976       variant of <xref linkend="libpq-PQcancelBlocking"/>.
6977<synopsis>
6978int PQrequestCancel(PGconn *conn);
6979</synopsis>
6980      </para>
6981
6982      <para>
6983       <xref linkend="libpq-PQrequestCancel"/> only exists because of backwards
6984       compatibility reasons. <xref linkend="libpq-PQcancelBlocking"/> should be
6985       used instead. There is no benefit to using
6986       <xref linkend="libpq-PQrequestCancel"/> over
6987       <xref linkend="libpq-PQcancelBlocking"/>.
6988      </para>
6989
6990      <para>
6991       Requests that the server abandon processing of the current
6992       command.  It operates directly on the
6993       <structname>PGconn</structname> object, and in case of failure stores the
6994       error message in the <structname>PGconn</structname> object (whence it can
6995       be retrieved by <xref linkend="libpq-PQerrorMessage"/>).  Although
6996       the functionality is the same, this approach is not safe within
6997       multiple-thread programs or signal handlers, since it is possible
6998       that overwriting the <structname>PGconn</structname>'s error message will
6999       mess up the operation currently in progress on the connection.
7000      </para>
7001     </listitem>
7002    </varlistentry>
7003   </variablelist>
7004  </sect2>
7005 </sect1>
7006
7007 <sect1 id="libpq-fastpath">
7008  <title>The Fast-Path Interface</title>
7009
7010  <indexterm zone="libpq-fastpath">
7011   <primary>fast path</primary>
7012  </indexterm>
7013
7014  <para>
7015   <productname>PostgreSQL</productname> provides a fast-path interface
7016   to send simple function calls to the server.
7017  </para>
7018
7019  <warning>
7020   <para>
7021    This interface is unsafe and should not be used.  When
7022    <parameter>result_is_int</parameter> is set to <literal>0</literal>,
7023    <function>PQfn</function> may write data beyond the end of
7024    <parameter>result_buf</parameter>, regardless of whether the buffer has
7025    enough space for the requested number of bytes.  Furthermore, it is
7026    obsolete, as one can achieve similar
7027    performance and greater functionality by setting up a prepared
7028    statement to define the function call.  Then, executing the statement
7029    with binary transmission of parameters and results substitutes for a
7030    fast-path function call.
7031   </para>
7032  </warning>
7033
7034  <para>
7035   The function <function id="libpq-PQfn">PQfn</function><indexterm><primary>PQfn</primary></indexterm>
7036   requests execution of a server function via the fast-path interface:
7037<synopsis>
7038PGresult *PQfn(PGconn *conn,
7039               int fnid,
7040               int *result_buf,
7041               int *result_len,
7042               int result_is_int,
7043               const PQArgBlock *args,
7044               int nargs);
7045
7046typedef struct
7047{
7048    int len;
7049    int isint;
7050    union
7051    {
7052        int *ptr;
7053        int integer;
7054    } u;
7055} PQArgBlock;
7056</synopsis>
7057  </para>
7058
7059  <para>
7060   The <parameter>fnid</parameter> argument is the OID of the function to be
7061   executed.  <parameter>args</parameter> and <parameter>nargs</parameter> define the
7062   parameters to be passed to the function; they must match the declared
7063   function argument list.  When the <parameter>isint</parameter> field of a
7064   parameter structure is true, the <parameter>u.integer</parameter> value is sent
7065   to the server as an integer of the indicated length (this must be
7066   2 or 4 bytes); proper byte-swapping occurs.  When <parameter>isint</parameter>
7067   is false, the indicated number of bytes at <parameter>*u.ptr</parameter> are
7068   sent with no processing; the data must be in the format expected by
7069   the server for binary transmission of the function's argument data
7070   type.  (The declaration of <parameter>u.ptr</parameter> as being of
7071   type <type>int *</type> is historical; it would be better to consider
7072   it <type>void *</type>.)
7073   <parameter>result_buf</parameter> points to the buffer in which to place
7074   the function's return value.  The caller must have allocated sufficient
7075   space to store the return value.  (There is no check!) The actual result
7076   length in bytes will be returned in the integer pointed to by
7077   <parameter>result_len</parameter>.  If a 2- or 4-byte integer result
7078   is expected, set <parameter>result_is_int</parameter> to 1, otherwise
7079   set it to 0.  Setting <parameter>result_is_int</parameter> to 1 causes
7080   <application>libpq</application> to byte-swap the value if necessary, so that it
7081   is delivered as a proper <type>int</type> value for the client machine;
7082   note that a 4-byte integer is delivered into <parameter>*result_buf</parameter>
7083   for either allowed result size.
7084   When <parameter>result_is_int</parameter> is 0, the binary-format byte string
7085   sent by the server is returned unmodified. (In this case it's better
7086   to consider <parameter>result_buf</parameter> as being of
7087   type <type>void *</type>.)
7088  </para>
7089
7090  <para>
7091   <function>PQfn</function> always returns a valid
7092   <structname>PGresult</structname> pointer, with
7093   status <literal>PGRES_COMMAND_OK</literal> for success
7094   or <literal>PGRES_FATAL_ERROR</literal> if some problem was encountered.
7095   The result status should be
7096   checked before the result is used.   The caller is responsible for
7097   freeing  the  <structname>PGresult</structname>  with
7098   <xref linkend="libpq-PQclear"/> when it is no longer needed.
7099  </para>
7100
7101  <para>
7102   To pass a NULL argument to the function, set
7103   the <parameter>len</parameter> field of that parameter structure
7104   to <literal>-1</literal>; the <parameter>isint</parameter>
7105   and <parameter>u</parameter> fields are then irrelevant.
7106  </para>
7107
7108  <para>
7109   If the function returns NULL, <parameter>*result_len</parameter> is set
7110   to <literal>-1</literal>, and <parameter>*result_buf</parameter> is not
7111   modified.
7112  </para>
7113
7114  <para>
7115   Note that it is not possible to handle set-valued results when using
7116   this interface.  Also, the function must be a plain function, not an
7117   aggregate, window function, or procedure.
7118  </para>
7119
7120 </sect1>
7121
7122 <sect1 id="libpq-notify">
7123  <title>Asynchronous Notification</title>
7124
7125  <indexterm zone="libpq-notify">
7126   <primary>NOTIFY</primary>
7127   <secondary>in libpq</secondary>
7128  </indexterm>
7129
7130  <para>
7131   <productname>PostgreSQL</productname> offers asynchronous notification
7132   via the <command>LISTEN</command> and <command>NOTIFY</command>
7133   commands.  A client session registers its interest in a particular
7134   notification channel with the <command>LISTEN</command> command (and
7135   can stop listening with the <command>UNLISTEN</command> command).  All
7136   sessions listening on a particular channel will be notified
7137   asynchronously when a <command>NOTIFY</command> command with that
7138   channel name is executed by any session. A <quote>payload</quote> string can
7139   be passed to communicate additional data to the listeners.
7140  </para>
7141
7142  <para>
7143   <application>libpq</application> applications submit
7144   <command>LISTEN</command>, <command>UNLISTEN</command>,
7145   and <command>NOTIFY</command> commands as
7146   ordinary SQL commands.  The arrival of <command>NOTIFY</command>
7147   messages can subsequently be detected by calling
7148   <function id="libpq-PQnotifies">PQnotifies</function>.<indexterm><primary>PQnotifies</primary></indexterm>
7149  </para>
7150
7151  <para>
7152   The function <function>PQnotifies</function> returns the next notification
7153   from a list of unhandled notification messages received from the server.
7154   It returns a null pointer if there are no pending notifications.  Once a
7155   notification is returned from <function>PQnotifies</function>, it is considered
7156   handled and will be removed from the list of notifications.
7157
7158<synopsis>
7159PGnotify *PQnotifies(PGconn *conn);
7160
7161typedef struct pgNotify
7162{
7163    char *relname;              /* notification channel name */
7164    int  be_pid;                /* process ID of notifying server process */
7165    char *extra;                /* notification payload string */
7166} PGnotify;
7167</synopsis>
7168
7169   After processing a <structname>PGnotify</structname> object returned
7170   by <function>PQnotifies</function>, be sure to free it with
7171   <xref linkend="libpq-PQfreemem"/>.  It is sufficient to free the
7172   <structname>PGnotify</structname> pointer; the
7173   <structfield>relname</structfield> and <structfield>extra</structfield>
7174   fields do not represent separate allocations.  (The names of these fields
7175   are historical; in particular, channel names need not have anything to
7176   do with relation names.)
7177  </para>
7178
7179  <para>
7180   <xref linkend="libpq-example-2"/> gives a sample program that illustrates
7181   the use of asynchronous notification.
7182  </para>
7183
7184  <para>
7185   <function>PQnotifies</function> does not actually read data from the
7186   server; it just returns messages previously absorbed by another
7187   <application>libpq</application> function.  In ancient releases of
7188   <application>libpq</application>, the only way to ensure timely receipt
7189   of <command>NOTIFY</command> messages was to constantly submit commands, even
7190   empty ones, and then check <function>PQnotifies</function> after each
7191   <xref linkend="libpq-PQexec"/>.  While this still works, it is deprecated
7192   as a waste of processing power.
7193  </para>
7194
7195  <para>
7196   A better way to check for <command>NOTIFY</command> messages when you have no
7197   useful commands to execute is to call
