7198   <xref linkend="libpq-PQconsumeInput"/>, then check
7199   <function>PQnotifies</function>.  You can use
7200   <function>select()</function> to wait for data to arrive from the
7201   server, thereby using no <acronym>CPU</acronym> power unless there is
7202   something to do.  (See <xref linkend="libpq-PQsocket"/> to obtain the file
7203   descriptor number to use with <function>select()</function>.) Note that
7204   this will work OK whether you submit commands with
7205   <xref linkend="libpq-PQsendQuery"/>/<xref linkend="libpq-PQgetResult"/> or
7206   simply use <xref linkend="libpq-PQexec"/>.  You should, however, remember
7207   to check <function>PQnotifies</function> after each
7208   <xref linkend="libpq-PQgetResult"/> or <xref linkend="libpq-PQexec"/>, to
7209   see if any notifications came in during the processing of the command.
7210  </para>
7211
7212 </sect1>
7213
7214 <sect1 id="libpq-copy">
7215  <title>Functions Associated with the <command>COPY</command> Command</title>
7216
7217  <indexterm zone="libpq-copy">
7218   <primary>COPY</primary>
7219   <secondary>with libpq</secondary>
7220  </indexterm>
7221
7222  <para>
7223   The <command>COPY</command> command in
7224   <productname>PostgreSQL</productname> has options to read from or write
7225   to the network connection used by <application>libpq</application>.
7226   The functions described in this section allow applications to take
7227   advantage of this capability by supplying or consuming copied data.
7228  </para>
7229
7230  <para>
7231   The overall process is that the application first issues the SQL
7232   <command>COPY</command> command via <xref linkend="libpq-PQexec"/> or one
7233   of the equivalent functions.  The response to this (if there is no
7234   error in the command) will be a <structname>PGresult</structname> object bearing
7235   a status code of <literal>PGRES_COPY_OUT</literal> or
7236   <literal>PGRES_COPY_IN</literal> (depending on the specified copy
7237   direction).  The application should then use the functions of this
7238   section to receive or transmit data rows.  When the data transfer is
7239   complete, another <structname>PGresult</structname> object is returned to indicate
7240   success or failure of the transfer.  Its status will be
7241   <literal>PGRES_COMMAND_OK</literal> for success or
7242   <literal>PGRES_FATAL_ERROR</literal> if some problem was encountered.
7243   At this point further SQL commands can be issued via
7244   <xref linkend="libpq-PQexec"/>.  (It is not possible to execute other SQL
7245   commands using the same connection while the <command>COPY</command>
7246   operation is in progress.)
7247  </para>
7248
7249  <para>
7250   If a <command>COPY</command> command is issued via
7251   <xref linkend="libpq-PQexec"/> in a string that could contain additional
7252   commands, the application must continue fetching results via
7253   <xref linkend="libpq-PQgetResult"/> after completing the <command>COPY</command>
7254   sequence.  Only when <xref linkend="libpq-PQgetResult"/> returns
7255   <symbol>NULL</symbol> is it certain that the <xref linkend="libpq-PQexec"/>
7256   command string is done and it is safe to issue more commands.
7257  </para>
7258
7259  <para>
7260   The functions of this section should be executed only after obtaining
7261   a result status of <literal>PGRES_COPY_OUT</literal> or
7262   <literal>PGRES_COPY_IN</literal> from <xref linkend="libpq-PQexec"/> or
7263   <xref linkend="libpq-PQgetResult"/>.
7264  </para>
7265
7266  <para>
7267   A <structname>PGresult</structname> object bearing one of these status values
7268   carries some additional data about the <command>COPY</command> operation
7269   that is starting.  This additional data is available using functions
7270   that are also used in connection with query results:
7271
7272   <variablelist>
7273    <varlistentry id="libpq-PQnfields-1">
7274     <term><function>PQnfields</function><indexterm
7275     ><primary>PQnfields</primary><secondary>with COPY</secondary></indexterm></term>
7276
7277     <listitem>
7278      <para>
7279       Returns the number of columns (fields) to be copied.
7280      </para>
7281     </listitem>
7282    </varlistentry>
7283
7284    <varlistentry id="libpq-PQbinaryTuples-1">
7285     <term><function>PQbinaryTuples</function><indexterm
7286     ><primary>PQbinaryTuples</primary><secondary>with COPY</secondary></indexterm></term>
7287
7288     <listitem>
7289      <para>
7290       0 indicates the overall copy format is textual (rows separated by
7291       newlines, columns separated by separator characters, etc.).  1
7292       indicates the overall copy format is binary.  See <xref
7293       linkend="sql-copy"/> for more information.
7294      </para>
7295     </listitem>
7296    </varlistentry>
7297
7298    <varlistentry id="libpq-PQfformat-1">
7299     <term><function>PQfformat</function><indexterm
7300     ><primary>PQfformat</primary><secondary>with COPY</secondary></indexterm></term>
7301
7302     <listitem>
7303      <para>
7304       Returns the format code (0 for text, 1 for binary) associated with
7305       each column of the copy operation.  The per-column format codes
7306       will always be zero when the overall copy format is textual, but
7307       the binary format can support both text and binary columns.
7308       (However, as of the current implementation of <command>COPY</command>,
7309       only binary columns appear in a binary copy; so the per-column
7310       formats always match the overall format at present.)
7311      </para>
7312     </listitem>
7313    </varlistentry>
7314   </variablelist>
7315  </para>
7316
7317  <sect2 id="libpq-copy-send">
7318   <title>Functions for Sending <command>COPY</command> Data</title>
7319
7320   <para>
7321    These functions are used to send data during <literal>COPY FROM
7322    STDIN</literal>.  They will fail if called when the connection is not in
7323    <literal>COPY_IN</literal> state.
7324   </para>
7325
7326   <variablelist>
7327    <varlistentry id="libpq-PQputCopyData">
7328     <term><function>PQputCopyData</function><indexterm><primary>PQputCopyData</primary></indexterm></term>
7329
7330     <listitem>
7331      <para>
7332       Sends data to the server during <literal>COPY_IN</literal> state.
7333<synopsis>
7334int PQputCopyData(PGconn *conn,
7335                  const char *buffer,
7336                  int nbytes);
7337</synopsis>
7338      </para>
7339
7340      <para>
7341       Transmits the <command>COPY</command> data in the specified
7342       <parameter>buffer</parameter>, of length <parameter>nbytes</parameter>, to the server.
7343       The result is 1 if the data was queued, zero if it was not queued
7344       because of full buffers (this will only happen in nonblocking mode),
7345       or -1 if an error occurred.
7346       (Use <xref linkend="libpq-PQerrorMessage"/> to retrieve details if
7347       the return value is -1.  If the value is zero, wait for write-ready
7348       and try again.)
7349      </para>
7350
7351      <para>
7352       The application can divide the <command>COPY</command> data stream
7353       into buffer loads of any convenient size.  Buffer-load boundaries
7354       have no semantic significance when sending.  The contents of the
7355       data stream must match the data format expected by the
7356       <command>COPY</command> command; see <xref linkend="sql-copy"/> for details.
7357      </para>
7358     </listitem>
7359    </varlistentry>
7360
7361    <varlistentry id="libpq-PQputCopyEnd">
7362     <term><function>PQputCopyEnd</function><indexterm><primary>PQputCopyEnd</primary></indexterm></term>
7363
7364     <listitem>
7365      <para>
7366       Sends end-of-data indication to the server during <literal>COPY_IN</literal> state.
7367<synopsis>
7368int PQputCopyEnd(PGconn *conn,
7369                 const char *errormsg);
7370</synopsis>
7371      </para>
7372
7373      <para>
7374       Ends the <literal>COPY_IN</literal> operation successfully if
7375       <parameter>errormsg</parameter> is <symbol>NULL</symbol>.  If
7376       <parameter>errormsg</parameter> is not <symbol>NULL</symbol> then the
7377       <command>COPY</command> is forced to fail, with the string pointed to by
7378       <parameter>errormsg</parameter> used as the error message.  (One should not
7379       assume that this exact error message will come back from the server,
7380       however, as the server might have already failed the
7381       <command>COPY</command> for its own reasons.)
7382      </para>
7383
7384      <para>
7385       The result is 1 if the termination message was sent; or in
7386       nonblocking mode, this may only indicate that the termination
7387       message was successfully queued.  (In nonblocking mode, to be
7388       certain that the data has been sent, you should next wait for
7389       write-ready and call <xref linkend="libpq-PQflush"/>, repeating until it
7390       returns zero.)  Zero indicates that the function could not queue
7391       the termination message because of full buffers; this will only
7392       happen in nonblocking mode.  (In this case, wait for
7393       write-ready and try the <xref linkend="libpq-PQputCopyEnd"/> call
7394       again.)  If a hard error occurs, -1 is returned; you can use
7395       <xref linkend="libpq-PQerrorMessage"/> to retrieve details.
7396      </para>
7397
7398      <para>
7399       After successfully calling <xref linkend="libpq-PQputCopyEnd"/>, call
7400       <xref linkend="libpq-PQgetResult"/> to obtain the final result status of the
7401       <command>COPY</command> command.  One can wait for this result to be
7402       available in the usual way.  Then return to normal operation.
7403      </para>
7404     </listitem>
7405    </varlistentry>
7406   </variablelist>
7407
7408  </sect2>
7409
7410  <sect2 id="libpq-copy-receive">
7411   <title>Functions for Receiving <command>COPY</command> Data</title>
7412
7413   <para>
7414    These functions are used to receive data during <literal>COPY TO
7415    STDOUT</literal>.  They will fail if called when the connection is not in
7416    <literal>COPY_OUT</literal> state.
7417   </para>
7418
7419   <variablelist>
7420    <varlistentry id="libpq-PQgetCopyData">
7421     <term><function>PQgetCopyData</function><indexterm><primary>PQgetCopyData</primary></indexterm></term>
7422
7423     <listitem>
7424      <para>
7425       Receives data from the server during <literal>COPY_OUT</literal> state.
7426<synopsis>
7427int PQgetCopyData(PGconn *conn,
7428                  char **buffer,
7429                  int async);
7430</synopsis>
7431      </para>
7432
7433      <para>
7434       Attempts to obtain another row of data from the server during a
7435       <command>COPY</command>.  Data is always returned one data row at
7436       a time; if only a partial row is available, it is not returned.
7437       Successful return of a data row involves allocating a chunk of
7438       memory to hold the data.  The <parameter>buffer</parameter> parameter must
7439       be non-<symbol>NULL</symbol>.  <parameter>*buffer</parameter> is set to
7440       point to the allocated memory, or to <symbol>NULL</symbol> in cases
7441       where no buffer is returned.  A non-<symbol>NULL</symbol> result
7442       buffer should be freed using <xref linkend="libpq-PQfreemem"/> when no longer
7443       needed.
7444      </para>
7445
7446      <para>
7447       When a row is successfully returned, the return value is the number
7448       of data bytes in the row (this will always be greater than zero).
7449       The returned string is always null-terminated, though this is
7450       probably only useful for textual <command>COPY</command>.  A result
7451       of zero indicates that the <command>COPY</command> is still in
7452       progress, but no row is yet available (this is only possible when
7453       <parameter>async</parameter> is true).  A result of -1 indicates that the
7454       <command>COPY</command> is done.  A result of -2 indicates that an
7455       error occurred (consult <xref linkend="libpq-PQerrorMessage"/> for the reason).
7456      </para>
7457
7458      <para>
7459       When <parameter>async</parameter> is true (not zero),
7460       <xref linkend="libpq-PQgetCopyData"/> will not block waiting for input; it
7461       will return zero if the <command>COPY</command> is still in progress
7462       but no complete row is available.  (In this case wait for read-ready
7463       and then call <xref linkend="libpq-PQconsumeInput"/> before calling
7464       <xref linkend="libpq-PQgetCopyData"/> again.)  When <parameter>async</parameter> is
7465       false (zero), <xref linkend="libpq-PQgetCopyData"/> will block until data is
7466       available or the operation completes.
7467      </para>
7468
7469      <para>
7470       After <xref linkend="libpq-PQgetCopyData"/> returns -1, call
7471       <xref linkend="libpq-PQgetResult"/> to obtain the final result status of the
7472       <command>COPY</command> command.  One can wait for this result to be
7473       available in the usual way.  Then return to normal operation.
7474      </para>
7475     </listitem>
7476    </varlistentry>
7477   </variablelist>
7478
7479  </sect2>
7480
7481  <sect2 id="libpq-copy-deprecated">
7482   <title>Obsolete Functions for <command>COPY</command></title>
7483
7484   <para>
7485    These functions represent older methods of handling <command>COPY</command>.
7486    Although they still work, they are deprecated due to poor error handling,
7487    inconvenient methods of detecting end-of-data, and lack of support for binary
7488    or nonblocking transfers.
7489   </para>
7490
7491   <variablelist>
7492    <varlistentry id="libpq-PQgetline">
7493     <term><function>PQgetline</function><indexterm><primary>PQgetline</primary></indexterm></term>
7494
7495     <listitem>
7496      <para>
7497       Reads  a  newline-terminated  line  of  characters (transmitted
7498       by the server) into a buffer string of size <parameter>length</parameter>.
7499<synopsis>
7500int PQgetline(PGconn *conn,
7501              char *buffer,
7502              int length);
7503</synopsis>
7504      </para>
7505
7506      <para>
7507       This function copies up to <parameter>length</parameter>-1 characters into
7508       the buffer and converts the terminating newline into a zero byte.
7509       <xref linkend="libpq-PQgetline"/> returns <symbol>EOF</symbol> at the
7510       end of input, 0 if the entire line has been read, and 1 if the
7511       buffer is full but the terminating newline has not yet been read.
7512       </para>
7513       <para>
7514       Note that the application must check to see if a new line consists
7515       of  the  two characters  <literal>\.</literal>, which  indicates
7516       that the server has finished sending the results  of  the
7517       <command>COPY</command> command.  If  the  application might receive
7518       lines that are more than <parameter>length</parameter>-1  characters  long,
7519       care is needed to be sure it recognizes the <literal>\.</literal>
7520       line correctly (and does not, for example, mistake the end of a
7521       long data line for a terminator line).
7522      </para>
7523     </listitem>
7524    </varlistentry>
7525
7526    <varlistentry id="libpq-PQgetlineAsync">
7527     <term><function>PQgetlineAsync</function><indexterm><primary>PQgetlineAsync</primary></indexterm></term>
7528
7529     <listitem>
7530      <para>
7531       Reads a row of <command>COPY</command> data (transmitted  by the
7532       server) into a buffer without blocking.
7533<synopsis>
7534int PQgetlineAsync(PGconn *conn,
7535                   char *buffer,
7536                   int bufsize);
7537</synopsis>
7538      </para>
7539
7540      <para>
7541       This function is similar to <xref linkend="libpq-PQgetline"/>, but it can be used
7542       by applications
7543       that must read <command>COPY</command> data asynchronously, that is, without blocking.
7544       Having issued the <command>COPY</command> command and gotten a <literal>PGRES_COPY_OUT</literal>
7545       response, the
7546       application should call <xref linkend="libpq-PQconsumeInput"/> and
7547       <xref linkend="libpq-PQgetlineAsync"/> until the
7548       end-of-data signal is detected.
7549       </para>
7550       <para>
7551       Unlike <xref linkend="libpq-PQgetline"/>, this function takes
7552       responsibility for detecting end-of-data.
7553      </para>
7554
7555      <para>
7556       On each call, <xref linkend="libpq-PQgetlineAsync"/> will return data if a
7557       complete data row is available in <application>libpq</application>'s input buffer.
7558       Otherwise, no data is returned until the rest of the row arrives.
7559       The function returns -1 if the end-of-copy-data marker has been recognized,
7560       or 0 if no data is available, or a positive number giving the number of
7561       bytes of data returned.  If -1 is returned, the caller must next call
7562       <xref linkend="libpq-PQendcopy"/>, and then return to normal processing.
7563      </para>
7564
7565      <para>
7566       The data returned will not extend beyond a data-row boundary.  If possible
7567       a whole row will be returned at one time.  But if the buffer offered by
7568       the caller is too small to hold a row sent by the server, then a partial
7569       data row will be returned.  With textual data this can be detected by testing
7570       whether the last returned byte is <literal>\n</literal> or not.  (In a binary
7571       <command>COPY</command>, actual parsing of the <command>COPY</command> data format will be needed to make the
7572       equivalent determination.)
7573       The returned string is not null-terminated.  (If you want to add a
7574       terminating null, be sure to pass a <parameter>bufsize</parameter> one smaller
7575       than the room actually available.)
7576      </para>
7577     </listitem>
7578    </varlistentry>
7579
7580    <varlistentry id="libpq-PQputline">
7581     <term><function>PQputline</function><indexterm><primary>PQputline</primary></indexterm></term>
7582
7583     <listitem>
7584      <para>
7585       Sends  a  null-terminated  string  to  the server.  Returns 0 if
7586       OK and <symbol>EOF</symbol> if unable to send the string.
7587<synopsis>
7588int PQputline(PGconn *conn,
7589              const char *string);
7590</synopsis>
7591      </para>
7592
7593      <para>
7594       The <command>COPY</command> data stream sent by a series of calls
7595       to <xref linkend="libpq-PQputline"/> has the same format as that
7596       returned by <xref linkend="libpq-PQgetlineAsync"/>, except that
7597       applications are not obliged to send exactly one data row per
7598       <xref linkend="libpq-PQputline"/> call; it is okay to send a partial
7599       line or multiple lines per call.
7600      </para>
7601
7602      <note>
7603       <para>
7604        Before <productname>PostgreSQL</productname> protocol 3.0, it was necessary
7605        for the application to explicitly send the two characters
7606        <literal>\.</literal> as a final line to indicate to the server that it had
7607        finished sending <command>COPY</command> data.  While this still works, it is deprecated and the
7608        special meaning of <literal>\.</literal> can be expected to be removed in a
7609        future release.  (It already will misbehave in <literal>CSV</literal>
7610        mode.)  It is sufficient to call <xref linkend="libpq-PQendcopy"/>
7611        after having sent the actual data.
7612       </para>
7613      </note>
7614     </listitem>
7615    </varlistentry>
7616
7617    <varlistentry id="libpq-PQputnbytes">
7618     <term><function>PQputnbytes</function><indexterm><primary>PQputnbytes</primary></indexterm></term>
7619
7620     <listitem>
7621      <para>
7622       Sends  a  non-null-terminated  string  to  the server.  Returns
7623       0 if OK and <symbol>EOF</symbol> if unable to send the string.
7624<synopsis>
7625int PQputnbytes(PGconn *conn,
7626                const char *buffer,
7627                int nbytes);
7628</synopsis>
7629      </para>
7630
7631      <para>
7632       This is exactly like <xref linkend="libpq-PQputline"/>, except that the data
7633       buffer need not be null-terminated since the number of bytes to send is
7634       specified directly.  Use this procedure when sending binary data.
7635      </para>
7636     </listitem>
7637    </varlistentry>
7638
7639    <varlistentry id="libpq-PQendcopy">
7640     <term><function>PQendcopy</function><indexterm><primary>PQendcopy</primary></indexterm></term>
7641
7642     <listitem>
7643      <para>
7644       Synchronizes with the server.
7645<synopsis>
7646int PQendcopy(PGconn *conn);
7647</synopsis>
7648       This function waits until the  server  has  finished  the copying.
7649       It should either be issued when the  last  string  has  been sent
7650       to  the  server using <xref linkend="libpq-PQputline"/> or when the
7651       last string has been  received  from  the  server using
7652       <function>PQgetline</function>.  It must be issued or the server
7653       will get <quote>out of sync</quote> with  the client.   Upon return
7654       from this function, the server is ready to receive the next SQL
7655       command.  The return value is 0  on  successful  completion,
7656       nonzero otherwise.  (Use <xref linkend="libpq-PQerrorMessage"/> to
7657       retrieve details if the return value is nonzero.)
7658      </para>
7659
7660      <para>
7661       When using <xref linkend="libpq-PQgetResult"/>, the application should
7662       respond to a <literal>PGRES_COPY_OUT</literal> result by executing
7663       <xref linkend="libpq-PQgetline"/> repeatedly, followed by
7664       <xref linkend="libpq-PQendcopy"/> after the terminator line is seen.
7665       It should then return to the <xref linkend="libpq-PQgetResult"/> loop
7666       until <xref linkend="libpq-PQgetResult"/> returns a null pointer.
7667       Similarly a <literal>PGRES_COPY_IN</literal> result is processed
7668       by a series of <xref linkend="libpq-PQputline"/> calls followed by
7669       <xref linkend="libpq-PQendcopy"/>, then return to the
7670       <xref linkend="libpq-PQgetResult"/> loop.  This arrangement will
7671       ensure that a <command>COPY</command> command embedded in a series
7672       of <acronym>SQL</acronym> commands will be executed correctly.
7673      </para>
7674
7675      <para>
7676       Older applications are likely to submit a <command>COPY</command>
7677       via <xref linkend="libpq-PQexec"/> and assume that the transaction
7678       is done after <xref linkend="libpq-PQendcopy"/>.  This will work
7679       correctly only if the <command>COPY</command> is the only
7680       <acronym>SQL</acronym> command in the command string.
7681      </para>
7682     </listitem>
7683    </varlistentry>
7684   </variablelist>
7685
7686  </sect2>
7687
7688 </sect1>
7689
7690 <sect1 id="libpq-control">
7691  <title>Control Functions</title>
7692
7693  <para>
7694   These functions control miscellaneous details of <application>libpq</application>'s
7695   behavior.
7696  </para>
7697
7698  <variablelist>
7699   <varlistentry id="libpq-PQclientEncoding">
7700    <term><function>PQclientEncoding</function><indexterm><primary>PQclientEncoding</primary></indexterm></term>
7701
7702    <listitem>
7703     <para>
7704      Returns the client encoding.
7705<synopsis>
7706int PQclientEncoding(const PGconn *<replaceable>conn</replaceable>);
7707</synopsis>
7708
7709      Note that it returns the encoding ID, not a symbolic string
7710      such as <literal>EUC_JP</literal>. If unsuccessful, it returns -1.
7711      To convert an encoding ID to an encoding name, you
7712      can use:
7713
7714<synopsis>
7715char *pg_encoding_to_char(int <replaceable>encoding_id</replaceable>);
7716</synopsis>
7717     </para>
7718    </listitem>
7719   </varlistentry>
7720
7721   <varlistentry id="libpq-PQsetClientEncoding">
7722    <term><function>PQsetClientEncoding</function><indexterm><primary>PQsetClientEncoding</primary></indexterm></term>
7723
7724    <listitem>
7725     <para>
7726      Sets the client encoding.
7727<synopsis>
7728int PQsetClientEncoding(PGconn *<replaceable>conn</replaceable>, const char *<replaceable>encoding</replaceable>);
7729</synopsis>
7730
7731      <replaceable>conn</replaceable> is a connection to the server,
7732      and <replaceable>encoding</replaceable> is the encoding you want to
7733      use. If the function successfully sets the encoding, it returns 0,
7734      otherwise -1. The current encoding for this connection can be
7735      determined by using <xref linkend="libpq-PQclientEncoding"/>.
7736     </para>
7737    </listitem>
7738   </varlistentry>
7739
7740   <varlistentry id="libpq-PQsetErrorVerbosity">
7741    <term><function>PQsetErrorVerbosity</function><indexterm><primary>PQsetErrorVerbosity</primary></indexterm></term>
7742
7743    <listitem>
7744     <para>
7745      Determines the verbosity of messages returned by
7746      <xref linkend="libpq-PQerrorMessage"/> and <xref linkend="libpq-PQresultErrorMessage"/>.
7747<synopsis>
7748typedef enum
7749{
7750    PQERRORS_TERSE,
7751    PQERRORS_DEFAULT,
7752    PQERRORS_VERBOSE,
7753    PQERRORS_SQLSTATE
7754} PGVerbosity;
7755
7756PGVerbosity PQsetErrorVerbosity(PGconn *conn, PGVerbosity verbosity);
7757</synopsis>
7758
7759      <xref linkend="libpq-PQsetErrorVerbosity"/> sets the verbosity mode,
7760      returning the connection's previous setting.
7761      In <firstterm>TERSE</firstterm> mode, returned messages include
7762      severity, primary text, and position only; this will normally fit on a
7763      single line.  The <firstterm>DEFAULT</firstterm> mode produces messages
7764      that include the above plus any detail, hint, or context fields (these
7765      might span multiple lines).  The <firstterm>VERBOSE</firstterm> mode
7766      includes all available fields.  The <firstterm>SQLSTATE</firstterm>
7767      mode includes only the error severity and the <symbol>SQLSTATE</symbol>
7768      error code, if one is available (if not, the output is like
7769      <firstterm>TERSE</firstterm> mode).
7770     </para>
7771
7772     <para>
7773      Changing the verbosity setting does not affect the messages available
7774      from already-existing <structname>PGresult</structname> objects, only
7775      subsequently-created ones.
7776      (But see <xref linkend="libpq-PQresultVerboseErrorMessage"/> if you
7777      want to print a previous error with a different verbosity.)
7778     </para>
7779    </listitem>
7780   </varlistentry>
7781
7782   <varlistentry id="libpq-PQsetErrorContextVisibility">
7783    <term><function>PQsetErrorContextVisibility</function><indexterm><primary>PQsetErrorContextVisibility</primary></indexterm></term>
7784
7785    <listitem>
7786     <para>
7787      Determines the handling of <literal>CONTEXT</literal> fields in messages
7788      returned by <xref linkend="libpq-PQerrorMessage"/>
7789      and <xref linkend="libpq-PQresultErrorMessage"/>.
7790<synopsis>
7791typedef enum
7792{
7793    PQSHOW_CONTEXT_NEVER,
7794    PQSHOW_CONTEXT_ERRORS,
7795    PQSHOW_CONTEXT_ALWAYS
7796} PGContextVisibility;
7797
7798PGContextVisibility PQsetErrorContextVisibility(PGconn *conn, PGContextVisibility show_context);
7799</synopsis>
7800
7801      <xref linkend="libpq-PQsetErrorContextVisibility"/> sets the context display mode,
7802      returning the connection's previous setting.  This mode controls
7803      whether the <literal>CONTEXT</literal> field is included in messages.
7804      The <firstterm>NEVER</firstterm> mode
7805      never includes <literal>CONTEXT</literal>, while <firstterm>ALWAYS</firstterm> always
7806      includes it if available.  In <firstterm>ERRORS</firstterm> mode (the
7807      default), <literal>CONTEXT</literal> fields are included only in error
7808      messages, not in notices and warnings.
7809      (However, if the verbosity setting is <firstterm>TERSE</firstterm>
7810      or <firstterm>SQLSTATE</firstterm>, <literal>CONTEXT</literal> fields
7811      are omitted regardless of the context display mode.)
7812     </para>
7813
7814     <para>
7815      Changing this mode does not
7816      affect the messages available from
7817      already-existing <structname>PGresult</structname> objects, only
7818      subsequently-created ones.
7819      (But see <xref linkend="libpq-PQresultVerboseErrorMessage"/> if you
7820      want to print a previous error with a different display mode.)
7821     </para>
7822    </listitem>
7823   </varlistentry>
7824
7825   <varlistentry id="libpq-PQtrace">
7826    <term><function>PQtrace</function><indexterm><primary>PQtrace</primary></indexterm></term>
7827
7828    <listitem>
7829     <para>
7830      Enables tracing of the client/server communication to a debugging file
7831      stream.
7832<synopsis>
7833void PQtrace(PGconn *conn, FILE *stream);
7834</synopsis>
7835     </para>
7836
7837     <para>
7838      Each line consists of: an optional timestamp, a direction indicator
7839      (<literal>F</literal> for messages from client to server
7840      or <literal>B</literal> for messages from server to client),
7841      message length, message type, and message contents.
7842      Non-message contents fields (timestamp, direction, length and message type)
7843      are separated by a tab. Message contents are separated by a space.
7844      Protocol strings are enclosed in double quotes, while strings used as data
7845      values are enclosed in single quotes.  Non-printable chars are printed as
7846      hexadecimal escapes.
7847      Further message-type-specific detail can be found in
7848      <xref linkend="protocol-message-formats"/>.
7849     </para>
7850
7851     <note>
7852      <para>
7853       On Windows, if the <application>libpq</application> library and an application are
7854       compiled with different flags, this function call will crash the
7855       application because the internal representation of the <literal>FILE</literal>
7856       pointers differ.  Specifically, multithreaded/single-threaded,
7857       release/debug, and static/dynamic flags should be the same for the
7858       library and all applications using that library.
7859      </para>
7860     </note>
7861
7862    </listitem>
7863   </varlistentry>
7864
7865   <varlistentry id="libpq-PQsetTraceFlags">
7866    <term><function>PQsetTraceFlags</function><indexterm><primary>PQsetTraceFlags</primary></indexterm></term>
7867
7868    <listitem>
7869     <para>
7870      Controls the tracing behavior of client/server communication.
7871<synopsis>
7872void PQsetTraceFlags(PGconn *conn, int flags);
7873</synopsis>
7874     </para>
7875
7876     <para>
7877      <literal>flags</literal> contains flag bits describing the operating mode
7878      of tracing.
7879      If <literal>flags</literal> contains <literal>PQTRACE_SUPPRESS_TIMESTAMPS</literal>,
7880      then the timestamp is not included when printing each message.
7881      If <literal>flags</literal> contains <literal>PQTRACE_REGRESS_MODE</literal>,
7882      then some fields are redacted when printing each message, such as object
7883      OIDs, to make the output more convenient to use in testing frameworks.
7884      This function must be called after calling <function>PQtrace</function>.
7885     </para>
7886
7887    </listitem>
7888   </varlistentry>
7889
7890   <varlistentry id="libpq-PQuntrace">
7891    <term><function>PQuntrace</function><indexterm><primary>PQuntrace</primary></indexterm></term>
7892
7893    <listitem>
7894     <para>
7895      Disables tracing started by <xref linkend="libpq-PQtrace"/>.
7896<synopsis>
7897void PQuntrace(PGconn *conn);
7898</synopsis>
7899     </para>
7900    </listitem>
7901   </varlistentry>
7902  </variablelist>
7903
7904 </sect1>
7905
7906 <sect1 id="libpq-misc">
7907  <title>Miscellaneous Functions</title>
7908
7909  <para>
7910   As always, there are some functions that just don't fit anywhere.
7911  </para>
7912
7913  <variablelist>
7914   <varlistentry id="libpq-PQfreemem">
7915    <term><function>PQfreemem</function><indexterm><primary>PQfreemem</primary></indexterm></term>
7916
7917    <listitem>
7918     <para>
7919      Frees memory allocated by <application>libpq</application>.
7920<synopsis>
7921void PQfreemem(void *ptr);
7922</synopsis>
7923     </para>
7924
7925     <para>
7926      Frees memory allocated by <application>libpq</application>, particularly
7927      <xref linkend="libpq-PQescapeByteaConn"/>,
7928      <xref linkend="libpq-PQescapeBytea"/>,
7929      <xref linkend="libpq-PQunescapeBytea"/>,
7930      and <function>PQnotifies</function>.
7931      It is particularly important that this function, rather than
7932      <function>free()</function>, be used on Microsoft Windows.  This is because
7933      allocating memory in a DLL and releasing it in the application works
7934      only if multithreaded/single-threaded, release/debug, and static/dynamic
7935      flags are the same for the DLL and the application.  On non-Microsoft
7936      Windows platforms, this function is the same as the standard library
7937      function <function>free()</function>.
7938     </para>
7939    </listitem>
7940   </varlistentry>
7941
7942   <varlistentry id="libpq-PQconninfoFree">
7943    <term><function>PQconninfoFree</function><indexterm><primary>PQconninfoFree</primary></indexterm></term>
7944
7945    <listitem>
7946     <para>
7947      Frees the data structures allocated by
7948      <xref linkend="libpq-PQconndefaults"/> or <xref linkend="libpq-PQconninfoParse"/>.
7949<synopsis>
7950void PQconninfoFree(PQconninfoOption *connOptions);
7951</synopsis>
7952      If the argument is a <symbol>NULL</symbol> pointer, no operation is
7953      performed.
7954     </para>
7955
7956     <para>
7957      A simple <xref linkend="libpq-PQfreemem"/> will not do for this, since
7958      the array contains references to subsidiary strings.
7959     </para>
7960    </listitem>
7961   </varlistentry>
7962
7963   <varlistentry id="libpq-PQencryptPasswordConn">
7964    <term><function>PQencryptPasswordConn</function><indexterm><primary>PQencryptPasswordConn</primary></indexterm></term>
7965
7966    <listitem>
7967     <para>
7968      Prepares the encrypted form of a <productname>PostgreSQL</productname> password.
7969<synopsis>
7970char *PQencryptPasswordConn(PGconn *conn, const char *passwd, const char *user, const char *algorithm);
7971</synopsis>
7972      This function is intended to be used by client applications that
7973      wish to send commands like <literal>ALTER USER joe PASSWORD
7974      'pwd'</literal>.  It is good practice not to send the original cleartext
7975      password in such a command, because it might be exposed in command
7976      logs, activity displays, and so on.  Instead, use this function to
7977      convert the password to encrypted form before it is sent.
7978     </para>
7979
7980     <para>
7981      The <parameter>passwd</parameter> and <parameter>user</parameter> arguments
7982      are the cleartext password, and the SQL name of the user it is for.
7983      <parameter>algorithm</parameter> specifies the encryption algorithm
7984      to use to encrypt the password. Currently supported algorithms are
7985      <literal>md5</literal> and <literal>scram-sha-256</literal> (<literal>on</literal> and
7986      <literal>off</literal> are also accepted as aliases for <literal>md5</literal>, for
7987      compatibility with older server versions). Note that support for
7988      <literal>scram-sha-256</literal> was introduced in <productname>PostgreSQL</productname>
7989      version 10, and will not work correctly with older server versions. If
7990      <parameter>algorithm</parameter> is <symbol>NULL</symbol>, this function will query
7991      the server for the current value of the
7992      <xref linkend="guc-password-encryption"/> setting. That can block, and
7993      will fail if the current transaction is aborted, or if the connection
7994      is busy executing another query. If you wish to use the default
7995      algorithm for the server but want to avoid blocking, query
7996      <varname>password_encryption</varname> yourself before calling
7997      <xref linkend="libpq-PQencryptPasswordConn"/>, and pass that value as the
7998      <parameter>algorithm</parameter>.
7999     </para>
8000
8001     <para>
8002      The return value is a string allocated by <function>malloc</function>.
8003      The caller can assume the string doesn't contain any special characters
8004      that would require escaping.  Use <xref linkend="libpq-PQfreemem"/> to free the
8005      result when done with it. On error, returns <symbol>NULL</symbol>, and
8006      a suitable message is stored in the connection object.
8007     </para>
8008
8009    </listitem>
8010   </varlistentry>
8011
8012   <varlistentry id="libpq-PQchangePassword">
8013    <term><function>PQchangePassword</function><indexterm><primary>PQchangePassword</primary></indexterm></term>
8014
8015    <listitem>
8016     <para>
8017      Changes a <productname>PostgreSQL</productname> password.
8018<synopsis>
8019PGresult *PQchangePassword(PGconn *conn, const char *user, const char *passwd);
8020</synopsis>
8021      This function uses <function>PQencryptPasswordConn</function>
8022      to build and execute the command <literal>ALTER USER ... PASSWORD
8023      '...'</literal>, thereby changing the user's password. It exists for
8024      the same reason as <function>PQencryptPasswordConn</function>, but
8025      is more convenient as it both builds and runs the command for you.
8026      <xref linkend="libpq-PQencryptPasswordConn"/> is passed a
8027      <symbol>NULL</symbol> for the algorithm argument, hence encryption is
8028      done according to the server's <xref linkend="guc-password-encryption"/>
8029      setting.
8030     </para>
8031
8032     <para>
8033      The <parameter>user</parameter> and <parameter>passwd</parameter> arguments
8034      are the SQL name of the target user, and the new cleartext password.
8035     </para>
8036
8037     <para>
8038      Returns a <structname>PGresult</structname> pointer representing
8039      the result of the <literal>ALTER USER</literal> command, or
8040      a null pointer if the routine failed before issuing any command.
8041      The <xref linkend="libpq-PQresultStatus"/> function should be called
8042      to check the return value for any errors (including the value of a null
8043      pointer, in which case it will return
8044      <symbol>PGRES_FATAL_ERROR</symbol>). Use
8045      <xref linkend="libpq-PQerrorMessage"/> to get more information about
8046      such errors.
8047     </para>
8048    </listitem>
8049   </varlistentry>
8050
8051   <varlistentry id="libpq-PQencryptPassword">
8052    <term><function>PQencryptPassword</function><indexterm><primary>PQencryptPassword</primary></indexterm></term>
8053
8054    <listitem>
8055     <para>
8056      Prepares the md5-encrypted form of a <productname>PostgreSQL</productname> password.
8057<synopsis>
8058char *PQencryptPassword(const char *passwd, const char *user);
8059</synopsis>
8060      <xref linkend="libpq-PQencryptPassword"/> is an older, deprecated version of
8061      <xref linkend="libpq-PQencryptPasswordConn"/>. The difference is that
8062      <xref linkend="libpq-PQencryptPassword"/> does not
8063      require a connection object, and <literal>md5</literal> is always used as the
8064      encryption algorithm.
8065     </para>
8066    </listitem>
8067   </varlistentry>
8068
8069   <varlistentry id="libpq-PQmakeEmptyPGresult">
8070    <term><function>PQmakeEmptyPGresult</function><indexterm><primary>PQmakeEmptyPGresult</primary></indexterm></term>
8071
8072    <listitem>
8073     <para>
8074      Constructs an empty <structname>PGresult</structname> object with the given status.
8075<synopsis>
8076PGresult *PQmakeEmptyPGresult(PGconn *conn, ExecStatusType status);
8077</synopsis>
8078     </para>
8079
8080     <para>
8081      This is <application>libpq</application>'s internal function to allocate and
8082      initialize an empty <structname>PGresult</structname> object.  This
8083      function returns <symbol>NULL</symbol> if memory could not be allocated. It is
8084      exported because some applications find it useful to generate result
8085      objects (particularly objects with error status) themselves.  If
8086      <parameter>conn</parameter> is not null and <parameter>status</parameter>
8087      indicates an error, the current error message of the specified
8088      connection is copied into the <structname>PGresult</structname>.
8089      Also, if <parameter>conn</parameter> is not null, any event procedures
8090      registered in the connection are copied into the
8091      <structname>PGresult</structname>.  (They do not get
8092      <literal>PGEVT_RESULTCREATE</literal> calls, but see
8093      <xref linkend="libpq-PQfireResultCreateEvents"/>.)
8094      Note that <xref linkend="libpq-PQclear"/> should eventually be called
8095      on the object, just as with a <structname>PGresult</structname>
8096      returned by <application>libpq</application> itself.
8097     </para>
8098    </listitem>
8099   </varlistentry>
8100
8101   <varlistentry id="libpq-PQfireResultCreateEvents">
8102    <term><function>PQfireResultCreateEvents</function><indexterm><primary>PQfireResultCreateEvents</primary></indexterm></term>
8103    <listitem>
8104     <para>
8105      Fires a <literal>PGEVT_RESULTCREATE</literal> event (see <xref
8106      linkend="libpq-events"/>) for each event procedure registered in the
8107      <structname>PGresult</structname> object.  Returns non-zero for success,
8108      zero if any event procedure fails.
8109
8110<synopsis>
8111int PQfireResultCreateEvents(PGconn *conn, PGresult *res);
8112</synopsis>
8113     </para>
8114
8115     <para>
8116      The <literal>conn</literal> argument is passed through to event procedures
8117      but not used directly.  It can be <symbol>NULL</symbol> if the event
8118      procedures won't use it.
8119     </para>
8120
8121     <para>
8122      Event procedures that have already received a
8123      <literal>PGEVT_RESULTCREATE</literal> or <literal>PGEVT_RESULTCOPY</literal> event
8124      for this object are not fired again.
8125     </para>
8126
8127     <para>
8128      The main reason that this function is separate from
8129      <xref linkend="libpq-PQmakeEmptyPGresult"/> is that it is often appropriate
8130      to create a <structname>PGresult</structname> and fill it with data
8131      before invoking the event procedures.
8132     </para>
8133    </listitem>
8134   </varlistentry>
8135
8136   <varlistentry id="libpq-PQcopyResult">
8137    <term><function>PQcopyResult</function><indexterm><primary>PQcopyResult</primary></indexterm></term>
8138
8139    <listitem>
8140     <para>
8141      Makes a copy of a <structname>PGresult</structname> object.  The copy is
8142      not linked to the source result in any way and
8143      <xref linkend="libpq-PQclear"/> must be called when the copy is no longer
8144      needed.  If the function fails, <symbol>NULL</symbol> is returned.
8145
8146<synopsis>
8147PGresult *PQcopyResult(const PGresult *src, int flags);
8148</synopsis>
8149     </para>
8150
8151     <para>
8152      This is not intended to make an exact copy.  The returned result is
8153      always put into <literal>PGRES_TUPLES_OK</literal> status, and does not
8154      copy any error message in the source.  (It does copy the command status
8155      string, however.)  The <parameter>flags</parameter> argument determines
8156      what else is copied.  It is a bitwise OR of several flags.
8157      <literal>PG_COPYRES_ATTRS</literal> specifies copying the source
8158      result's attributes (column definitions).
8159      <literal>PG_COPYRES_TUPLES</literal> specifies copying the source
8160      result's tuples.  (This implies copying the attributes, too.)
8161      <literal>PG_COPYRES_NOTICEHOOKS</literal> specifies
8162      copying the source result's notify hooks.
8163      <literal>PG_COPYRES_EVENTS</literal> specifies copying the source
8164      result's events.  (But any instance data associated with the source
8165      is not copied.)
8166      The event procedures receive <literal>PGEVT_RESULTCOPY</literal> events.
8167     </para>
8168    </listitem>
8169   </varlistentry>
8170
8171   <varlistentry id="libpq-PQsetResultAttrs">
8172    <term><function>PQsetResultAttrs</function><indexterm><primary>PQsetResultAttrs</primary></indexterm></term>
8173
8174    <listitem>
8175     <para>
8176      Sets the attributes of a <structname>PGresult</structname> object.
8177<synopsis>
8178int PQsetResultAttrs(PGresult *res, int numAttributes, PGresAttDesc *attDescs);
8179</synopsis>
8180     </para>
8181
8182     <para>
8183      The provided <parameter>attDescs</parameter> are copied into the result.
8184      If the <parameter>attDescs</parameter> pointer is <symbol>NULL</symbol> or
8185      <parameter>numAttributes</parameter> is less than one, the request is
8186      ignored and the function succeeds.  If <parameter>res</parameter>
8187      already contains attributes, the function will fail.  If the function
8188      fails, the return value is zero.  If the function succeeds, the return
8189      value is non-zero.
8190     </para>
8191    </listitem>
8192   </varlistentry>
8193
8194   <varlistentry id="libpq-PQsetvalue">
8195    <term><function>PQsetvalue</function><indexterm><primary>PQsetvalue</primary></indexterm></term>
8196
8197    <listitem>
8198     <para>
8199      Sets a tuple field value of a <structname>PGresult</structname> object.
8200<synopsis>
8201int PQsetvalue(PGresult *res, int tup_num, int field_num, char *value, int len);
8202</synopsis>
8203     </para>
8204
8205     <para>
8206      The function will automatically grow the result's internal tuples array
8207      as needed.  However, the <parameter>tup_num</parameter> argument must be
8208      less than or equal to <xref linkend="libpq-PQntuples"/>, meaning this
8209      function can only grow the tuples array one tuple at a time.  But any
8210      field of any existing tuple can be modified in any order.  If a value at
8211      <parameter>field_num</parameter> already exists, it will be overwritten.
8212      If <parameter>len</parameter> is -1 or
8213      <parameter>value</parameter> is <symbol>NULL</symbol>, the field value
8214      will be set to an SQL null value.  The
8215      <parameter>value</parameter> is copied into the result's private storage,
8216      thus is no longer needed after the function
8217      returns.  If the function fails, the return value is zero.  If the
8218      function succeeds, the return value is non-zero.
8219     </para>
8220    </listitem>
8221   </varlistentry>
8222
8223   <varlistentry id="libpq-PQresultAlloc">
8224    <term><function>PQresultAlloc</function><indexterm><primary>PQresultAlloc</primary></indexterm></term>
8225
8226    <listitem>
8227     <para>
8228      Allocate subsidiary storage for a <structname>PGresult</structname> object.
8229<synopsis>
8230void *PQresultAlloc(PGresult *res, size_t nBytes);
8231</synopsis>
8232     </para>
8233
8234     <para>
8235      Any memory allocated with this function will be freed when
8236      <parameter>res</parameter> is cleared.  If the function fails,
8237      the return value is <symbol>NULL</symbol>.  The result is
8238      guaranteed to be adequately aligned for any type of data,
8239      just as for <function>malloc</function>.
8240     </para>
8241    </listitem>
8242   </varlistentry>
8243
8244   <varlistentry id="libpq-PQresultMemorySize">
8245    <term><function>PQresultMemorySize</function><indexterm><primary>PQresultMemorySize</primary></indexterm></term>
8246
8247    <listitem>
8248     <para>
8249      Retrieves the number of bytes allocated for
8250      a <structname>PGresult</structname> object.
8251<synopsis>
8252size_t PQresultMemorySize(const PGresult *res);
8253</synopsis>
8254     </para>
8255
8256     <para>
8257      This value is the sum of all <function>malloc</function> requests
8258      associated with the <structname>PGresult</structname> object, that is,
8259      all the memory that will be freed by <xref linkend="libpq-PQclear"/>.
8260      This information can be useful for managing memory consumption.
8261     </para>
8262    </listitem>
8263   </varlistentry>
8264
8265   <varlistentry id="libpq-PQlibVersion">
8266    <term><function>PQlibVersion</function><indexterm
8267    ><primary>PQlibVersion</primary><seealso>PQserverVersion</seealso></indexterm></term>
8268
8269    <listitem>
8270     <para>
8271      Return the version of <application>libpq</application> that is being used.
8272<synopsis>
8273int PQlibVersion(void);
8274</synopsis>
8275     </para>
8276
8277     <para>
8278      The result of this function can be used to determine, at
8279      run time, whether specific functionality is available in the currently
8280      loaded version of libpq. The function can be used, for example,
8281      to determine which connection options are available in
8282      <xref linkend="libpq-PQconnectdb"/>.
8283     </para>
8284
8285     <para>
8286      The result is formed by multiplying the library's major version
8287      number by 10000 and adding the minor version number.  For example,
8288      version 10.1 will be returned as 100001, and version 11.0 will be
8289      returned as 110000.
8290     </para>
8291
8292     <para>
8293      Prior to major version 10, <productname>PostgreSQL</productname> used
8294      three-part version numbers in which the first two parts together
8295      represented the major version.  For those
8296      versions, <xref linkend="libpq-PQlibVersion"/> uses two digits for each
8297      part; for example version 9.1.5 will be returned as 90105, and
8298      version 9.2.0 will be returned as 90200.
8299     </para>
8300
8301     <para>
8302      Therefore, for purposes of determining feature compatibility,
8303      applications should divide the result of <xref linkend="libpq-PQlibVersion"/>
8304      by 100 not 10000 to determine a logical major version number.
8305      In all release series, only the last two digits differ between
8306      minor releases (bug-fix releases).
8307     </para>
8308
8309     <note>
8310      <para>
8311       This function appeared in <productname>PostgreSQL</productname> version 9.1, so
8312       it cannot be used to detect required functionality in earlier
8313       versions, since calling it will create a link dependency
8314       on version 9.1 or later.
8315      </para>
8316     </note>
8317    </listitem>
8318   </varlistentry>
8319
8320   <varlistentry id="libpq-PQgetCurrentTimeUSec">
8321    <term><function>PQgetCurrentTimeUSec</function><indexterm><primary>PQgetCurrentTimeUSec</primary></indexterm></term>
8322
8323    <listitem>
8324     <para>
8325      Retrieves the current time, expressed as the number of microseconds
8326      since the Unix epoch (that is, <type>time_t</type> times 1 million).
8327<synopsis>
8328pg_usec_time_t PQgetCurrentTimeUSec(void);
8329</synopsis>
8330     </para>
8331
8332     <para>
8333      This is primarily useful for calculating timeout values to use with
8334      <xref linkend="libpq-PQsocketPoll"/>.
8335     </para>
8336    </listitem>
8337   </varlistentry>
8338
8339  </variablelist>
8340
8341 </sect1>
8342
8343 <sect1 id="libpq-notice-processing">
8344  <title>Notice Processing</title>
8345
8346  <indexterm zone="libpq-notice-processing">
8347   <primary>notice processing</primary>
8348   <secondary>in libpq</secondary>
8349  </indexterm>
8350
8351  <para>
8352   Notice and warning messages generated by the server are not returned
8353   by the query execution functions, since they do not imply failure of
8354   the query.  Instead they are passed to a notice handling function, and
8355   execution continues normally after the handler returns.  The default
8356   notice handling function prints the message on
8357   <filename>stderr</filename>, but the application can override this
8358   behavior by supplying its own handling function.
8359  </para>
8360
8361  <para>
8362   For historical reasons, there are two levels of notice handling, called
8363   the notice receiver and notice processor.  The default behavior is for
8364   the notice receiver to format the notice and pass a string to the notice
8365   processor for printing.  However, an application that chooses to provide
8366   its own notice receiver will typically ignore the notice processor
8367   layer and just do all the work in the notice receiver.
8368  </para>
8369
8370  <para>
8371   The function <function id="libpq-PQsetNoticeReceiver">PQsetNoticeReceiver</function>
8372   <indexterm><primary>notice receiver</primary></indexterm>
8373   <indexterm><primary>PQsetNoticeReceiver</primary></indexterm> sets or
8374   examines the current notice receiver for a connection object.
8375   Similarly, <function id="libpq-PQsetNoticeProcessor">PQsetNoticeProcessor</function>
8376   <indexterm><primary>notice processor</primary></indexterm>
8377   <indexterm><primary>PQsetNoticeProcessor</primary></indexterm> sets or
8378   examines the current notice processor.
8379
8380<synopsis>
8381typedef void (*PQnoticeReceiver) (void *arg, const PGresult *res);
8382
8383PQnoticeReceiver
8384PQsetNoticeReceiver(PGconn *conn,
8385                    PQnoticeReceiver proc,
8386                    void *arg);
8387
8388typedef void (*PQnoticeProcessor) (void *arg, const char *message);
8389
8390PQnoticeProcessor
8391PQsetNoticeProcessor(PGconn *conn,
8392                     PQnoticeProcessor proc,
8393                     void *arg);
8394</synopsis>
8395
8396   Each of these functions returns the previous notice receiver or
8397   processor function pointer, and sets the new value.  If you supply a
8398   null function pointer, no action is taken, but the current pointer is
8399   returned.
8400  </para>
8401
8402  <para>
8403   When a notice or warning message is received from the server, or
8404   generated internally by <application>libpq</application>, the notice
8405   receiver function is called.  It is passed the message in the form of
8406   a <symbol>PGRES_NONFATAL_ERROR</symbol>
8407   <structname>PGresult</structname>.  (This allows the receiver to extract
8408   individual fields using <xref linkend="libpq-PQresultErrorField"/>, or obtain a
8409   complete preformatted message using <xref linkend="libpq-PQresultErrorMessage"/>
8410   or <xref linkend="libpq-PQresultVerboseErrorMessage"/>.)  The same
8411   void pointer passed to <function>PQsetNoticeReceiver</function> is also
8412   passed.  (This pointer can be used to access application-specific state
8413   if needed.)
8414  </para>
8415
8416  <para>
8417   The default notice receiver simply extracts the message (using
8418   <xref linkend="libpq-PQresultErrorMessage"/>) and passes it to the notice
8419   processor.
8420  </para>
8421
8422  <para>
8423   The notice processor is responsible for handling a notice or warning
8424   message given in text form.  It is passed the string text of the message
8425   (including a trailing newline), plus a void pointer that is the same
8426   one passed to <function>PQsetNoticeProcessor</function>.  (This pointer
8427   can be used to access application-specific state if needed.)
8428  </para>
8429
8430  <para>
8431   The default notice processor is simply:
8432<programlisting>
8433static void
8434defaultNoticeProcessor(void *arg, const char *message)
8435{
8436    fprintf(stderr, "%s", message);
8437}
8438</programlisting>
8439  </para>
8440
8441  <para>
8442   Once you have set a notice receiver or processor, you should expect
8443   that that function could be called as long as either the
8444   <structname>PGconn</structname> object or <structname>PGresult</structname> objects made
8445   from it exist.  At creation of a <structname>PGresult</structname>, the
8446   <structname>PGconn</structname>'s current notice handling pointers are copied
8447   into the <structname>PGresult</structname> for possible use by functions like
8448   <xref linkend="libpq-PQgetvalue"/>.
8449  </para>
8450
8451 </sect1>
8452
8453 <sect1 id="libpq-events">
8454  <title>Event System</title>
8455
8456  <para>
8457   <application>libpq</application>'s event system is designed to notify
8458   registered event handlers about interesting
8459   <application>libpq</application> events, such as the creation or
8460   destruction of <structname>PGconn</structname> and
8461   <structname>PGresult</structname> objects.  A principal use case is that
8462   this allows applications to associate their own data with a
8463   <structname>PGconn</structname> or <structname>PGresult</structname>
8464   and ensure that that data is freed at an appropriate time.
8465  </para>
8466
8467  <para>
8468   Each registered event handler is associated with two pieces of data,
8469   known to <application>libpq</application> only as opaque <literal>void *</literal>
8470   pointers.  There is a <firstterm>pass-through</firstterm> pointer that is provided
8471   by the application when the event handler is registered with a
8472   <structname>PGconn</structname>.  The pass-through pointer never changes for the
8473   life of the <structname>PGconn</structname> and all <structname>PGresult</structname>s
8474   generated from it; so if used, it must point to long-lived data.
8475   In addition there is an <firstterm>instance data</firstterm> pointer, which starts
8476   out <symbol>NULL</symbol> in every <structname>PGconn</structname> and <structname>PGresult</structname>.
8477   This pointer can be manipulated using the
8478   <xref linkend="libpq-PQinstanceData"/>,
8479   <xref linkend="libpq-PQsetInstanceData"/>,
8480   <xref linkend="libpq-PQresultInstanceData"/> and
8481   <xref linkend="libpq-PQresultSetInstanceData"/> functions.  Note that
8482   unlike the pass-through pointer, instance data of a <structname>PGconn</structname>
8483   is not automatically inherited by <structname>PGresult</structname>s created from
8484   it.  <application>libpq</application> does not know what pass-through
8485   and instance data pointers point to (if anything) and will never attempt
8486   to free them &mdash; that is the responsibility of the event handler.
8487  </para>
8488
8489  <sect2 id="libpq-events-types">
8490   <title>Event Types</title>
8491
8492   <para>
8493    The enum <literal>PGEventId</literal> names the types of events handled by
8494    the event system.  All its values have names beginning with
8495    <literal>PGEVT</literal>.  For each event type, there is a corresponding
8496    event info structure that carries the parameters passed to the event
8497    handlers.  The event types are:
8498   </para>
8499
8500   <variablelist>
8501    <varlistentry id="libpq-pgevt-register">
8502     <term><literal>PGEVT_REGISTER</literal></term>
8503     <listitem>
8504      <para>
8505       The register event occurs when <xref linkend="libpq-PQregisterEventProc"/>
8506       is called.  It is the ideal time to initialize any
8507       <literal>instanceData</literal> an event procedure may need.  Only one
8508       register event will be fired per event handler per connection.  If the
8509       event procedure fails (returns zero), the registration is canceled.
8510
8511<synopsis>
8512typedef struct
8513{
8514    PGconn *conn;
8515} PGEventRegister;
8516</synopsis>
8517
8518       When a <literal>PGEVT_REGISTER</literal> event is received, the
8519       <parameter>evtInfo</parameter> pointer should be cast to a
8520       <structname>PGEventRegister *</structname>.  This structure contains a
8521       <structname>PGconn</structname> that should be in the
8522       <symbol>CONNECTION_OK</symbol> status; guaranteed if one calls
8523       <xref linkend="libpq-PQregisterEventProc"/> right after obtaining a good
8524       <structname>PGconn</structname>.  When returning a failure code, all
8525       cleanup must be performed as no <literal>PGEVT_CONNDESTROY</literal>
8526       event will be sent.
8527      </para>
8528     </listitem>
8529    </varlistentry>
8530
8531    <varlistentry id="libpq-pgevt-connreset">
8532     <term><literal>PGEVT_CONNRESET</literal></term>
8533     <listitem>
8534      <para>
8535       The connection reset event is fired on completion of
8536       <xref linkend="libpq-PQreset"/> or <function>PQresetPoll</function>.  In
8537       both cases, the event is only fired if the reset was successful.
8538       The return value of the event procedure is ignored
8539       in <productname>PostgreSQL</productname> v15 and later.
8540       With earlier versions, however, it's important to return success
8541       (nonzero) or the connection will be aborted.
8542
8543<synopsis>
8544typedef struct
8545{
8546    PGconn *conn;
8547} PGEventConnReset;
8548</synopsis>
8549
8550       When a <literal>PGEVT_CONNRESET</literal> event is received, the
8551       <parameter>evtInfo</parameter> pointer should be cast to a
8552       <structname>PGEventConnReset *</structname>.  Although the contained
8553       <structname>PGconn</structname> was just reset, all event data remains
8554       unchanged.  This event should be used to reset/reload/requery any
8555       associated <literal>instanceData</literal>.  Note that even if the
8556       event procedure fails to process <literal>PGEVT_CONNRESET</literal>, it will
8557       still receive a <literal>PGEVT_CONNDESTROY</literal> event when the connection
8558       is closed.
8559      </para>
8560     </listitem>
8561    </varlistentry>
8562
8563    <varlistentry id="libpq-pgevt-conndestroy">
8564     <term><literal>PGEVT_CONNDESTROY</literal></term>
8565     <listitem>
8566      <para>
8567       The connection destroy event is fired in response to
8568       <xref linkend="libpq-PQfinish"/>.  It is the event procedure's
8569       responsibility to properly clean up its event data as libpq has no
8570       ability to manage this memory.  Failure to clean up will lead
8571       to memory leaks.
8572
8573<synopsis>
8574typedef struct
8575{
8576    PGconn *conn;
8577} PGEventConnDestroy;
8578</synopsis>
8579
8580       When a <literal>PGEVT_CONNDESTROY</literal> event is received, the
8581       <parameter>evtInfo</parameter> pointer should be cast to a
8582       <structname>PGEventConnDestroy *</structname>.  This event is fired
8583       prior to <xref linkend="libpq-PQfinish"/> performing any other cleanup.
8584       The return value of the event procedure is ignored since there is no
8585       way of indicating a failure from <xref linkend="libpq-PQfinish"/>.  Also,
8586       an event procedure failure should not abort the process of cleaning up
8587       unwanted memory.
8588      </para>
8589     </listitem>
8590    </varlistentry>
8591
8592    <varlistentry id="libpq-pgevt-resultcreate">
8593     <term><literal>PGEVT_RESULTCREATE</literal></term>
8594     <listitem>
8595      <para>
8596       The result creation event is fired in response to any query execution
8597       function that generates a result, including
8598       <xref linkend="libpq-PQgetResult"/>.  This event will only be fired after
8599       the result has been created successfully.
8600
8601<synopsis>
8602typedef struct
8603{
8604    PGconn *conn;
8605    PGresult *result;
8606} PGEventResultCreate;
8607</synopsis>
8608
8609       When a <literal>PGEVT_RESULTCREATE</literal> event is received, the
8610       <parameter>evtInfo</parameter> pointer should be cast to a
8611       <structname>PGEventResultCreate *</structname>.  The
8612       <parameter>conn</parameter> is the connection used to generate the
8613       result.  This is the ideal place to initialize any
8614       <literal>instanceData</literal> that needs to be associated with the
8615       result.  If an event procedure fails (returns zero), that event
8616       procedure will be ignored for the remaining lifetime of the result;
8617       that is, it will not receive <literal>PGEVT_RESULTCOPY</literal>
8618       or <literal>PGEVT_RESULTDESTROY</literal> events for this result or
8619       results copied from it.
8620      </para>
8621     </listitem>
8622    </varlistentry>
8623
8624    <varlistentry id="libpq-pgevt-resultcopy">
8625     <term><literal>PGEVT_RESULTCOPY</literal></term>
8626     <listitem>
8627      <para>
8628       The result copy event is fired in response to
8629       <xref linkend="libpq-PQcopyResult"/>.  This event will only be fired after
8630       the copy is complete.  Only event procedures that have
8631       successfully handled the <literal>PGEVT_RESULTCREATE</literal>
8632       or <literal>PGEVT_RESULTCOPY</literal> event for the source result
8633       will receive <literal>PGEVT_RESULTCOPY</literal> events.
8634
8635<synopsis>
8636typedef struct
8637{
8638    const PGresult *src;
8639    PGresult *dest;
8640} PGEventResultCopy;
8641</synopsis>
8642
8643       When a <literal>PGEVT_RESULTCOPY</literal> event is received, the
8644       <parameter>evtInfo</parameter> pointer should be cast to a
8645       <structname>PGEventResultCopy *</structname>.  The
8646       <parameter>src</parameter> result is what was copied while the
8647       <parameter>dest</parameter> result is the copy destination.  This event
8648       can be used to provide a deep copy of <literal>instanceData</literal>,
8649       since <literal>PQcopyResult</literal> cannot do that.  If an event
8650       procedure fails (returns zero), that event procedure will be
8651       ignored for the remaining lifetime of the new result; that is, it
8652       will not receive <literal>PGEVT_RESULTCOPY</literal>
8653       or <literal>PGEVT_RESULTDESTROY</literal> events for that result or
8654       results copied from it.
8655      </para>
8656     </listitem>
8657    </varlistentry>
8658
8659    <varlistentry id="libpq-pgevt-resultdestroy">
8660     <term><literal>PGEVT_RESULTDESTROY</literal></term>
8661     <listitem>
8662      <para>
8663       The result destroy event is fired in response to a
8664       <xref linkend="libpq-PQclear"/>.  It is the event procedure's
8665       responsibility to properly clean up its event data as libpq has no
8666       ability to manage this memory.  Failure to clean up will lead
8667       to memory leaks.
8668
8669<synopsis>
8670typedef struct
8671{
8672    PGresult *result;
8673} PGEventResultDestroy;
8674</synopsis>
8675
8676       When a <literal>PGEVT_RESULTDESTROY</literal> event is received, the
8677       <parameter>evtInfo</parameter> pointer should be cast to a
8678       <structname>PGEventResultDestroy *</structname>.  This event is fired
8679       prior to <xref linkend="libpq-PQclear"/> performing any other cleanup.
8680       The return value of the event procedure is ignored since there is no
8681       way of indicating a failure from <xref linkend="libpq-PQclear"/>.  Also,
8682       an event procedure failure should not abort the process of cleaning up
8683       unwanted memory.
8684      </para>
8685     </listitem>
8686    </varlistentry>
8687   </variablelist>
8688  </sect2>
8689
8690  <sect2 id="libpq-events-proc">
8691   <title>Event Callback Procedure</title>
8692
8693   <variablelist>
8694    <varlistentry id="libpq-PGEventProc">
8695     <term><literal>PGEventProc</literal><indexterm><primary>PGEventProc</primary></indexterm></term>
8696
8697     <listitem>
8698      <para>
8699       <literal>PGEventProc</literal> is a typedef for a pointer to an
8700       event procedure, that is, the user callback function that receives
8701       events from libpq.  The signature of an event procedure must be
8702
8703<synopsis>
8704int eventproc(PGEventId evtId, void *evtInfo, void *passThrough)
8705</synopsis>
8706
8707       The <parameter>evtId</parameter> parameter indicates which
8708       <literal>PGEVT</literal> event occurred.  The
8709       <parameter>evtInfo</parameter> pointer must be cast to the appropriate
8710       structure type to obtain further information about the event.
8711       The <parameter>passThrough</parameter> parameter is the pointer
8712       provided to <xref linkend="libpq-PQregisterEventProc"/> when the event
8713       procedure was registered.  The function should return a non-zero value
8714       if it succeeds and zero if it fails.
8715      </para>
8716
8717      <para>
8718       A particular event procedure can be registered only once in any
8719       <structname>PGconn</structname>.  This is because the address of the procedure
8720       is used as a lookup key to identify the associated instance data.
8721      </para>
8722
8723      <caution>
8724       <para>
8725        On Windows, functions can have two different addresses: one visible
8726        from outside a DLL and another visible from inside the DLL.  One
8727        should be careful that only one of these addresses is used with
8728        <application>libpq</application>'s event-procedure functions, else confusion will
8729        result.  The simplest rule for writing code that will work is to
8730        ensure that event procedures are declared <literal>static</literal>.  If the
8731        procedure's address must be available outside its own source file,
8732        expose a separate function to return the address.
8733       </para>
8734      </caution>
8735     </listitem>
8736    </varlistentry>
8737   </variablelist>
8738  </sect2>
8739
8740  <sect2 id="libpq-events-funcs">
8741   <title>Event Support Functions</title>
8742
8743    <variablelist>
8744    <varlistentry id="libpq-PQregisterEventProc">
8745     <term><function>PQregisterEventProc</function><indexterm><primary>PQregisterEventProc</primary></indexterm></term>
8746
8747     <listitem>
8748      <para>
8749       Registers an event callback procedure with libpq.
8750
8751<synopsis>
8752int PQregisterEventProc(PGconn *conn, PGEventProc proc,
8753                        const char *name, void *passThrough);
8754</synopsis>
8755      </para>
8756
8757      <para>
8758       An event procedure must be registered once on each
8759       <structname>PGconn</structname> you want to receive events about.  There is no
8760       limit, other than memory, on the number of event procedures that
8761       can be registered with a connection.  The function returns a non-zero
8762       value if it succeeds and zero if it fails.
8763      </para>
8764
8765      <para>
8766       The <parameter>proc</parameter> argument will be called when a libpq
8767       event is fired.  Its memory address is also used to lookup
8768       <literal>instanceData</literal>.  The <parameter>name</parameter>
8769       argument is used to refer to the event procedure in error messages.
8770       This value cannot be <symbol>NULL</symbol> or a zero-length string.  The name string is
8771       copied into the <structname>PGconn</structname>, so what is passed need not be
8772       long-lived.  The <parameter>passThrough</parameter> pointer is passed
8773       to the <parameter>proc</parameter> whenever an event occurs. This
8774       argument can be <symbol>NULL</symbol>.
8775      </para>
8776     </listitem>
8777    </varlistentry>
8778
8779    <varlistentry id="libpq-PQsetInstanceData">
8780     <term><function>PQsetInstanceData</function><indexterm><primary>PQsetInstanceData</primary></indexterm></term>
8781     <listitem>
8782      <para>
8783       Sets the connection <parameter>conn</parameter>'s <literal>instanceData</literal>
8784       for procedure <parameter>proc</parameter> to <parameter>data</parameter>.  This
8785       returns non-zero for success and zero for failure.  (Failure is
8786       only possible if <parameter>proc</parameter> has not been properly
8787       registered in <parameter>conn</parameter>.)
8788
8789<synopsis>
8790int PQsetInstanceData(PGconn *conn, PGEventProc proc, void *data);
8791</synopsis>
8792      </para>
8793     </listitem>
8794    </varlistentry>
8795
8796    <varlistentry id="libpq-PQinstanceData">
8797     <term><function>PQinstanceData</function><indexterm><primary>PQinstanceData</primary></indexterm></term>
8798     <listitem>
8799      <para>
8800       Returns the
8801       connection <parameter>conn</parameter>'s <literal>instanceData</literal>
8802       associated with procedure <parameter>proc</parameter>,
8803       or <symbol>NULL</symbol> if there is none.
8804
8805<synopsis>
8806void *PQinstanceData(const PGconn *conn, PGEventProc proc);
8807</synopsis>
8808      </para>
8809     </listitem>
8810    </varlistentry>
8811
8812    <varlistentry id="libpq-PQresultSetInstanceData">
8813     <term><function>PQresultSetInstanceData</function><indexterm><primary>PQresultSetInstanceData</primary></indexterm></term>
8814     <listitem>
8815      <para>
8816       Sets the result's <literal>instanceData</literal>
8817       for <parameter>proc</parameter> to <parameter>data</parameter>.  This returns
8818       non-zero for success and zero for failure.  (Failure is only
8819       possible if <parameter>proc</parameter> has not been properly registered
8820       in the result.)
8821
8822<synopsis>
8823int PQresultSetInstanceData(PGresult *res, PGEventProc proc, void *data);
8824</synopsis>
8825      </para>
8826
8827      <para>
8828       Beware that any storage represented by <parameter>data</parameter>
8829       will not be accounted for by <xref linkend="libpq-PQresultMemorySize"/>,
8830       unless it is allocated using <xref linkend="libpq-PQresultAlloc"/>.
8831       (Doing so is recommendable because it eliminates the need to free
8832       such storage explicitly when the result is destroyed.)
8833      </para>
8834     </listitem>
8835    </varlistentry>
8836
8837    <varlistentry id="libpq-PQresultInstanceData">
8838     <term><function>PQresultInstanceData</function><indexterm><primary>PQresultInstanceData</primary></indexterm></term>
8839     <listitem>
8840      <para>
8841       Returns the result's <literal>instanceData</literal> associated with <parameter>proc</parameter>, or <symbol>NULL</symbol>
8842       if there is none.
8843
8844<synopsis>
8845void *PQresultInstanceData(const PGresult *res, PGEventProc proc);
8846</synopsis>
8847      </para>
8848     </listitem>
8849    </varlistentry>
8850   </variablelist>
8851  </sect2>
8852
8853  <sect2 id="libpq-events-example">
8854   <title>Event Example</title>
8855
8856   <para>
8857    Here is a skeleton example of managing private data associated with
8858    libpq connections and results.
8859   </para>
8860
8861<programlisting>
8862<![CDATA[
8863/* required header for libpq events (note: includes libpq-fe.h) */
8864#include <libpq-events.h>
8865
8866/* The instanceData */
8867typedef struct
8868{
8869    int n;
8870    char *str;
8871} mydata;
8872
8873/* PGEventProc */
8874static int myEventProc(PGEventId evtId, void *evtInfo, void *passThrough);
8875
8876int
8877main(void)
8878{
8879    mydata *data;
8880    PGresult *res;
8881    PGconn *conn =
8882        PQconnectdb("dbname=postgres options=-csearch_path=");
8883
8884    if (PQstatus(conn) != CONNECTION_OK)
8885    {
8886        /* PQerrorMessage's result includes a trailing newline */
8887        fprintf(stderr, "%s", PQerrorMessage(conn));
8888        PQfinish(conn);
8889        return 1;
8890    }
8891
8892    /* called once on any connection that should receive events.
8893     * Sends a PGEVT_REGISTER to myEventProc.
8894     */
8895    if (!PQregisterEventProc(conn, myEventProc, "mydata_proc", NULL))
8896    {
8897        fprintf(stderr, "Cannot register PGEventProc\n");
8898        PQfinish(conn);
8899        return 1;
8900    }
8901
8902    /* conn instanceData is available */
8903    data = PQinstanceData(conn, myEventProc);
8904
8905    /* Sends a PGEVT_RESULTCREATE to myEventProc */
8906    res = PQexec(conn, "SELECT 1 + 1");
8907
8908    /* result instanceData is available */
8909    data = PQresultInstanceData(res, myEventProc);
8910
8911    /* If PG_COPYRES_EVENTS is used, sends a PGEVT_RESULTCOPY to myEventProc */
8912    res_copy = PQcopyResult(res, PG_COPYRES_TUPLES | PG_COPYRES_EVENTS);
8913
8914    /* result instanceData is available if PG_COPYRES_EVENTS was
8915     * used during the PQcopyResult call.
8916     */
8917    data = PQresultInstanceData(res_copy, myEventProc);
8918
8919    /* Both clears send a PGEVT_RESULTDESTROY to myEventProc */
8920    PQclear(res);
8921    PQclear(res_copy);
8922
8923    /* Sends a PGEVT_CONNDESTROY to myEventProc */
8924    PQfinish(conn);
8925
8926    return 0;
8927}
8928
8929static int
8930myEventProc(PGEventId evtId, void *evtInfo, void *passThrough)
8931{
8932    switch (evtId)
8933    {
8934        case PGEVT_REGISTER:
8935        {
8936            PGEventRegister *e = (PGEventRegister *)evtInfo;
8937            mydata *data = get_mydata(e->conn);
8938
8939            /* associate app specific data with connection */
8940            PQsetInstanceData(e->conn, myEventProc, data);
8941            break;
8942        }
8943
8944        case PGEVT_CONNRESET:
8945        {
8946            PGEventConnReset *e = (PGEventConnReset *)evtInfo;
8947            mydata *data = PQinstanceData(e->conn, myEventProc);
8948
8949            if (data)
8950              memset(data, 0, sizeof(mydata));
8951            break;
8952        }
8953
8954        case PGEVT_CONNDESTROY:
8955        {
8956            PGEventConnDestroy *e = (PGEventConnDestroy *)evtInfo;
8957            mydata *data = PQinstanceData(e->conn, myEventProc);
8958
8959            /* free instance data because the conn is being destroyed */
8960            if (data)
8961              free_mydata(data);
8962            break;
8963        }
8964
8965        case PGEVT_RESULTCREATE:
8966        {
8967            PGEventResultCreate *e = (PGEventResultCreate *)evtInfo;
8968            mydata *conn_data = PQinstanceData(e->conn, myEventProc);
8969            mydata *res_data = dup_mydata(conn_data);
8970
8971            /* associate app specific data with result (copy it from conn) */
8972            PQresultSetInstanceData(e->result, myEventProc, res_data);
8973            break;
8974        }
8975
8976        case PGEVT_RESULTCOPY:
8977        {
8978            PGEventResultCopy *e = (PGEventResultCopy *)evtInfo;
8979            mydata *src_data = PQresultInstanceData(e->src, myEventProc);
8980            mydata *dest_data = dup_mydata(src_data);
8981
8982            /* associate app specific data with result (copy it from a result) */
8983            PQresultSetInstanceData(e->dest, myEventProc, dest_data);
8984            break;
8985        }
8986
8987        case PGEVT_RESULTDESTROY:
8988        {
8989            PGEventResultDestroy *e = (PGEventResultDestroy *)evtInfo;
8990            mydata *data = PQresultInstanceData(e->result, myEventProc);
8991
8992            /* free instance data because the result is being destroyed */
8993            if (data)
8994              free_mydata(data);
8995            break;
8996        }
8997
8998        /* unknown event ID, just return true. */
8999        default:
9000            break;
9001    }
9002
9003    return true; /* event processing succeeded */
