9004}
9005]]>
9006</programlisting>
9007  </sect2>
9008 </sect1>
9009
9010 <sect1 id="libpq-envars">
9011  <title>Environment Variables</title>
9012
9013  <indexterm zone="libpq-envars">
9014   <primary>environment variable</primary>
9015  </indexterm>
9016
9017  <para>
9018   The following environment variables can be used to select default
9019   connection parameter values, which will be used by
9020   <xref linkend="libpq-PQconnectdb"/>, <xref linkend="libpq-PQsetdbLogin"/> and
9021   <xref linkend="libpq-PQsetdb"/> if no value is directly specified by the calling
9022   code.  These are useful to avoid hard-coding database connection
9023   information into simple client applications, for example.
9024
9025   <itemizedlist>
9026    <listitem>
9027     <para>
9028      <indexterm>
9029       <primary><envar>PGHOST</envar></primary>
9030      </indexterm>
9031      <envar>PGHOST</envar> behaves the same as the <xref
9032      linkend="libpq-connect-host"/> connection parameter.
9033     </para>
9034    </listitem>
9035
9036    <listitem>
9037     <para>
9038      <indexterm>
9039       <primary><envar>PGSSLNEGOTIATION</envar></primary>
9040      </indexterm>
9041      <envar>PGSSLNEGOTIATION</envar> behaves the same as the <xref
9042      linkend="libpq-connect-sslnegotiation"/> connection parameter.
9043     </para>
9044    </listitem>
9045
9046    <listitem>
9047     <para>
9048      <indexterm>
9049       <primary><envar>PGHOSTADDR</envar></primary>
9050      </indexterm>
9051      <envar>PGHOSTADDR</envar> behaves the same as the <xref
9052      linkend="libpq-connect-hostaddr"/> connection parameter.
9053      This can be set instead of or in addition to <envar>PGHOST</envar>
9054      to avoid DNS lookup overhead.
9055     </para>
9056    </listitem>
9057
9058    <listitem>
9059     <para>
9060      <indexterm>
9061       <primary><envar>PGPORT</envar></primary>
9062      </indexterm>
9063      <envar>PGPORT</envar> behaves the same as the <xref
9064      linkend="libpq-connect-port"/> connection parameter.
9065     </para>
9066    </listitem>
9067
9068    <listitem>
9069     <para>
9070      <indexterm>
9071       <primary><envar>PGDATABASE</envar></primary>
9072      </indexterm>
9073      <envar>PGDATABASE</envar> behaves the same as the <xref
9074      linkend="libpq-connect-dbname"/> connection parameter.
9075      </para>
9076    </listitem>
9077
9078    <listitem>
9079     <para>
9080      <indexterm>
9081       <primary><envar>PGUSER</envar></primary>
9082      </indexterm>
9083      <envar>PGUSER</envar> behaves the same as the <xref
9084      linkend="libpq-connect-user"/> connection parameter.
9085     </para>
9086    </listitem>
9087
9088    <listitem>
9089     <para>
9090      <indexterm>
9091       <primary><envar>PGPASSWORD</envar></primary>
9092      </indexterm>
9093      <envar>PGPASSWORD</envar> behaves the same as the <xref
9094      linkend="libpq-connect-password"/> connection parameter.
9095      Use of this environment variable
9096      is not recommended for security reasons, as some operating systems
9097      allow non-root users to see process environment variables via
9098      <application>ps</application>; instead consider using a password file
9099      (see <xref linkend="libpq-pgpass"/>).
9100     </para>
9101    </listitem>
9102
9103    <listitem>
9104     <para>
9105      <indexterm>
9106       <primary><envar>PGPASSFILE</envar></primary>
9107      </indexterm>
9108      <envar>PGPASSFILE</envar> behaves the same as the <xref
9109      linkend="libpq-connect-passfile"/> connection parameter.
9110     </para>
9111    </listitem>
9112
9113    <listitem>
9114     <para>
9115      <indexterm>
9116       <primary><envar>PGREQUIREAUTH</envar></primary>
9117      </indexterm>
9118      <envar>PGREQUIREAUTH</envar> behaves the same as the <xref
9119      linkend="libpq-connect-require-auth"/> connection parameter.
9120     </para>
9121    </listitem>
9122
9123    <listitem>
9124     <para>
9125      <indexterm>
9126       <primary><envar>PGCHANNELBINDING</envar></primary>
9127      </indexterm>
9128      <envar>PGCHANNELBINDING</envar> behaves the same as the <xref
9129      linkend="libpq-connect-channel-binding"/> connection parameter.
9130     </para>
9131    </listitem>
9132
9133    <listitem>
9134     <para>
9135      <indexterm>
9136       <primary><envar>PGSERVICE</envar></primary>
9137      </indexterm>
9138      <envar>PGSERVICE</envar> behaves the same as the <xref
9139      linkend="libpq-connect-service"/> connection parameter.
9140     </para>
9141    </listitem>
9142
9143    <listitem>
9144     <para>
9145      <indexterm>
9146       <primary><envar>PGSERVICEFILE</envar></primary>
9147      </indexterm>
9148      <envar>PGSERVICEFILE</envar> specifies the name of the per-user
9149      connection service file
9150      (see <xref linkend="libpq-pgservice"/>).
9151      Defaults to <filename>~/.pg_service.conf</filename>, or
9152      <filename>%APPDATA%\postgresql\.pg_service.conf</filename> on
9153      Microsoft Windows.
9154     </para>
9155    </listitem>
9156
9157    <listitem>
9158     <para>
9159      <indexterm>
9160       <primary><envar>PGOPTIONS</envar></primary>
9161      </indexterm>
9162      <envar>PGOPTIONS</envar> behaves the same as the <xref
9163      linkend="libpq-connect-options"/> connection parameter.
9164     </para>
9165    </listitem>
9166
9167    <listitem>
9168     <para>
9169      <indexterm>
9170       <primary><envar>PGAPPNAME</envar></primary>
9171      </indexterm>
9172      <envar>PGAPPNAME</envar> behaves the same as the <xref
9173      linkend="libpq-connect-application-name"/> connection parameter.
9174     </para>
9175    </listitem>
9176
9177    <listitem>
9178     <para>
9179      <indexterm>
9180       <primary><envar>PGSSLMODE</envar></primary>
9181      </indexterm>
9182      <envar>PGSSLMODE</envar> behaves the same as the <xref
9183      linkend="libpq-connect-sslmode"/> connection parameter.
9184     </para>
9185    </listitem>
9186
9187    <listitem>
9188     <para>
9189      <indexterm>
9190       <primary><envar>PGREQUIRESSL</envar></primary>
9191      </indexterm>
9192      <envar>PGREQUIRESSL</envar> behaves the same as the <xref
9193      linkend="libpq-connect-requiressl"/> connection parameter.
9194      This environment variable is deprecated in favor of the
9195      <envar>PGSSLMODE</envar> variable; setting both variables suppresses the
9196      effect of this one.
9197     </para>
9198    </listitem>
9199
9200    <listitem>
9201     <para>
9202      <indexterm>
9203       <primary><envar>PGSSLCOMPRESSION</envar></primary>
9204      </indexterm>
9205      <envar>PGSSLCOMPRESSION</envar> behaves the same as the <xref
9206      linkend="libpq-connect-sslcompression"/> connection parameter.
9207     </para>
9208    </listitem>
9209
9210    <listitem>
9211     <para>
9212      <indexterm>
9213       <primary><envar>PGSSLCERT</envar></primary>
9214      </indexterm>
9215      <envar>PGSSLCERT</envar> behaves the same as the <xref
9216      linkend="libpq-connect-sslcert"/> connection parameter.
9217     </para>
9218    </listitem>
9219
9220    <listitem>
9221     <para>
9222      <indexterm>
9223       <primary><envar>PGSSLKEY</envar></primary>
9224      </indexterm>
9225      <envar>PGSSLKEY</envar> behaves the same as the <xref
9226      linkend="libpq-connect-sslkey"/> connection parameter.
9227     </para>
9228    </listitem>
9229
9230    <listitem>
9231     <para>
9232      <indexterm>
9233       <primary><envar>PGSSLCERTMODE</envar></primary>
9234      </indexterm>
9235      <envar>PGSSLCERTMODE</envar> behaves the same as the <xref
9236      linkend="libpq-connect-sslcertmode"/> connection parameter.
9237     </para>
9238    </listitem>
9239
9240    <listitem>
9241     <para>
9242      <indexterm>
9243       <primary><envar>PGSSLROOTCERT</envar></primary>
9244      </indexterm>
9245      <envar>PGSSLROOTCERT</envar>  behaves the same as the <xref
9246      linkend="libpq-connect-sslrootcert"/> connection parameter.
9247     </para>
9248    </listitem>
9249
9250    <listitem>
9251     <para>
9252      <indexterm>
9253       <primary><envar>PGSSLCRL</envar></primary>
9254      </indexterm>
9255      <envar>PGSSLCRL</envar>  behaves the same as the <xref
9256      linkend="libpq-connect-sslcrl"/> connection parameter.
9257     </para>
9258    </listitem>
9259
9260    <listitem>
9261     <para>
9262      <indexterm>
9263       <primary><envar>PGSSLCRLDIR</envar></primary>
9264      </indexterm>
9265      <envar>PGSSLCRLDIR</envar> behaves the same as the <xref
9266      linkend="libpq-connect-sslcrldir"/> connection parameter.
9267     </para>
9268    </listitem>
9269
9270    <listitem>
9271     <para>
9272      <indexterm>
9273       <primary><envar>PGSSLSNI</envar></primary>
9274      </indexterm>
9275      <envar>PGSSLSNI</envar>  behaves the same as the <xref
9276      linkend="libpq-connect-sslsni"/> connection parameter.
9277     </para>
9278    </listitem>
9279
9280    <listitem>
9281     <para>
9282      <indexterm>
9283       <primary><envar>PGREQUIREPEER</envar></primary>
9284      </indexterm>
9285      <envar>PGREQUIREPEER</envar> behaves the same as the <xref
9286      linkend="libpq-connect-requirepeer"/> connection parameter.
9287     </para>
9288    </listitem>
9289
9290    <listitem>
9291     <para>
9292      <indexterm>
9293       <primary><envar>PGSSLMINPROTOCOLVERSION</envar></primary>
9294      </indexterm>
9295      <envar>PGSSLMINPROTOCOLVERSION</envar> behaves the same as the <xref
9296      linkend="libpq-connect-ssl-min-protocol-version"/> connection parameter.
9297     </para>
9298    </listitem>
9299
9300    <listitem>
9301     <para>
9302      <indexterm>
9303       <primary><envar>PGSSLMAXPROTOCOLVERSION</envar></primary>
9304      </indexterm>
9305      <envar>PGSSLMAXPROTOCOLVERSION</envar> behaves the same as the <xref
9306      linkend="libpq-connect-ssl-max-protocol-version"/> connection parameter.
9307     </para>
9308    </listitem>
9309
9310    <listitem>
9311     <para>
9312      <indexterm>
9313       <primary><envar>PGGSSENCMODE</envar></primary>
9314      </indexterm>
9315      <envar>PGGSSENCMODE</envar> behaves the same as the <xref
9316      linkend="libpq-connect-gssencmode"/> connection parameter.
9317     </para>
9318    </listitem>
9319
9320    <listitem>
9321     <para>
9322      <indexterm>
9323       <primary><envar>PGKRBSRVNAME</envar></primary>
9324      </indexterm>
9325      <envar>PGKRBSRVNAME</envar>  behaves the same as the <xref
9326      linkend="libpq-connect-krbsrvname"/> connection parameter.
9327     </para>
9328    </listitem>
9329
9330    <listitem>
9331     <para>
9332      <indexterm>
9333       <primary><envar>PGGSSLIB</envar></primary>
9334      </indexterm>
9335      <envar>PGGSSLIB</envar> behaves the same as the <xref
9336      linkend="libpq-connect-gsslib"/> connection parameter.
9337     </para>
9338    </listitem>
9339
9340    <listitem>
9341     <para>
9342      <indexterm>
9343       <primary><envar>PGGSSDELEGATION</envar></primary>
9344      </indexterm>
9345      <envar>PGGSSDELEGATION</envar> behaves the same as the <xref
9346      linkend="libpq-connect-gssdelegation"/> connection parameter.
9347     </para>
9348    </listitem>
9349
9350    <listitem>
9351     <para>
9352      <indexterm>
9353       <primary><envar>PGCONNECT_TIMEOUT</envar></primary>
9354      </indexterm>
9355      <envar>PGCONNECT_TIMEOUT</envar>  behaves the same as the <xref
9356      linkend="libpq-connect-connect-timeout"/> connection parameter.
9357     </para>
9358    </listitem>
9359
9360    <listitem>
9361     <para>
9362      <indexterm>
9363       <primary><envar>PGCLIENTENCODING</envar></primary>
9364      </indexterm>
9365      <envar>PGCLIENTENCODING</envar> behaves the same as the <xref
9366      linkend="libpq-connect-client-encoding"/> connection parameter.
9367     </para>
9368    </listitem>
9369
9370    <listitem>
9371     <para>
9372      <indexterm>
9373       <primary><envar>PGTARGETSESSIONATTRS</envar></primary>
9374      </indexterm>
9375      <envar>PGTARGETSESSIONATTRS</envar> behaves the same as the <xref
9376      linkend="libpq-connect-target-session-attrs"/> connection parameter.
9377     </para>
9378    </listitem>
9379
9380    <listitem>
9381     <para>
9382      <indexterm>
9383       <primary><envar>PGLOADBALANCEHOSTS</envar></primary>
9384      </indexterm>
9385      <envar>PGLOADBALANCEHOSTS</envar> behaves the same as the <xref
9386      linkend="libpq-connect-load-balance-hosts"/> connection parameter.
9387     </para>
9388    </listitem>
9389
9390    <listitem>
9391     <para>
9392      <indexterm>
9393       <primary><envar>PGMINPROTOCOLVERSION</envar></primary>
9394      </indexterm>
9395      <envar>PGMINPROTOCOLVERSION</envar> behaves the same as the <xref
9396      linkend="libpq-connect-min-protocol-version"/> connection parameter.
9397     </para>
9398    </listitem>
9399
9400    <listitem>
9401     <para>
9402      <indexterm>
9403       <primary><envar>PGMAXPROTOCOLVERSION</envar></primary>
9404      </indexterm>
9405      <envar>PGMAXPROTOCOLVERSION</envar> behaves the same as the <xref
9406      linkend="libpq-connect-max-protocol-version"/> connection parameter.
9407     </para>
9408    </listitem>
9409   </itemizedlist>
9410  </para>
9411
9412  <para>
9413   The following environment variables can be used to specify default
9414   behavior for each <productname>PostgreSQL</productname> session.  (See
9415   also the <xref linkend="sql-alterrole"/>
9416   and <xref linkend="sql-alterdatabase"/>
9417   commands for ways to set default behavior on a per-user or per-database
9418   basis.)
9419
9420   <itemizedlist>
9421    <listitem>
9422     <para>
9423      <indexterm>
9424       <primary><envar>PGDATESTYLE</envar></primary>
9425      </indexterm>
9426      <envar>PGDATESTYLE</envar> sets the default style of date/time
9427      representation.  (Equivalent to <literal>SET datestyle TO
9428      ...</literal>.)
9429     </para>
9430    </listitem>
9431
9432    <listitem>
9433     <para>
9434      <indexterm>
9435       <primary><envar>PGTZ</envar></primary>
9436      </indexterm>
9437      <envar>PGTZ</envar> sets the default time zone.  (Equivalent to
9438      <literal>SET timezone TO ...</literal>.)
9439     </para>
9440    </listitem>
9441
9442    <listitem>
9443     <para>
9444      <indexterm>
9445       <primary><envar>PGGEQO</envar></primary>
9446      </indexterm>
9447      <envar>PGGEQO</envar> sets the default mode for the genetic query
9448      optimizer.  (Equivalent to <literal>SET geqo TO ...</literal>.)
9449     </para>
9450    </listitem>
9451   </itemizedlist>
9452
9453   Refer to the <acronym>SQL</acronym> command <xref linkend="sql-set"/>
9454   for information on correct values for these
9455   environment variables.
9456  </para>
9457
9458  <para>
9459   The following environment variables determine internal behavior of
9460   <application>libpq</application>; they override compiled-in defaults.
9461
9462   <itemizedlist>
9463    <listitem>
9464     <para>
9465      <indexterm>
9466       <primary><envar>PGSYSCONFDIR</envar></primary>
9467      </indexterm>
9468      <envar>PGSYSCONFDIR</envar> sets the directory containing the
9469      <filename>pg_service.conf</filename> file and in a future version
9470      possibly other system-wide configuration files.
9471     </para>
9472    </listitem>
9473
9474    <listitem>
9475     <para>
9476      <indexterm>
9477       <primary><envar>PGLOCALEDIR</envar></primary>
9478      </indexterm>
9479      <envar>PGLOCALEDIR</envar> sets the directory containing the
9480      <literal>locale</literal> files for message localization.
9481     </para>
9482    </listitem>
9483   </itemizedlist>
9484  </para>
9485
9486 </sect1>
9487
9488
9489 <sect1 id="libpq-pgpass">
9490  <title>The Password File</title>
9491
9492  <indexterm zone="libpq-pgpass">
9493   <primary>password file</primary>
9494  </indexterm>
9495  <indexterm zone="libpq-pgpass">
9496   <primary>.pgpass</primary>
9497  </indexterm>
9498
9499  <para>
9500   The file <filename>.pgpass</filename> in a user's home directory can
9501   contain passwords to
9502   be used if the connection requires a password (and no password has been
9503   specified otherwise). On Unix systems, the directory can be specified by
9504   the <envar>HOME</envar> environment variable, or if undefined, the home
9505   directory of the effective user.  On Microsoft Windows the file is named
9506   <filename>%APPDATA%\postgresql\pgpass.conf</filename> (where
9507   <filename>%APPDATA%</filename> refers to the Application Data subdirectory in
9508   the user's profile).
9509   Alternatively, the password file to use can be specified
9510   using the connection parameter <xref linkend="libpq-connect-passfile"/>
9511   or the environment variable <envar>PGPASSFILE</envar>.
9512  </para>
9513
9514  <para>
9515   This file should contain lines of the following format:
9516<synopsis>
9517<replaceable>hostname</replaceable>:<replaceable>port</replaceable>:<replaceable>database</replaceable>:<replaceable>username</replaceable>:<replaceable>password</replaceable>
9518</synopsis>
9519   (You can add a reminder comment to the file by copying the line above and
9520   preceding it with <literal>#</literal>.)
9521   Each of the first four fields can be a literal value, or
9522   <literal>*</literal>, which matches anything.  The password field from
9523   the first line that matches the current connection parameters will be
9524   used.  (Therefore, put more-specific entries first when you are using
9525   wildcards.) If an entry needs to contain <literal>:</literal> or
9526   <literal>\</literal>, escape this character with <literal>\</literal>.
9527   The host name field is matched to the <literal>host</literal> connection
9528   parameter if that is specified, otherwise to
9529   the <literal>hostaddr</literal> parameter if that is specified; if neither
9530   are given then the host name <literal>localhost</literal> is searched for.
9531   The host name <literal>localhost</literal> is also searched for when
9532   the connection is a Unix-domain socket connection and
9533   the <literal>host</literal> parameter
9534   matches <application>libpq</application>'s default socket directory path.
9535   In a standby server, a database field of <literal>replication</literal>
9536   matches streaming replication connections made to the primary server.
9537   The database field is of limited usefulness otherwise, because users have
9538   the same password for all databases in the same cluster.
9539  </para>
9540
9541  <para>
9542   On Unix systems, the permissions on a password file must
9543   disallow any access to world or group; achieve this by a command such as
9544   <command>chmod 0600 ~/.pgpass</command>.  If the permissions are less
9545   strict than this, the file will be ignored.  On Microsoft Windows, it
9546   is assumed that the file is stored in a directory that is secure, so
9547   no special permissions check is made.
9548  </para>
9549 </sect1>
9550
9551
9552 <sect1 id="libpq-pgservice">
9553  <title>The Connection Service File</title>
9554
9555  <indexterm zone="libpq-pgservice">
9556   <primary>connection service file</primary>
9557  </indexterm>
9558  <indexterm zone="libpq-pgservice">
9559   <primary>pg_service.conf</primary>
9560  </indexterm>
9561  <indexterm zone="libpq-pgservice">
9562   <primary>.pg_service.conf</primary>
9563  </indexterm>
9564
9565  <para>
9566   The connection service file allows libpq connection parameters to be
9567   associated with a single service name. That service name can then be
9568   specified using the <literal>service</literal> key word
9569   in a libpq connection string, and the associated settings will be
9570   used. This allows connection parameters to be modified without requiring
9571   a recompile of the libpq-using application. The service name can also be
9572   specified using the <envar>PGSERVICE</envar> environment variable.
9573  </para>
9574
9575  <para>
9576   Service names can be defined in either a per-user service file or a
9577   system-wide file.  If the same service name exists in both the user
9578   and the system file, the user file takes precedence.
9579   By default, the per-user service file is named
9580   <filename>~/.pg_service.conf</filename>.
9581   On Microsoft Windows, it is named
9582   <filename>%APPDATA%\postgresql\.pg_service.conf</filename> (where
9583   <filename>%APPDATA%</filename> refers to the Application Data subdirectory
9584   in the user's profile).  A different file name can be specified by
9585   setting the environment variable <envar>PGSERVICEFILE</envar>.
9586   The system-wide file is named <filename>pg_service.conf</filename>.
9587   By default it is sought in the <filename>etc</filename> directory
9588   of the <productname>PostgreSQL</productname> installation
9589   (use <literal>pg_config --sysconfdir</literal> to identify this
9590   directory precisely).  Another directory, but not a different file
9591   name, can be specified by setting the environment variable
9592   <envar>PGSYSCONFDIR</envar>.
9593  </para>
9594
9595  <para>
9596   Either service file uses an <quote>INI file</quote> format where the section
9597   name is the service name and the parameters are connection
9598   parameters; see <xref linkend="libpq-paramkeywords"/> for a list.  For
9599   example:
9600<programlisting>
9601# comment
9602[mydb]
9603host=somehost
9604port=5433
9605user=admin
9606</programlisting>
9607   An example file is provided in
9608   the <productname>PostgreSQL</productname> installation at
9609   <filename>share/pg_service.conf.sample</filename>.
9610  </para>
9611
9612  <para>
9613   Connection parameters obtained from a service file are combined with
9614   parameters obtained from other sources.  A service file setting
9615   overrides the corresponding environment variable, and in turn can be
9616   overridden by a value given directly in the connection string.
9617   For example, using the above service file, a connection string
9618   <literal>service=mydb port=5434</literal> will use
9619   host <literal>somehost</literal>, port <literal>5434</literal>,
9620   user <literal>admin</literal>, and other parameters as set by
9621   environment variables or built-in defaults.
9622  </para>
9623 </sect1>
9624
9625
9626 <sect1 id="libpq-ldap">
9627  <title>LDAP Lookup of Connection Parameters</title>
9628
9629  <indexterm zone="libpq-ldap">
9630   <primary>LDAP connection parameter lookup</primary>
9631  </indexterm>
9632
9633  <para>
9634   If <application>libpq</application> has been compiled with LDAP support (option
9635   <literal><option>--with-ldap</option></literal> for <command>configure</command>)
9636   it is possible to retrieve connection options like <literal>host</literal>
9637   or <literal>dbname</literal> via LDAP from a central server.
9638   The advantage is that if the connection parameters for a database change,
9639   the connection information doesn't have to be updated on all client machines.
9640  </para>
9641
9642  <para>
9643   LDAP connection parameter lookup uses the connection service file
9644   <filename>pg_service.conf</filename> (see <xref
9645   linkend="libpq-pgservice"/>).  A line in a
9646   <filename>pg_service.conf</filename> stanza that starts with
9647   <literal>ldap://</literal> will be recognized as an LDAP URL and an
9648   LDAP query will be performed. The result must be a list of
9649   <literal>keyword = value</literal> pairs which will be used to set
9650   connection options.  The URL must conform to
9651   <ulink url="https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc1959">RFC 1959</ulink>
9652   and be of the form
9653<synopsis>
9654ldap://[<replaceable>hostname</replaceable>[:<replaceable>port</replaceable>]]/<replaceable>search_base</replaceable>?<replaceable>attribute</replaceable>?<replaceable>search_scope</replaceable>?<replaceable>filter</replaceable>
9655</synopsis>
9656   where <replaceable>hostname</replaceable> defaults to
9657   <literal>localhost</literal> and <replaceable>port</replaceable>
9658   defaults to 389.
9659  </para>
9660
9661  <para>
9662   Processing of <filename>pg_service.conf</filename> is terminated after
9663   a successful LDAP lookup, but is continued if the LDAP server cannot
9664   be contacted.  This is to provide a fallback with further LDAP URL
9665   lines that point to different LDAP servers, classical <literal>keyword
9666   = value</literal> pairs, or default connection options.  If you would
9667   rather get an error message in this case, add a syntactically incorrect
9668   line after the LDAP URL.
9669  </para>
9670
9671  <para>
9672   A sample LDAP entry that has been created with the LDIF file
9673<programlisting>
9674version:1
9675dn:cn=mydatabase,dc=mycompany,dc=com
9676changetype:add
9677objectclass:top
9678objectclass:device
9679cn:mydatabase
9680description:host=dbserver.mycompany.com
9681description:port=5439
9682description:dbname=mydb
9683description:user=mydb_user
9684description:sslmode=require
9685</programlisting>
9686   might be queried with the following LDAP URL:
9687<programlisting>
9688ldap://ldap.mycompany.com/dc=mycompany,dc=com?description?one?(cn=mydatabase)
9689</programlisting>
9690  </para>
9691
9692  <para>
9693   You can also mix regular service file entries with LDAP lookups.
9694   A complete example for a stanza in <filename>pg_service.conf</filename>
9695   would be:
9696<programlisting>
9697# only host and port are stored in LDAP, specify dbname and user explicitly
9698[customerdb]
9699dbname=customer
9700user=appuser
9701ldap://ldap.acme.com/cn=dbserver,cn=hosts?pgconnectinfo?base?(objectclass=*)
9702</programlisting>
9703  </para>
9704
9705 </sect1>
9706
9707
9708 <sect1 id="libpq-ssl">
9709  <title>SSL Support</title>
9710
9711  <indexterm zone="libpq-ssl">
9712   <primary>SSL</primary>
9713   <secondary>TLS</secondary>
9714  </indexterm>
9715
9716  <para>
9717   <productname>PostgreSQL</productname> has native support for using <acronym>SSL</acronym>
9718   connections to encrypt client/server communications using
9719   <acronym>TLS</acronym> protocols for increased security.
9720   See <xref linkend="ssl-tcp"/> for details about the server-side
9721   <acronym>SSL</acronym> functionality.
9722  </para>
9723
9724  <para>
9725   <application>libpq</application> reads the system-wide
9726   <productname>OpenSSL</productname> configuration file. By default, this
9727   file is named <filename>openssl.cnf</filename> and is located in the
9728   directory reported by <literal>openssl version -d</literal>.  This default
9729   can be overridden by setting environment variable
9730   <envar>OPENSSL_CONF</envar> to the name of the desired configuration
9731   file.
9732  </para>
9733
9734 <sect2 id="libq-ssl-certificates">
9735  <title>Client Verification of Server Certificates</title>
9736
9737  <para>
9738   By default, <productname>PostgreSQL</productname> will not perform any verification of
9739   the server certificate. This means that it is possible to spoof the server
9740   identity (for example by modifying a DNS record or by taking over the server
9741   IP address) without the client knowing. In order to prevent spoofing,
9742   the client must be able to verify the server's identity via a chain of
9743   trust.  A chain of trust is established by placing a root (self-signed)
9744   certificate authority (<acronym>CA</acronym>) certificate on one
9745   computer and a leaf certificate <emphasis>signed</emphasis> by the
9746   root certificate on another computer.  It is also possible to use an
9747   <quote>intermediate</quote> certificate which is signed by the root
9748   certificate and signs leaf certificates.
9749  </para>
9750
9751  <para>
9752   To allow the client to verify the identity of the server, place a root
9753   certificate on the client and a leaf certificate signed by the root
9754   certificate on the server.  To allow the server to verify the identity
9755   of the client, place a root certificate on the server and a leaf
9756   certificate signed by the root certificate on the client.  One or more
9757   intermediate certificates (usually stored with the leaf certificate)
9758   can also be used to link the leaf certificate to the root certificate.
9759  </para>
9760
9761  <para>
9762   Once a chain of trust has been established, there are two ways for
9763   the client to validate the leaf certificate sent by the server.
9764   If the parameter <literal>sslmode</literal> is set to <literal>verify-ca</literal>,
9765   libpq will verify that the server is trustworthy by checking the
9766   certificate chain up to the root certificate stored on the client.
9767   If <literal>sslmode</literal> is set to <literal>verify-full</literal>,
9768   libpq will <emphasis>also</emphasis> verify that the server host
9769   name matches the name stored in the server certificate. The
9770   SSL connection will fail if the server certificate cannot be
9771   verified. <literal>verify-full</literal> is recommended in most
9772   security-sensitive environments.
9773  </para>
9774
9775  <para>
9776   In <literal>verify-full</literal> mode, the host name is matched against the
9777   certificate's Subject Alternative Name attribute(s) (SAN), or against the
9778   Common Name attribute if no SAN of type <literal>dNSName</literal> is
9779   present.  If the certificate's name attribute starts with an asterisk
9780   (<literal>*</literal>), the asterisk will be treated as
9781   a wildcard, which will match all characters <emphasis>except</emphasis> a dot
9782   (<literal>.</literal>). This means the certificate will not match subdomains.
9783   If the connection is made using an IP address instead of a host name, the
9784   IP address will be matched (without doing any DNS lookups) against SANs of
9785   type <literal>iPAddress</literal> or <literal>dNSName</literal>.  If no
9786   <literal>iPAddress</literal> SAN is present and no
9787   matching <literal>dNSName</literal> SAN is present, the host IP address is
9788   matched against the Common Name attribute.
9789  </para>
9790
9791  <note>
9792   <para>
9793    For backward compatibility with earlier versions of PostgreSQL, the host
9794    IP address is verified in a manner different
9795    from <ulink url="https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc6125">RFC 6125</ulink>.
9796    The host IP address is always matched against <literal>dNSName</literal>
9797    SANs as well as <literal>iPAddress</literal> SANs, and can be matched
9798    against the Common Name attribute if no relevant SANs exist.
9799   </para>
9800  </note>
9801
9802  <para>
9803   To allow server certificate verification, one or more root certificates
9804   must be placed in the file <filename>~/.postgresql/root.crt</filename>
9805   in the user's home directory.  (On Microsoft Windows the file is named
9806   <filename>%APPDATA%\postgresql\root.crt</filename>.)  Intermediate
9807   certificates should also be added to the file if they are needed to link
9808   the certificate chain sent by the server to the root certificates
9809   stored on the client.
9810  </para>
9811
9812  <para>
9813   Certificate Revocation List (CRL) entries are also checked
9814   if the file <filename>~/.postgresql/root.crl</filename> exists
9815   (<filename>%APPDATA%\postgresql\root.crl</filename> on Microsoft
9816   Windows).
9817  </para>
9818
9819  <para>
9820   The location of the root certificate file and the CRL can be changed by
9821   setting
9822   the connection parameters <literal>sslrootcert</literal> and <literal>sslcrl</literal>
9823   or the environment variables <envar>PGSSLROOTCERT</envar> and <envar>PGSSLCRL</envar>.
9824   <literal>sslcrldir</literal> or the environment variable <envar>PGSSLCRLDIR</envar>
9825   can also be used to specify a directory containing CRL files.
9826  </para>
9827
9828  <note>
9829   <para>
9830    For backwards compatibility with earlier versions of PostgreSQL, if a
9831    root CA file exists, the behavior of
9832    <literal>sslmode=require</literal> will be the same
9833    as that of <literal>verify-ca</literal>, meaning the server certificate
9834    is validated against the CA. Relying on this behavior is discouraged,
9835    and applications that need certificate validation should always use
9836    <literal>verify-ca</literal> or <literal>verify-full</literal>.
9837   </para>
9838  </note>
9839 </sect2>
9840
9841 <sect2 id="libpq-ssl-clientcert">
9842  <title>Client Certificates</title>
9843
9844  <para>
9845   If the server attempts to verify the identity of the
9846   client by requesting the client's leaf certificate,
9847   <application>libpq</application> will send the certificate(s) stored in
9848   file <filename>~/.postgresql/postgresql.crt</filename> in the user's home
9849   directory.  The certificates must chain to the root certificate trusted
9850   by the server.  A matching
9851   private key file <filename>~/.postgresql/postgresql.key</filename> must also
9852   be present.
9853   On Microsoft Windows these files are named
9854   <filename>%APPDATA%\postgresql\postgresql.crt</filename> and
9855   <filename>%APPDATA%\postgresql\postgresql.key</filename>.
9856   The location of the certificate and key files can be overridden by the
9857   connection parameters <literal>sslcert</literal>
9858   and <literal>sslkey</literal>, or by the
9859   environment variables <envar>PGSSLCERT</envar> and <envar>PGSSLKEY</envar>.
9860  </para>
9861
9862  <para>
9863   On Unix systems, the permissions on the private key file must disallow
9864   any access to world or group; achieve this by a command such as
9865   <command>chmod 0600 ~/.postgresql/postgresql.key</command>.
9866   Alternatively, the file can be owned by root and have group read access
9867   (that is, <literal>0640</literal> permissions).  That setup is intended
9868   for installations where certificate and key files are managed by the
9869   operating system.  The user of <application>libpq</application> should
9870   then be made a member of the group that has access to those certificate
9871   and key files.  (On Microsoft Windows, there is no file permissions
9872   check, since the <filename>%APPDATA%\postgresql</filename> directory is
9873   presumed secure.)
9874  </para>
9875
9876  <para>
9877   The first certificate in <filename>postgresql.crt</filename> must be the
9878   client's certificate because it must match the client's private key.
9879   <quote>Intermediate</quote> certificates can be optionally appended
9880   to the file &mdash; doing so avoids requiring storage of intermediate
9881   certificates on the server (<xref linkend="guc-ssl-ca-file"/>).
9882  </para>
9883
9884  <para>
9885   The certificate and key may be in PEM or ASN.1 DER format.
9886  </para>
9887
9888  <para>
9889   The key may be
9890   stored in cleartext or encrypted with a passphrase using any algorithm
9891   supported by <productname>OpenSSL</productname>, like AES-128. If the key
9892   is stored encrypted, then the passphrase may be provided in the
9893   <xref linkend="libpq-connect-sslpassword"/> connection option. If an
9894   encrypted key is supplied and the <literal>sslpassword</literal> option
9895   is absent or blank, a password will be prompted for interactively by
9896   <productname>OpenSSL</productname> with a
9897   <literal>Enter PEM pass phrase:</literal> prompt if a TTY is available.
9898   Applications can override the client certificate prompt and the handling
9899   of the <literal>sslpassword</literal> parameter by supplying their own
9900   key password callback; see
9901   <xref linkend="libpq-pqsetsslkeypasshook-openssl"/>.
9902  </para>
9903
9904  <para>
9905   For instructions on creating certificates, see <xref
9906   linkend="ssl-certificate-creation"/>.
9907  </para>
9908 </sect2>
9909
9910 <sect2 id="libpq-ssl-protection">
9911  <title>Protection Provided in Different Modes</title>
9912
9913  <para>
9914   The different values for the <literal>sslmode</literal> parameter provide different
9915   levels of protection. SSL can provide
9916   protection against three types of attacks:
9917
9918   <variablelist>
9919    <varlistentry>
9920     <term>Eavesdropping</term>
9921     <listitem>
9922      <para>If a third party can examine the network traffic between the
9923       client and the server, it can read both connection information (including
9924       the user name and password) and the data that is passed. <acronym>SSL</acronym>
9925       uses encryption to prevent this.
9926      </para>
9927     </listitem>
9928    </varlistentry>
9929
9930    <varlistentry>
9931     <term>Man-in-the-middle (<acronym>MITM</acronym>)</term>
9932     <listitem>
9933      <para>If a third party can modify the data while passing between the
9934       client and server, it can pretend to be the server and therefore see and
9935       modify data <emphasis>even if it is encrypted</emphasis>. The third party can then
9936       forward the connection information and data to the original server,
9937       making it impossible to detect this attack. Common vectors to do this
9938       include DNS poisoning and address hijacking, whereby the client is directed
9939       to a different server than intended. There are also several other
9940       attack methods that can accomplish this. <acronym>SSL</acronym> uses certificate
9941       verification to prevent this, by authenticating the server to the client.
9942      </para>
9943     </listitem>
9944    </varlistentry>
9945
9946    <varlistentry>
9947     <term>Impersonation</term>
9948     <listitem>
9949      <para>If a third party can pretend to be an authorized client, it can
9950       simply access data it should not have access to. Typically this can
9951       happen through insecure password management. <acronym>SSL</acronym> uses
9952       client certificates to prevent this, by making sure that only holders
9953       of valid certificates can access the server.
9954      </para>
9955     </listitem>
9956    </varlistentry>
9957   </variablelist>
9958  </para>
9959
9960  <para>
9961   For a connection to be known SSL-secured, SSL usage must be configured
9962   on <emphasis>both the client and the server</emphasis> before the connection
9963   is made. If it is only configured on the server, the client may end up
9964   sending sensitive information (e.g., passwords) before
9965   it knows that the server requires high security. In libpq, secure
9966   connections can be ensured
9967   by setting the <literal>sslmode</literal> parameter to <literal>verify-full</literal> or
9968   <literal>verify-ca</literal>, and providing the system with a root certificate to
9969   verify against. This is analogous to using an <literal>https</literal>
9970   <acronym>URL</acronym> for encrypted web browsing.
9971  </para>
9972
9973  <para>
9974   Once the server has been authenticated, the client can pass sensitive data.
9975   This means that up until this point, the client does not need to know if
9976   certificates will be used for authentication, making it safe to specify that
9977   only in the server configuration.
9978  </para>
9979
9980  <para>
9981   All <acronym>SSL</acronym> options carry overhead in the form of encryption and
9982   key-exchange, so there is a trade-off that has to be made between performance
9983   and security. <xref linkend="libpq-ssl-sslmode-statements"/>
9984   illustrates the risks the different <literal>sslmode</literal> values
9985   protect against, and what statement they make about security and overhead.
9986  </para>
9987
9988  <table id="libpq-ssl-sslmode-statements">
9989   <title>SSL Mode Descriptions</title>
9990   <tgroup cols="4">
9991    <colspec colname="col1" colwidth="1*"/>
9992    <colspec colname="col2" colwidth="1*"/>
9993    <colspec colname="col3" colwidth="1*"/>
9994    <colspec colname="col4" colwidth="2*"/>
9995    <thead>
9996     <row>
9997      <entry><literal>sslmode</literal></entry>
9998      <entry>Eavesdropping protection</entry>
9999      <entry><acronym>MITM</acronym> protection</entry>
10000      <entry>Statement</entry>
10001     </row>
10002    </thead>
10003
10004    <tbody>
10005     <row>
10006      <entry><literal>disable</literal></entry>
10007      <entry>No</entry>
10008      <entry>No</entry>
10009      <entry>I don't care about security, and I don't want to pay the overhead
10010       of encryption.
10011      </entry>
10012     </row>
10013
10014     <row>
10015      <entry><literal>allow</literal></entry>
10016      <entry>Maybe</entry>
10017      <entry>No</entry>
10018      <entry>I don't care about security, but I will pay the overhead of
10019       encryption if the server insists on it.
10020      </entry>
10021     </row>
10022
10023     <row>
10024      <entry><literal>prefer</literal></entry>
10025      <entry>Maybe</entry>
10026      <entry>No</entry>
10027      <entry>I don't care about encryption, but I wish to pay the overhead of
10028       encryption if the server supports it.
10029      </entry>
10030     </row>
10031
10032     <row>
10033      <entry><literal>require</literal></entry>
10034      <entry>Yes</entry>
10035      <entry>No</entry>
10036      <entry>I want my data to be encrypted, and I accept the overhead. I trust
10037       that the network will make sure I always connect to the server I want.
10038      </entry>
10039     </row>
10040
10041     <row>
10042      <entry><literal>verify-ca</literal></entry>
10043      <entry>Yes</entry>
10044      <entry>Depends on CA policy</entry>
10045      <entry>I want my data encrypted, and I accept the overhead. I want to be
10046       sure that I connect to a server that I trust.
10047      </entry>
10048     </row>
10049
10050     <row>
10051      <entry><literal>verify-full</literal></entry>
10052       <entry>Yes</entry>
10053       <entry>Yes</entry>
10054       <entry>I want my data encrypted, and I accept the overhead. I want to be
10055        sure that I connect to a server I trust, and that it's the one I
10056        specify.
10057       </entry>
10058      </row>
10059
10060    </tbody>
10061   </tgroup>
10062  </table>
10063
10064  <para>
10065   The difference between <literal>verify-ca</literal> and <literal>verify-full</literal>
10066   depends on the policy of the root <acronym>CA</acronym>. If a public
10067   <acronym>CA</acronym> is used, <literal>verify-ca</literal> allows connections to a server
10068   that <emphasis>somebody else</emphasis> may have registered with the <acronym>CA</acronym>.
10069   In this case, <literal>verify-full</literal> should always be used. If
10070   a local <acronym>CA</acronym> is used, or even a self-signed certificate, using
10071   <literal>verify-ca</literal> often provides enough protection.
10072  </para>
10073
10074  <para>
10075   The default value for <literal>sslmode</literal> is <literal>prefer</literal>. As is shown
10076   in the table, this makes no sense from a security point of view, and it only
10077   promises performance overhead if possible. It is only provided as the default
10078   for backward compatibility, and is not recommended in secure deployments.
10079  </para>
10080
10081 </sect2>
10082
10083 <sect2 id="libpq-ssl-fileusage">
10084  <title>SSL Client File Usage</title>
10085
10086  <para>
10087   <xref linkend="libpq-ssl-file-usage"/> summarizes the files that are
10088   relevant to the SSL setup on the client.
10089  </para>
10090
10091  <table id="libpq-ssl-file-usage">
10092   <title>Libpq/Client SSL File Usage</title>
10093   <tgroup cols="3">
10094    <thead>
10095     <row>
10096      <entry>File</entry>
10097      <entry>Contents</entry>
10098      <entry>Effect</entry>
10099     </row>
10100    </thead>
10101
10102    <tbody>
10103
10104     <row>
10105      <entry><filename>~/.postgresql/postgresql.crt</filename></entry>
10106      <entry>client certificate</entry>
10107      <entry>sent to server</entry>
10108     </row>
10109
10110     <row>
10111      <entry><filename>~/.postgresql/postgresql.key</filename></entry>
10112      <entry>client private key</entry>
10113      <entry>proves client certificate sent by owner; does not indicate
10114      certificate owner is trustworthy</entry>
10115     </row>
10116
10117     <row>
10118      <entry><filename>~/.postgresql/root.crt</filename></entry>
10119      <entry>trusted certificate authorities</entry>
10120      <entry>checks that server certificate is signed by a trusted certificate
10121      authority</entry>
10122     </row>
10123
10124     <row>
10125      <entry><filename>~/.postgresql/root.crl</filename></entry>
10126      <entry>certificates revoked by certificate authorities</entry>
10127      <entry>server certificate must not be on this list</entry>
10128     </row>
10129
10130    </tbody>
10131   </tgroup>
10132  </table>
10133 </sect2>
10134
10135 <sect2 id="libpq-ssl-initialize">
10136  <title>SSL Library Initialization</title>
10137
10138  <para>
10139   Applications which need to be compatible with older versions of
10140   <productname>PostgreSQL</productname>, using <productname>OpenSSL</productname>
10141   version 1.0.2 or older, need to initialize the SSL library before using it.
10142   Applications which initialize <literal>libssl</literal> and/or
10143   <literal>libcrypto</literal> libraries should call
10144   <xref linkend="libpq-PQinitOpenSSL"/> to tell <application>libpq</application>
10145   that the <literal>libssl</literal> and/or <literal>libcrypto</literal> libraries
10146   have been initialized by your application, so that
10147   <application>libpq</application> will not also initialize those libraries.
10148   However, this is unnecessary when using <productname>OpenSSL</productname>
10149   version 1.1.0 or later, as duplicate initializations are no longer problematic.
10150  </para>
10151  <para>
10152   Refer to the documentation for the version of <productname>PostgreSQL</productname>
10153   that you are targeting for details on their use.
10154  </para>
10155
10156  <para>
10157   <variablelist>
10158    <varlistentry id="libpq-PQinitOpenSSL">
10159     <term><function>PQinitOpenSSL</function><indexterm><primary>PQinitOpenSSL</primary></indexterm></term>
10160
10161     <listitem>
10162      <para>
10163       Allows applications to select which security libraries to initialize.
10164<synopsis>
10165void PQinitOpenSSL(int do_ssl, int do_crypto);
10166</synopsis>
10167      </para>
10168
10169      <para>
10170       This function is deprecated and only present for backwards compatibility,
10171       it does nothing.
10172      </para>
10173     </listitem>
10174    </varlistentry>
10175
10176    <varlistentry id="libpq-PQinitSSL">
10177     <term><function>PQinitSSL</function><indexterm><primary>PQinitSSL</primary></indexterm></term><listitem>
10178      <para>
10179       Allows applications to select which security libraries to initialize.
10180<synopsis>
10181void PQinitSSL(int do_ssl);
10182</synopsis>
10183      </para>
10184
10185      <para>
10186       This function is equivalent to
10187       <literal>PQinitOpenSSL(do_ssl, do_ssl)</literal>.
10188       This function is deprecated and only present for backwards compatibility,
10189       it does nothing.
10190      </para>
10191
10192      <para>
10193       <xref linkend="libpq-PQinitSSL"/> and <xref linkend="libpq-PQinitOpenSSL"/>
10194       are maintained for backwards compatibility, but are no longer required
10195       since <productname>PostgreSQL</productname> 18.
10196       <xref linkend="libpq-PQinitSSL"/> has been present since
10197       <productname>PostgreSQL</productname> 8.0, while <xref linkend="libpq-PQinitOpenSSL"/>
10198       was added in <productname>PostgreSQL</productname> 8.4, so <xref linkend="libpq-PQinitSSL"/>
10199       might be preferable for applications that need to work with older
10200       versions of <application>libpq</application>.
10201      </para>
10202     </listitem>
10203    </varlistentry>
10204   </variablelist>
10205  </para>
10206 </sect2>
10207
10208 </sect1>
10209
10210 <sect1 id="libpq-oauth">
10211  <title>OAuth Support</title>
10212
10213  <para>
10214   <application>libpq</application> implements support for the OAuth v2 Device Authorization client flow,
10215   documented in
10216   <ulink url="https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc8628">RFC 8628</ulink>,
10217   as an optional module. See the <link linkend="configure-option-with-libcurl">
10218   installation documentation</link> for information on how to enable support
10219   for Device Authorization as a builtin flow.
10220  </para>
10221  <para>
10222   When support is enabled and the optional module installed, <application>libpq</application>
10223   will use the builtin flow by default if the server
10224   <link linkend="auth-oauth">requests a bearer token</link> during
10225   authentication. This flow can be utilized even if the system running the
10226   client application does not have a usable web browser, for example when
10227   running a client via <acronym>SSH</acronym>.
10228  </para>
10229  <para>
10230   The builtin flow will, by default, print a URL to visit and a user code to
10231   enter there:
10232<programlisting>
10233$ psql 'dbname=postgres oauth_issuer=https://example.com oauth_client_id=...'
10234Visit https://example.com/device and enter the code: ABCD-EFGH
10235</programlisting>
10236   (This prompt may be
10237   <link linkend="libpq-oauth-authdata-prompt-oauth-device">customized</link>.)
10238   The user will then log into their OAuth provider, which will ask whether
10239   to allow libpq and the server to perform actions on their behalf. It is always
10240   a good idea to carefully review the URL and permissions displayed, to ensure
10241   they match expectations, before continuing. Permissions should not be given
10242   to untrusted third parties.
10243  </para>
10244  <para>
10245   Client applications may implement their own flows to customize interaction
10246   and integration with applications. See <xref linkend="libpq-oauth-authdata-hooks"/>
10247   for more information on how add a custom flow to <application>libpq</application>.
10248  </para>
10249  <para>
10250   For an OAuth client flow to be usable, the connection string must at minimum
10251   contain <xref linkend="libpq-connect-oauth-issuer"/> and
10252   <xref linkend="libpq-connect-oauth-client-id"/>. (These settings are
10253   determined by your organization's OAuth provider.) The builtin flow
10254   additionally requires the OAuth authorization server to publish a device
10255   authorization endpoint.
10256  </para>
10257
10258  <note>
10259   <para>
10260    The builtin Device Authorization flow is not currently supported on Windows.
10261    Custom client flows may still be implemented.
10262   </para>
10263  </note>
10264
10265  <sect2 id="libpq-oauth-authdata-hooks">
10266   <title>Authdata Hooks</title>
10267
10268   <para>
10269    The behavior of the OAuth flow may be modified or replaced by a client using
10270    the following hook API:
10271
10272    <variablelist>
10273     <varlistentry id="libpq-PQsetAuthDataHook">
10274      <term><function>PQsetAuthDataHook</function><indexterm><primary>PQsetAuthDataHook</primary></indexterm></term>
10275
10276      <listitem>
10277       <para>
10278        Sets the <symbol>PGauthDataHook</symbol>, overriding
10279        <application>libpq</application>'s handling of one or more aspects of
10280        its OAuth client flow.
10281<synopsis>
10282void PQsetAuthDataHook(PQauthDataHook_type hook);
10283</synopsis>
10284        If <replaceable>hook</replaceable> is <literal>NULL</literal>, the
10285        default handler will be reinstalled. Otherwise, the application passes
10286        a pointer to a callback function with the signature:
10287<programlisting>
10288int hook_fn(PGauthData type, PGconn *conn, void *data);
10289</programlisting>
10290        which <application>libpq</application> will call when an action is
10291        required of the application. <replaceable>type</replaceable> describes
10292        the request being made, <replaceable>conn</replaceable> is the
10293        connection handle being authenticated, and <replaceable>data</replaceable>
10294        points to request-specific metadata. The contents of this pointer are
10295        determined by <replaceable>type</replaceable>; see
10296        <xref linkend="libpq-oauth-authdata-hooks-types"/> for the supported
10297        list.
10298       </para>
10299       <para>
10300        Hooks can be chained together to allow cooperative and/or fallback
10301        behavior. In general, a hook implementation should examine the incoming
10302        <replaceable>type</replaceable> (and, potentially, the request metadata
10303        and/or the settings for the particular <replaceable>conn</replaceable>
10304        in use) to decide whether or not to handle a specific piece of authdata.
10305        If not, it should delegate to the previous hook in the chain
10306        (retrievable via <function>PQgetAuthDataHook</function>).
10307       </para>
10308       <para>
10309        Success is indicated by returning an integer greater than zero.
10310        Returning a negative integer signals an error condition and abandons the
10311        connection attempt. (A zero value is reserved for the default
10312        implementation.)
10313       </para>
10314      </listitem>
10315     </varlistentry>
10316
10317     <varlistentry id="libpq-PQgetAuthDataHook">
10318      <term><function>PQgetAuthDataHook</function><indexterm><primary>PQgetAuthDataHook</primary></indexterm></term>
10319
10320      <listitem>
10321       <para>
10322        Retrieves the current value of <symbol>PGauthDataHook</symbol>.
10323<synopsis>
10324PQauthDataHook_type PQgetAuthDataHook(void);
10325</synopsis>
10326        At initialization time (before the first call to
10327        <function>PQsetAuthDataHook</function>), this function will return
10328        <symbol>PQdefaultAuthDataHook</symbol>.
10329       </para>
10330      </listitem>
10331     </varlistentry>
10332    </variablelist>
10333   </para>
10334
10335   <sect3 id="libpq-oauth-authdata-hooks-types">
10336    <title>Hook Types</title>
10337    <para>
10338     The following <symbol>PGauthData</symbol> types and their corresponding
10339     <replaceable>data</replaceable> structures are defined:
10340
10341     <variablelist>
10342      <varlistentry id="libpq-oauth-authdata-prompt-oauth-device">
10343       <term>
10344        <symbol>PQAUTHDATA_PROMPT_OAUTH_DEVICE</symbol>
10345        <indexterm><primary>PQAUTHDATA_PROMPT_OAUTH_DEVICE</primary></indexterm>
10346       </term>
10347       <listitem>
10348        <para>
10349         Replaces the default user prompt during the builtin device
10350         authorization client flow. <replaceable>data</replaceable> points to
10351         an instance of <symbol>PGpromptOAuthDevice</symbol>:
10352<synopsis>
10353typedef struct _PGpromptOAuthDevice
10354{
10355    const char *verification_uri;   /* verification URI to visit */
10356    const char *user_code;          /* user code to enter */
10357    const char *verification_uri_complete;  /* optional combination of URI and
10358                                             * code, or NULL */
10359    int         expires_in;         /* seconds until user code expires */
10360} PGpromptOAuthDevice;
10361</synopsis>
10362        </para>
10363        <para>
10364         The OAuth Device Authorization flow which
10365         <link linkend="configure-option-with-libcurl">can be included</link>
10366         in <application>libpq</application>
10367         requires the end user to visit a URL with a browser, then enter a code
10368         which permits <application>libpq</application> to connect to the server
10369         on their behalf. The default prompt simply prints the
10370         <literal>verification_uri</literal> and <literal>user_code</literal>
10371         on standard error. Replacement implementations may display this
10372         information using any preferred method, for example with a GUI.
10373        </para>
10374        <para>
10375         This callback is only invoked during the builtin device
10376         authorization flow. If the application installs a
10377         <link linkend="libpq-oauth-authdata-oauth-bearer-token">custom OAuth
10378         flow</link>, or <application>libpq</application> was not built with
10379         support for the builtin flow, this authdata type will not be used.
10380        </para>
10381        <para>
10382         If a non-NULL <structfield>verification_uri_complete</structfield> is
10383         provided, it may optionally be used for non-textual verification (for
10384         example, by displaying a QR code). The URL and user code should still
10385         be displayed to the end user in this case, because the code will be
10386         manually confirmed by the provider, and the URL lets users continue
10387         even if they can't use the non-textual method. For more information,
10388         see section 3.3.1 in
10389         <ulink url="https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc8628#section-3.3.1">RFC 8628</ulink>.
10390        </para>
10391       </listitem>
10392      </varlistentry>
10393
10394      <varlistentry id="libpq-oauth-authdata-oauth-bearer-token">
10395       <term>
10396        <symbol>PQAUTHDATA_OAUTH_BEARER_TOKEN</symbol>
10397        <indexterm><primary>PQAUTHDATA_OAUTH_BEARER_TOKEN</primary></indexterm>
10398       </term>
10399       <listitem>
10400        <para>
10401         Adds a custom implementation of a flow, replacing the builtin flow if
10402         it is <link linkend="configure-option-with-libcurl">installed</link>.
10403         The hook should either directly return a Bearer token for the current
10404         user/issuer/scope combination, if one is available without blocking, or
10405         else set up an asynchronous callback to retrieve one.
10406        </para>
10407        <para>
10408         <replaceable>data</replaceable> points to an instance
10409         of <symbol>PGoauthBearerRequest</symbol>, which should be filled in
10410         by the implementation:
10411<synopsis>
10412typedef struct PGoauthBearerRequest
10413{
10414    /* Hook inputs (constant across all calls) */
10415    const char *openid_configuration; /* OIDC discovery URL */
10416    const char *scope;                /* required scope(s), or NULL */
10417
10418    /* Hook outputs */
10419
10420    /*
10421     * Callback implementing a custom asynchronous OAuth flow. The signature is
10422     * platform-dependent: PQ_SOCKTYPE is SOCKET on Windows, and int everywhere
10423     * else.
10424     */
10425    PostgresPollingStatusType (*async) (PGconn *conn,
10426                                        struct PGoauthBearerRequest *request,
10427                                        PQ_SOCKTYPE *altsock);
10428
10429    /* Callback to clean up custom allocations. */
10430    void        (*cleanup) (PGconn *conn, struct PGoauthBearerRequest *request);
10431
10432    char       *token;   /* acquired Bearer token */
10433    void       *user;    /* hook-defined allocated data */
10434} PGoauthBearerRequest;
10435</synopsis>
10436        </para>
10437        <para>
10438         Two pieces of information are provided to the hook by
10439         <application>libpq</application>:
10440         <replaceable>openid_configuration</replaceable> contains the URL of an
10441         OAuth discovery document describing the authorization server's
10442         supported flows, and <replaceable>scope</replaceable> contains a
10443         (possibly empty) space-separated list of OAuth scopes which are
10444         required to access the server. Either or both may be
10445         <literal>NULL</literal> to indicate that the information was not
10446         discoverable. (In this case, implementations may be able to establish
10447         the requirements using some other preconfigured knowledge, or they may
10448         choose to fail.)
10449        </para>
10450        <para>
10451         The final output of the hook is <replaceable>token</replaceable>, which
10452         must point to a valid Bearer token for use on the connection. (This
10453         token should be issued by the
10454         <xref linkend="libpq-connect-oauth-issuer"/> and hold the requested
10455         scopes, or the connection will be rejected by the server's validator
10456         module.) The allocated token string must remain valid until
10457         <application>libpq</application> is finished connecting; the hook
10458         should set a <replaceable>cleanup</replaceable> callback which will be
10459         called when <application>libpq</application> no longer requires it.
10460        </para>
10461        <para>
10462         If an implementation cannot immediately produce a
10463         <replaceable>token</replaceable> during the initial call to the hook,
10464         it should set the <replaceable>async</replaceable> callback to handle
10465         nonblocking communication with the authorization server.
10466         <footnote>
10467          <para>
10468           Performing blocking operations during the
10469           <symbol>PQAUTHDATA_OAUTH_BEARER_TOKEN</symbol> hook callback will
10470           interfere with nonblocking connection APIs such as
10471           <function>PQconnectPoll</function> and prevent concurrent connections
10472           from making progress. Applications which only ever use the
10473           synchronous connection primitives, such as
10474           <function>PQconnectdb</function>, may synchronously retrieve a token
10475           during the hook instead of implementing the
10476           <replaceable>async</replaceable> callback, but they will necessarily
10477           be limited to one connection at a time.
10478          </para>
10479         </footnote>
10480         This will be called to begin the flow immediately upon return from the
10481         hook. When the callback cannot make further progress without blocking,
10482         it should return either <symbol>PGRES_POLLING_READING</symbol> or
10483         <symbol>PGRES_POLLING_WRITING</symbol> after setting
10484         <literal>*altsock</literal> to the file descriptor that will be marked
10485         ready to read/write when progress can be made again. (This descriptor
10486         is then provided to the top-level polling loop via
10487         <function>PQsocket()</function>.) Return <symbol>PGRES_POLLING_OK</symbol>
10488         after setting <replaceable>token</replaceable> when the flow is
10489         complete, or <symbol>PGRES_POLLING_FAILED</symbol> to indicate failure.
10490        </para>
10491        <para>
10492         Implementations may wish to store additional data for bookkeeping
10493         across calls to the <replaceable>async</replaceable> and
10494         <replaceable>cleanup</replaceable> callbacks. The
10495         <replaceable>user</replaceable> pointer is provided for this purpose;
10496         <application>libpq</application> will not touch its contents and the
10497         application may use it at its convenience. (Remember to free any
10498         allocations during token cleanup.)
10499        </para>
10500       </listitem>
10501      </varlistentry>
10502     </variablelist>
10503    </para>
10504   </sect3>
10505  </sect2>
10506
10507  <sect2 id="libpq-oauth-debugging">
10508   <title>Debugging and Developer Settings</title>
10509
10510   <para>
10511    A "dangerous debugging mode" may be enabled by setting the environment
10512    variable <envar>PGOAUTHDEBUG=UNSAFE</envar>. This functionality is provided
10513    for ease of local development and testing only. It does several things that
10514    you will not want a production system to do:
10515
10516    <itemizedlist spacing="compact">
10517     <listitem>
10518      <para>
10519       permits the use of unencrypted HTTP during the OAuth provider exchange
10520      </para>
10521     </listitem>
10522     <listitem>
10523      <para>
10524       allows the system's trusted CA list to be completely replaced using the
10525       <envar>PGOAUTHCAFILE</envar> environment variable
10526      </para>
10527     </listitem>
10528     <listitem>
10529      <para>
10530       prints HTTP traffic (containing several critical secrets) to standard
10531       error during the OAuth flow
10532      </para>
10533     </listitem>
10534     <listitem>
10535      <para>
10536       permits the use of zero-second retry intervals, which can cause the
10537       client to busy-loop and pointlessly consume CPU
10538      </para>
10539     </listitem>
10540    </itemizedlist>
10541   </para>
10542   <warning>
10543    <para>
10544     Do not share the output of the OAuth flow traffic with third parties. It
10545     contains secrets that can be used to attack your clients and servers.
10546    </para>
10547   </warning>
10548  </sect2>
10549 </sect1>
10550
10551
10552 <sect1 id="libpq-threading">
10553  <title>Behavior in Threaded Programs</title>
10554
10555  <indexterm zone="libpq-threading">
10556   <primary>threads</primary>
10557   <secondary>with libpq</secondary>
10558  </indexterm>
10559
10560  <para>
10561   As of version 17, <application>libpq</application> is always reentrant and thread-safe.
10562   However, one restriction is that no two threads attempt to manipulate
10563   the same <structname>PGconn</structname> object at the same time. In particular,
10564   you cannot issue concurrent commands from different threads through
10565   the same connection object. (If you need to run concurrent commands,
10566   use multiple connections.)
10567  </para>
10568
10569  <para>
10570   <structname>PGresult</structname> objects are normally read-only after creation,
10571   and so can be passed around freely between threads.  However, if you use
10572   any of the <structname>PGresult</structname>-modifying functions described in
10573   <xref linkend="libpq-misc"/> or <xref linkend="libpq-events"/>, it's up
10574   to you to avoid concurrent operations on the same <structname>PGresult</structname>,
10575   too.
10576  </para>
10577
10578  <para>
10579   In earlier versions, <application>libpq</application> could be compiled
10580   with or without thread support, depending on compiler options. This
10581   function allows the querying of <application>libpq</application>'s
10582   thread-safe status:
10583  </para>
10584
10585  <variablelist>
10586   <varlistentry id="libpq-PQisthreadsafe">
10587    <term><function>PQisthreadsafe</function><indexterm><primary>PQisthreadsafe</primary></indexterm></term>
10588
10589    <listitem>
10590     <para>
10591      Returns the thread safety status of the
10592      <application>libpq</application> library.
10593<synopsis>
10594int PQisthreadsafe();
10595</synopsis>
10596     </para>
10597
10598     <para>
10599      Returns 1 if the <application>libpq</application> is thread-safe
10600      and 0 if it is not. Always returns 1 on version 17 and above.
10601     </para>
10602    </listitem>
10603   </varlistentry>
10604  </variablelist>
10605
10606  <para>
10607   The deprecated functions <xref linkend="libpq-PQrequestCancel"/> and
10608   <xref linkend="libpq-PQoidStatus"/> are not thread-safe and should not be
10609   used in multithread programs.  <xref linkend="libpq-PQrequestCancel"/>
10610   can be replaced by <xref linkend="libpq-PQcancelBlocking"/>.
10611   <xref linkend="libpq-PQoidStatus"/> can be replaced by
10612   <xref linkend="libpq-PQoidValue"/>.
10613  </para>
10614
10615  <para>
10616   If you are using Kerberos inside your application (in addition to inside
10617   <application>libpq</application>), you will need to do locking around
10618   Kerberos calls because Kerberos functions are not thread-safe.  See
10619   function <function>PQregisterThreadLock</function> in the
10620   <application>libpq</application> source code for a way to do cooperative
10621   locking between <application>libpq</application> and your application.
10622  </para>
10623
10624  <para>
10625   Similarly, if you are using <productname>Curl</productname> inside your application,
10626   <emphasis>and</emphasis> you do not already
10627   <ulink url="https://curl.se/libcurl/c/curl_global_init.html">initialize
10628   libcurl globally</ulink> before starting new threads, you will need to
10629   cooperatively lock (again via <function>PQregisterThreadLock</function>)
10630   around any code that may initialize libcurl. This restriction is lifted for
10631   more recent versions of <productname>Curl</productname> that are built to support thread-safe
10632   initialization; those builds can be identified by the advertisement of a
10633   <literal>threadsafe</literal> feature in their version metadata.
10634  </para>
10635 </sect1>
10636
10637
10638 <sect1 id="libpq-build">
10639  <title>Building <application>libpq</application> Programs</title>
10640
10641  <indexterm zone="libpq-build">
10642   <primary>compiling</primary>
10643   <secondary>libpq applications</secondary>
10644  </indexterm>
10645
10646  <para>
10647   To build (i.e., compile and link) a program using
10648   <application>libpq</application> you need to do all of the following
10649   things:
10650
10651   <itemizedlist>
10652    <listitem>
10653     <para>
10654      Include the <filename>libpq-fe.h</filename> header file:
10655<programlisting>
10656#include &lt;libpq-fe.h&gt;
10657</programlisting>
10658      If you failed to do that then you will normally get error messages
10659      from your compiler similar to:
10660<screen>
10661foo.c: In function `main':
10662foo.c:34: `PGconn' undeclared (first use in this function)
10663foo.c:35: `PGresult' undeclared (first use in this function)
10664foo.c:54: `CONNECTION_BAD' undeclared (first use in this function)
10665foo.c:68: `PGRES_COMMAND_OK' undeclared (first use in this function)
10666foo.c:95: `PGRES_TUPLES_OK' undeclared (first use in this function)
10667</screen>
10668     </para>
10669    </listitem>
10670
10671    <listitem>
10672     <para>
10673      Point your compiler to the directory where the <productname>PostgreSQL</productname> header
10674      files were installed, by supplying the
10675      <literal>-I<replaceable>directory</replaceable></literal> option
10676      to your compiler.  (In some cases the compiler will look into
10677      the directory in question by default, so you can omit this
10678      option.)  For instance, your compile command line could look
10679      like:
10680<programlisting>
10681cc -c -I/usr/local/pgsql/include testprog.c
10682</programlisting>
10683      If you are using makefiles then add the option to the
10684      <varname>CPPFLAGS</varname> variable:
10685<programlisting>
10686CPPFLAGS += -I/usr/local/pgsql/include
10687</programlisting>
10688     </para>
10689
10690     <para>
10691      If there is any chance that your program might be compiled by
10692      other users then you should not hardcode the directory location
10693      like that.  Instead, you can run the utility
10694      <command>pg_config</command><indexterm><primary>pg_config</primary><secondary
10695      sortas="libpq">with libpq</secondary></indexterm> to find out where the header
10696      files are on the local system:
10697<screen>
10698<prompt>$</prompt> pg_config --includedir
10699<computeroutput>/usr/local/include</computeroutput>
10700</screen>
10701     </para>
10702
10703     <para>
10704      If you
10705      have <command>pkg-config</command><indexterm><primary>pkg-config</primary><secondary sortas="libpq">with
10706      libpq</secondary></indexterm> installed, you can run instead:
10707<screen>
10708<prompt>$</prompt> pkg-config --cflags libpq
10709<computeroutput>-I/usr/local/include</computeroutput>
10710</screen>
10711      Note that this will already include the <option>-I</option> in front of
10712      the path.
10713     </para>
10714
10715     <para>
10716      Failure to specify the correct option to the compiler will
10717      result in an error message such as:
10718<screen>
10719testlibpq.c:8:22: libpq-fe.h: No such file or directory
10720</screen>
10721     </para>
10722    </listitem>
10723
10724    <listitem>
10725     <para>
10726      When linking the final program, specify the option
10727      <literal>-lpq</literal> so that the <application>libpq</application>
10728      library gets pulled in, as well as the option
10729      <literal>-L<replaceable>directory</replaceable></literal> to point
10730      the compiler to the directory where the
10731      <application>libpq</application> library resides.  (Again, the
10732      compiler will search some directories by default.)  For maximum
10733      portability, put the <option>-L</option> option before the
10734      <option>-lpq</option> option.  For example:
10735<programlisting>
10736cc -o testprog testprog1.o testprog2.o -L/usr/local/pgsql/lib -lpq
10737</programlisting>
10738     </para>
10739
10740     <para>
10741      You can find out the library directory using
10742      <command>pg_config</command> as well:
10743<screen>
10744<prompt>$</prompt> pg_config --libdir
10745<computeroutput>/usr/local/pgsql/lib</computeroutput>
10746</screen>
10747     </para>
10748
10749     <para>
10750      Or again use <command>pkg-config</command>:
10751<screen>
10752<prompt>$</prompt> pkg-config --libs libpq
10753<computeroutput>-L/usr/local/pgsql/lib -lpq</computeroutput>
10754</screen>
10755      Note again that this prints the full options, not only the path.
10756     </para>
10757
10758     <para>
10759      Error messages that point to problems in this area could look like
10760      the following:
10761<screen>
10762testlibpq.o: In function `main':
10763testlibpq.o(.text+0x60): undefined reference to `PQsetdbLogin'
10764testlibpq.o(.text+0x71): undefined reference to `PQstatus'
10765testlibpq.o(.text+0xa4): undefined reference to `PQerrorMessage'
10766</screen>
10767      This means you forgot <option>-lpq</option>.
10768<screen>
10769/usr/bin/ld: cannot find -lpq
10770</screen>
10771      This means you forgot the <option>-L</option> option or did not
10772      specify the right directory.
10773     </para>
10774    </listitem>
10775   </itemizedlist>
10776  </para>
10777
10778 </sect1>
10779
10780
10781 <sect1 id="libpq-example">
10782  <title>Example Programs</title>
10783
10784  <para>
10785   These examples and others can be found in the
10786   directory <filename>src/test/examples</filename> in the source code
10787   distribution.
10788  </para>
10789
10790  <example id="libpq-example-1">
10791   <title><application>libpq</application> Example Program 1</title>
10792
10793<programlisting>
10794<![CDATA[
10795/*
10796 * src/test/examples/testlibpq.c
10797 *
10798 *
10799 * testlibpq.c
10800 *
10801 *      Test the C version of libpq, the PostgreSQL frontend library.
10802 */
10803#include <stdio.h>
10804#include <stdlib.h>
10805#include "libpq-fe.h"
10806
10807static void
10808exit_nicely(PGconn *conn)
10809{
10810    PQfinish(conn);
10811    exit(1);
10812}
10813
10814int
10815main(int argc, char **argv)
10816{
10817    const char *conninfo;
10818    PGconn     *conn;
10819    PGresult   *res;
10820    int         nFields;
10821    int         i,
10822                j;
10823
10824    /*
10825     * If the user supplies a parameter on the command line, use it as the
10826     * conninfo string; otherwise default to setting dbname=postgres and using
10827     * environment variables or defaults for all other connection parameters.
10828     */
10829    if (argc > 1)
10830        conninfo = argv[1];
10831    else
10832        conninfo = "dbname = postgres";
10833
10834    /* Make a connection to the database */
10835    conn = PQconnectdb(conninfo);
10836
10837    /* Check to see that the backend connection was successfully made */
10838    if (PQstatus(conn) != CONNECTION_OK)
10839    {
10840        fprintf(stderr, "%s", PQerrorMessage(conn));
10841        exit_nicely(conn);
10842    }
10843
10844    /* Set always-secure search path, so malicious users can't take control. */
10845    res = PQexec(conn,
10846                 "SELECT pg_catalog.set_config('search_path', '', false)");
10847    if (PQresultStatus(res) != PGRES_TUPLES_OK)
10848    {
10849        fprintf(stderr, "SET failed: %s", PQerrorMessage(conn));
10850        PQclear(res);
10851        exit_nicely(conn);
10852    }
10853
10854    /*
10855     * Should PQclear PGresult whenever it is no longer needed to avoid memory
10856     * leaks
10857     */
10858    PQclear(res);
10859
10860    /*
10861     * Our test case here involves using a cursor, for which we must be inside
10862     * a transaction block.  We could do the whole thing with a single
10863     * PQexec() of "select * from pg_database", but that's too trivial to make
10864     * a good example.
10865     */
10866
10867    /* Start a transaction block */
10868    res = PQexec(conn, "BEGIN");
10869    if (PQresultStatus(res) != PGRES_COMMAND_OK)
10870    {
10871        fprintf(stderr, "BEGIN command failed: %s", PQerrorMessage(conn));
10872        PQclear(res);
10873        exit_nicely(conn);
10874    }
10875    PQclear(res);
10876
10877    /*
10878     * Fetch rows from pg_database, the system catalog of databases
10879     */
10880    res = PQexec(conn, "DECLARE myportal CURSOR FOR select * from pg_database");
10881    if (PQresultStatus(res) != PGRES_COMMAND_OK)
10882    {
10883        fprintf(stderr, "DECLARE CURSOR failed: %s", PQerrorMessage(conn));
10884        PQclear(res);
10885        exit_nicely(conn);
10886    }
10887    PQclear(res);
10888
10889    res = PQexec(conn, "FETCH ALL in myportal");
10890    if (PQresultStatus(res) != PGRES_TUPLES_OK)
10891    {
10892        fprintf(stderr, "FETCH ALL failed: %s", PQerrorMessage(conn));
10893        PQclear(res);
10894        exit_nicely(conn);
10895    }
10896
10897    /* first, print out the attribute names */
10898    nFields = PQnfields(res);
10899    for (i = 0; i < nFields; i++)
10900        printf("%-15s", PQfname(res, i));
10901    printf("\n\n");
10902
10903    /* next, print out the rows */
10904    for (i = 0; i < PQntuples(res); i++)
10905    {
10906        for (j = 0; j < nFields; j++)
10907            printf("%-15s", PQgetvalue(res, i, j));
10908        printf("\n");
10909    }
10910
10911    PQclear(res);
10912
10913    /* close the portal ... we don't bother to check for errors ... */
10914    res = PQexec(conn, "CLOSE myportal");
10915    PQclear(res);
10916
10917    /* end the transaction */
10918    res = PQexec(conn, "END");
10919    PQclear(res);
10920
10921    /* close the connection to the database and cleanup */
10922    PQfinish(conn);
10923
10924    return 0;
10925}
10926]]>
10927</programlisting>
10928  </example>
10929
10930  <example id="libpq-example-2">
10931   <title><application>libpq</application> Example Program 2</title>
10932
10933<programlisting>
10934<![CDATA[
10935/*
10936 * src/test/examples/testlibpq2.c
10937 *
10938 *
10939 * testlibpq2.c
10940 *      Test of the asynchronous notification interface
10941 *
10942 * Start this program, then from psql in another window do
10943 *   NOTIFY TBL2;
10944 * Repeat four times to get this program to exit.
10945 *
10946 * Or, if you want to get fancy, try this:
