10947 * populate a database with the following commands
10948 * (provided in src/test/examples/testlibpq2.sql):
10949 *
10950 *   CREATE SCHEMA TESTLIBPQ2;
10951 *   SET search_path = TESTLIBPQ2;
10952 *   CREATE TABLE TBL1 (i int4);
10953 *   CREATE TABLE TBL2 (i int4);
10954 *   CREATE RULE r1 AS ON INSERT TO TBL1 DO
10955 *     (INSERT INTO TBL2 VALUES (new.i); NOTIFY TBL2);
10956 *
10957 * Start this program, then from psql do this four times:
10958 *
10959 *   INSERT INTO TESTLIBPQ2.TBL1 VALUES (10);
10960 */
10961
10962#ifdef WIN32
10963#include <windows.h>
10964#endif
10965#include <stdio.h>
10966#include <stdlib.h>
10967#include <string.h>
10968#include <errno.h>
10969#include <sys/select.h>
10970#include <sys/time.h>
10971#include <sys/types.h>
10972
10973#include "libpq-fe.h"
10974
10975static void
10976exit_nicely(PGconn *conn)
10977{
10978    PQfinish(conn);
10979    exit(1);
10980}
10981
10982int
10983main(int argc, char **argv)
10984{
10985    const char *conninfo;
10986    PGconn     *conn;
10987    PGresult   *res;
10988    PGnotify   *notify;
10989    int         nnotifies;
10990
10991    /*
10992     * If the user supplies a parameter on the command line, use it as the
10993     * conninfo string; otherwise default to setting dbname=postgres and using
10994     * environment variables or defaults for all other connection parameters.
10995     */
10996    if (argc > 1)
10997        conninfo = argv[1];
10998    else
10999        conninfo = "dbname = postgres";
11000
11001    /* Make a connection to the database */
11002    conn = PQconnectdb(conninfo);
11003
11004    /* Check to see that the backend connection was successfully made */
11005    if (PQstatus(conn) != CONNECTION_OK)
11006    {
11007        fprintf(stderr, "%s", PQerrorMessage(conn));
11008        exit_nicely(conn);
11009    }
11010
11011    /* Set always-secure search path, so malicious users can't take control. */
11012    res = PQexec(conn,
11013                 "SELECT pg_catalog.set_config('search_path', '', false)");
11014    if (PQresultStatus(res) != PGRES_TUPLES_OK)
11015    {
11016        fprintf(stderr, "SET failed: %s", PQerrorMessage(conn));
11017        PQclear(res);
11018        exit_nicely(conn);
11019    }
11020
11021    /*
11022     * Should PQclear PGresult whenever it is no longer needed to avoid memory
11023     * leaks
11024     */
11025    PQclear(res);
11026
11027    /*
11028     * Issue LISTEN command to enable notifications from the rule's NOTIFY.
11029     */
11030    res = PQexec(conn, "LISTEN TBL2");
11031    if (PQresultStatus(res) != PGRES_COMMAND_OK)
11032    {
11033        fprintf(stderr, "LISTEN command failed: %s", PQerrorMessage(conn));
11034        PQclear(res);
11035        exit_nicely(conn);
11036    }
11037    PQclear(res);
11038
11039    /* Quit after four notifies are received. */
11040    nnotifies = 0;
11041    while (nnotifies < 4)
11042    {
11043        /*
11044         * Sleep until something happens on the connection.  We use select(2)
11045         * to wait for input, but you could also use poll() or similar
11046         * facilities.
11047         */
11048        int         sock;
11049        fd_set      input_mask;
11050
11051        sock = PQsocket(conn);
11052
11053        if (sock < 0)
11054            break;              /* shouldn't happen */
11055
11056        FD_ZERO(&input_mask);
11057        FD_SET(sock, &input_mask);
11058
11059        if (select(sock + 1, &input_mask, NULL, NULL, NULL) < 0)
11060        {
11061            fprintf(stderr, "select() failed: %s\n", strerror(errno));
11062            exit_nicely(conn);
11063        }
11064
11065        /* Now check for input */
11066        PQconsumeInput(conn);
11067        while ((notify = PQnotifies(conn)) != NULL)
11068        {
11069            fprintf(stderr,
11070                    "ASYNC NOTIFY of '%s' received from backend PID %d\n",
11071                    notify->relname, notify->be_pid);
11072            PQfreemem(notify);
11073            nnotifies++;
11074            PQconsumeInput(conn);
11075        }
11076    }
11077
11078    fprintf(stderr, "Done.\n");
11079
11080    /* close the connection to the database and cleanup */
11081    PQfinish(conn);
11082
11083    return 0;
11084}
11085]]>
11086</programlisting>
11087  </example>
11088
11089  <example id="libpq-example-3">
11090   <title><application>libpq</application> Example Program 3</title>
11091
11092<programlisting>
11093<![CDATA[
11094/*
11095 * src/test/examples/testlibpq3.c
11096 *
11097 *
11098 * testlibpq3.c
11099 *      Test out-of-line parameters and binary I/O.
11100 *
11101 * Before running this, populate a database with the following commands
11102 * (provided in src/test/examples/testlibpq3.sql):
11103 *
11104 * CREATE SCHEMA testlibpq3;
11105 * SET search_path = testlibpq3;
11106 * SET standard_conforming_strings = ON;
11107 * CREATE TABLE test1 (i int4, t text, b bytea);
11108 * INSERT INTO test1 values (1, 'joe''s place', '\000\001\002\003\004');
11109 * INSERT INTO test1 values (2, 'ho there', '\004\003\002\001\000');
11110 *
11111 * The expected output is:
11112 *
11113 * tuple 0: got
11114 *  i = (4 bytes) 1
11115 *  t = (11 bytes) 'joe's place'
11116 *  b = (5 bytes) \000\001\002\003\004
11117 *
11118 * tuple 0: got
11119 *  i = (4 bytes) 2
11120 *  t = (8 bytes) 'ho there'
11121 *  b = (5 bytes) \004\003\002\001\000
11122 */
11123
11124#ifdef WIN32
11125#include <windows.h>
11126#endif
11127
11128#include <stdio.h>
11129#include <stdlib.h>
11130#include <stdint.h>
11131#include <string.h>
11132#include <sys/types.h>
11133#include "libpq-fe.h"
11134
11135/* for ntohl/htonl */
11136#include <netinet/in.h>
11137#include <arpa/inet.h>
11138
11139
11140static void
11141exit_nicely(PGconn *conn)
11142{
11143    PQfinish(conn);
11144    exit(1);
11145}
11146
11147/*
11148 * This function prints a query result that is a binary-format fetch from
11149 * a table defined as in the comment above.  We split it out because the
11150 * main() function uses it twice.
11151 */
11152static void
11153show_binary_results(PGresult *res)
11154{
11155    int         i,
11156                j;
11157    int         i_fnum,
11158                t_fnum,
11159                b_fnum;
11160
11161    /* Use PQfnumber to avoid assumptions about field order in result */
11162    i_fnum = PQfnumber(res, "i");
11163    t_fnum = PQfnumber(res, "t");
11164    b_fnum = PQfnumber(res, "b");
11165
11166    for (i = 0; i < PQntuples(res); i++)
11167    {
11168        char       *iptr;
11169        char       *tptr;
11170        char       *bptr;
11171        int         blen;
11172        int         ival;
11173
11174        /* Get the field values (we ignore possibility they are null!) */
11175        iptr = PQgetvalue(res, i, i_fnum);
11176        tptr = PQgetvalue(res, i, t_fnum);
11177        bptr = PQgetvalue(res, i, b_fnum);
11178
11179        /*
11180         * The binary representation of INT4 is in network byte order, which
11181         * we'd better coerce to the local byte order.
11182         */
11183        ival = ntohl(*((uint32_t *) iptr));
11184
11185        /*
11186         * The binary representation of TEXT is, well, text, and since libpq
11187         * was nice enough to append a zero byte to it, it'll work just fine
11188         * as a C string.
11189         *
11190         * The binary representation of BYTEA is a bunch of bytes, which could
11191         * include embedded nulls so we have to pay attention to field length.
11192         */
11193        blen = PQgetlength(res, i, b_fnum);
11194
11195        printf("tuple %d: got\n", i);
11196        printf(" i = (%d bytes) %d\n",
11197               PQgetlength(res, i, i_fnum), ival);
11198        printf(" t = (%d bytes) '%s'\n",
11199               PQgetlength(res, i, t_fnum), tptr);
11200        printf(" b = (%d bytes) ", blen);
11201        for (j = 0; j < blen; j++)
11202            printf("\\%03o", bptr[j]);
11203        printf("\n\n");
11204    }
11205}
11206
11207int
11208main(int argc, char **argv)
11209{
11210    const char *conninfo;
11211    PGconn     *conn;
11212    PGresult   *res;
11213    const char *paramValues[1];
11214    int         paramLengths[1];
11215    int         paramFormats[1];
11216    uint32_t    binaryIntVal;
11217
11218    /*
11219     * If the user supplies a parameter on the command line, use it as the
11220     * conninfo string; otherwise default to setting dbname=postgres and using
11221     * environment variables or defaults for all other connection parameters.
11222     */
11223    if (argc > 1)
11224        conninfo = argv[1];
11225    else
11226        conninfo = "dbname = postgres";
11227
11228    /* Make a connection to the database */
11229    conn = PQconnectdb(conninfo);
11230
11231    /* Check to see that the backend connection was successfully made */
11232    if (PQstatus(conn) != CONNECTION_OK)
11233    {
11234        fprintf(stderr, "%s", PQerrorMessage(conn));
11235        exit_nicely(conn);
11236    }
11237
11238    /* Set always-secure search path, so malicious users can't take control. */
11239    res = PQexec(conn, "SET search_path = testlibpq3");
11240    if (PQresultStatus(res) != PGRES_COMMAND_OK)
11241    {
11242        fprintf(stderr, "SET failed: %s", PQerrorMessage(conn));
11243        PQclear(res);
11244        exit_nicely(conn);
11245    }
11246    PQclear(res);
11247
11248    /*
11249     * The point of this program is to illustrate use of PQexecParams() with
11250     * out-of-line parameters, as well as binary transmission of data.
11251     *
11252     * This first example transmits the parameters as text, but receives the
11253     * results in binary format.  By using out-of-line parameters we can avoid
11254     * a lot of tedious mucking about with quoting and escaping, even though
11255     * the data is text.  Notice how we don't have to do anything special with
11256     * the quote mark in the parameter value.
11257     */
11258
11259    /* Here is our out-of-line parameter value */
11260    paramValues[0] = "joe's place";
11261
11262    res = PQexecParams(conn,
11263                       "SELECT * FROM test1 WHERE t = $1",
11264                       1,       /* one param */
11265                       NULL,    /* let the backend deduce param type */
11266                       paramValues,
11267                       NULL,    /* don't need param lengths since text */
11268                       NULL,    /* default to all text params */
11269                       1);      /* ask for binary results */
11270
11271    if (PQresultStatus(res) != PGRES_TUPLES_OK)
11272    {
11273        fprintf(stderr, "SELECT failed: %s", PQerrorMessage(conn));
11274        PQclear(res);
11275        exit_nicely(conn);
11276    }
11277
11278    show_binary_results(res);
11279
11280    PQclear(res);
11281
11282    /*
11283     * In this second example we transmit an integer parameter in binary form,
11284     * and again retrieve the results in binary form.
11285     *
11286     * Although we tell PQexecParams we are letting the backend deduce
11287     * parameter type, we really force the decision by casting the parameter
11288     * symbol in the query text.  This is a good safety measure when sending
11289     * binary parameters.
11290     */
11291
11292    /* Convert integer value "2" to network byte order */
11293    binaryIntVal = htonl((uint32_t) 2);
11294
11295    /* Set up parameter arrays for PQexecParams */
11296    paramValues[0] = (char *) &binaryIntVal;
11297    paramLengths[0] = sizeof(binaryIntVal);
11298    paramFormats[0] = 1;        /* binary */
11299
11300    res = PQexecParams(conn,
11301                       "SELECT * FROM test1 WHERE i = $1::int4",
11302                       1,       /* one param */
11303                       NULL,    /* let the backend deduce param type */
11304                       paramValues,
11305                       paramLengths,
11306                       paramFormats,
11307                       1);      /* ask for binary results */
11308
11309    if (PQresultStatus(res) != PGRES_TUPLES_OK)
11310    {
11311        fprintf(stderr, "SELECT failed: %s", PQerrorMessage(conn));
11312        PQclear(res);
11313        exit_nicely(conn);
11314    }
11315
11316    show_binary_results(res);
11317
11318    PQclear(res);
11319
11320    /* close the connection to the database and cleanup */
11321    PQfinish(conn);
11322
11323    return 0;
11324}
11325]]>
11326</programlisting>
11327  </example>
11328
11329 </sect1>
11330</chapter>
